You can build a really strong connection with the characters, and I feel like it's not just because of the script... The actors were spectacular, and the atmosphere was exactly my style—it reminded me of "Seinfeld" and the way Jerry's stand-up tied into his personal life. In this project, we're introduced to Donna, who has a sense of humor very similar to mine—ironic and unfiltered.  

It ends in the best way possible, with him asking if she wanted to watch a movie. Not all men are the same, okay? There's a humble and honest sub-species out there.


Edit: The scene where she finds out she's pregnant is simply brilliant—the way she processes everything in those minutes before looking at the test. The radio "talking" to her… incredible. One of the best scenes I've ever seen in projects of this genre.

reviews.Name: The Disaster Artist
reviews.Review:
What a wild journey—I didn't even know it was based on a true story.  

Throughout the entire movie, I felt sorry for Tommy. At first, I thought he was a genius in the film industry, but as you learn more about the struggles each person had in getting roles, you start to realize just how bad he actually was.  

It was a lot of fun, but at the same time, some jokes weren’t executed in the best way, and the delivery from some actors wasn’t great either. A solid film, but with some flaws—especially in the comedy.

Seeing Bryan Cranston play Bryan Cranston was the peak of the project.

reviews.Name: Lamb
reviews.Review:
I love weird movies… but I prefer the kind of projects that make me think—strange yet philosophical. The premise they gave this feature film is just plain stupid, and there’s no real sense to it.  

I was on the verge of giving it half a star, I swear. But I won’t deny that there are some stunning scenes, breathtaking visuals—if not for that, I wouldn’t have finished the movie. And speaking of the ending… what was that? I get the idea that others had done something similar in the past, but her portrayal, combined with the camera focusing on her expressions, didn’t convince me at all.

reviews.Name: Dicks: The Musical
reviews.Review:
A musical meant to make you laugh… Unfortunately, I felt so much cringe that the jokes ended up fading into the background. I tried to give it a chance—there are a few (very few) positives, with one or two solid songs, but other than that...  

The best part of it was Megan Thee Stallion.

reviews.Name: False Positive
reviews.Review:
Discomfort the entire time—and honestly, it’s excellent to feel something like that in a project like this. I felt extremely anxious, stressed, unsure of what to think, and they implemented that feeling really well. But…  

The final act might be one of the worst I’ve ever seen in terms of cinematography—some scenes were downright terrible and extremely bizarre. I spent an hour waiting for a strong conclusion, only to end up wishing I hadn’t seen any of it. It felt like even the actors weren’t trying anymore, as if they had given up on the script long ago.  

A horror movie I have no intention of revisiting, with extremely obvious and unconvincing jumpscares. The suspense saved a lot of scenes, and because of that, I have to give it a higher rating than I originally planned.

reviews.Name: The Whale
reviews.Review:
As much as it made me cry my eyes out, I have to be critical of the project as a whole. It has quite a few flaws, especially in character development, and Thomas felt like he was thrown into the story at random. The critique (which, to me, is there, even if indirectly) of the U.S. healthcare system wasn’t well executed—but then again, I’m not sure if that was even their intention. It seemed like they wanted to address it but barely scratched the surface in a way that didn’t feel convincing.  

I can’t give it less than 3.5 stars because of the absolutely outstanding performances from every actor and the soundtrack throughout the film. They carried it on their backs.  

I also have to highlight the ending, which was clearly designed to make us cry. The symbolism and the analogy to the moment the father and daughter had at the beach were brilliant.

PS: When I die, I’d also like someone to read something for me… If I’m able, I might even recite my all-time favorite poem myself—"Guerra Civil" by Miguel Torga, a Portuguese author I highly recommend.

reviews.Name: Ginger & Rosa
reviews.Review:
Elle Fanning was only 14 when she did this, and she managed to be the most captivating presence on screen. Yes, her character ended up being the main focus, but she left me speechless—what an impressive actress.
As for the film itself… It has some flaws, another one that lacks character development, and I really wanted to see and learn more about some of the characters that appeared. Some scenes could have been filmed better—I sensed a bit of amateurism behind the camera.

What the father did was so disgusting it sent shivers down my spine—and saying he was in love is no excuse.

reviews.Name: Funny Pages
reviews.Review:
I can imagine a version of this film that would be absolutely perfect. But truth be told, it fell short in a few areas — and I say this with complete honesty: I never really cared about Wallace’s life or career. He just showed up out of nowhere, gave overly simple answers about his work, and never truly won me over. Even the ending — both his and the film’s — felt all over the place and unnecessarily chaotic.

Still, there are plenty of strong points. Like I said, I can easily picture a masterpiece in this style: an outstanding soundtrack, excellent camera work paired with details that brought the era to life (the color grading, the fonts, even the grainy look in some scenes). There was a character that genuinely made me care the entire time (Robert), and a very clear and well-presented issue in this type of artistic market — narcissism, the “I’m the best” mindset, the popularity of certain types of cartoons, and the lack of funding for more niche artists.

reviews.Name: Causeway
reviews.Review:
I don't care what anyone else thinks about this… I've read so many comments saying it's boring — enough. This is hauntingly beautiful and chilling. The pacing was nearly perfect, I was completely drawn to the characters, I wanted to know more about the two of them, and I honestly don't think it was just because of the actors: the script is chilling, simple, and effective.

The ending left me in pieces, and the final line perfectly captured what both of them were looking for: “I'm trying to make a friend.” Sometimes, just a quiet cup of coffee in the morning with the right company can mean the world.

reviews.Name: Amy
reviews.Review:
This was one of the hardest projects to watch. Amy had such a huge impact on the love I have for music that, while watching it, I felt pieces of myself falling apart.  
Seeing and hearing everything she did—not just in the studio but also on tour—hurt, even knowing everything that had happened. In fact, I feel like knowing her full story only intensified my experience even more.  

She was—and will always be—one of my favorite artists. She still inspires me to do the little, silly things I do with my guitar and my voice.  

My favorite part, without a doubt, was when "Back to Black" started playing with just her voice… then transitioned to the studio version, and finally ended again with her singing “black” at the mic. It completely broke me.

reviews.Name: Marcel the Shell with Shoes On
reviews.Review:
It was an incredible experience, and yes, everyone loves Marcel.
The peculiarities of this film were so well used, so well structured, so well executed. I was honestly surprised at how deeply I felt connected to the little guy.
Even the “problems” tied to fame, or the difficulty of adjusting to a new environment, far from family — I teared up when Marcel started talking about it.

I really wasn’t expecting it… what a breath of fresh air — something so different and so good, so heartwarming.

reviews.Name: Menashe
reviews.Review:
I really wanted to be more engaged while watching this film, but the truth is it just didn’t captivate me as much as I expected. I know the film was about Menashe, but not giving some of the other characters more attention felt, to me, like a missed opportunity.
I also wish we had gotten to know a bit more about his life — his future, whether it could be better or not... Ending it the way it did just wasn’t satisfying.

reviews.Name: Mojave
reviews.Review:
I can’t believe I had the courage to watch this all the way through. Not even Oscar Isaac could save it.
Such mediocre and unrelatable characters — it was honestly cringe-worthy every time they randomly threw in Shakespeare and Stuart Mill into conversations, it never felt natural.
And the ending... they might as well have killed each other, I would’ve been much happier with that — what a misery.

reviews.Name: Mississippi Grind
reviews.Review: So, I’m giving this rating for two reasons: as much as I liked the cinematography, the actors, the soundtrack, and even the message of the film, many scenes felt unnecessary, the script was a bit forced, and the ending kind of irritated me (it took away some of the magic behind the moral of the story).

reviews.Name: Zola
reviews.Review:
It was quite an interesting watch, especially knowing it was based on a true story. The actors themselves were brilliant, and I can’t stop thinking about the range Colman Domingo has—he can play SO many different roles, he’s a true genius in cinema, no doubt.
There are some issues though, especially with the script. Zola’s narration felt random—every 30 minutes or so we’d suddenly hear her voice telling the story, as if they forgot she was supposed to be narrating it all along. A few completely unnecessary scenes also took away from my overall experience, but all in all, a pretty solid film.

reviews.Name: Under the Skin
reviews.Review:
What I took from this film: the plot isn’t what matters, it’s the perspective. 

What a sensational experience... So simple yet so abstract — visually haunting in a way that contrasts beautifully with the human experience, the experience of just being... human.
Scarlett’s character slowly begins to understand the meaning we, as humans, give to life — things like kindness, solidarity, care for others… or even monstrous instincts (like the man in the forest).
This was without a doubt a cinematic experience that will stay with me forever, in the best way possible. I also know this isn’t a film for everyone, and many might not enjoy the heavy abstraction… But me? I LOVED it — and I’m still considering the 5 stars.

reviews.Name: Trespass Against Us
reviews.Review:
It’s one of those films where, if it weren’t for the extraordinary actors, I’d have rated it lower. Fassbender, Gleeson, and Keoghan truly carried what, to me, is an implausible story. I don’t think I’ve ever seen such idiotic police characters in a film — constantly chasing the same guy without any sort of warrant... An absolute disaster in that regard; they made the police look like a bunch of incompetents.
Other than that, I don’t have much to add, because, again — the actors were absolutely brilliant.

reviews.Name: Slice
reviews.Review:
A24… you keep surprising me, but now in the worst way possible. I never thought watching Chance The Rapper playing a werewolf would be something I checked off my 2025 list — and honestly, I wish I hadn’t.
The cinematography was actually pretty decent, but the acting fell flat and the script was too bad to be true: the witches were the absolute peak of mediocrity. I laughed a grand total of zero times and still managed to call every single character cringe — now that’s a great comedy! (See, Austin? I managed to be ironic and satirical better than you, and in way less time.)
Anyway, I’ll keep going with this A24 journey… I just hope I don’t come across more films like this. I still believe A24 is the best studio out there.

reviews.Name: Ex Machina
reviews.Review:
Well… after a long battle with "Men (2022)", my expectations for another film by Alex were pretty low, even though I'd been told this would be an interesting experience for me. As I started watching, I immediately noticed a familiar pattern already “implemented” in "Men (2022)" — the themes of misogyny and sexism. I honestly didn't expect that kind of message, that kind of moral approach, in a story like this.

And I have to be frank… aside from Oscar Isaac, the actors didn't really impress me, and I genuinely think they could've given a lot more.

It's a very solid film, but at the same time, I feel like it could've explored a lot more. Still, I'm giving it 4 stars… Let's just hope I don't regret that rating if I ever decide to rewatch it.

reviews.Name: It Comes at Night
reviews.Review:
So, it's easy to tell throughout the film that "it" refers to fear, terror, or paranoia that comes at night — which isn't particularly clever, and it ends up being just another generic horror movie. Yes, there are some really interesting moments, or at least ones that are decently executed — like Travis' dreams, or even the final shot of the film, with the parents sitting at the table, staring at each other (which, in my opinion, was a brilliant touch).

Even with these positives, the lack of coherence in the characters made this feel extremely ordinary: it's yet another horror film where a character is dumb enough to run into the woods at night, with a deadly syndrome just waiting to get inside them. The illness itself is never explained, but it clearly works as a metaphor for fear...

Overall, a horror film like so many others, with one or two standout moments in terms of aesthetic and cinematography.

reviews.Name: Low Tide
reviews.Review:
The story is really simple but quite effective… I don’t think being a short film was a problem at all — it might’ve actually helped with the pacing (which was already slow enough).
That said… It’s a decent film, but it needed a lot more substance for me to say it deserved more than 3 stars.

Ah, the things love does to a man… makes him stop robbing houses, imagine that!

reviews.Name: Cut Bank
reviews.Review:
Big-name actors only to end up watching Liam Hemsworth deliver a downright mediocre performance. I wasn’t ready for something so poorly explained, so nonsensical, and above all, so passionless.

The only decent takeaway from this film is a handful of visuals — and especially Michael Stuhlbarg, who completely sold me. He did his absolute best with the script he was given. I’ve always said Michael is a brilliant actor, and this just reinforces my point.
As for the rest… Terrible.

“That, indeed, is the taking of a human life” — NO SHIT SHERLOCK.

reviews.Name: C'mon C'mon
reviews.Review:
This was beautiful. I can’t find another word to define what I just experienced… I might be biased when I call this film a masterpiece: I just became an uncle (well, a year and a few — I’d even say brief — months ago). What I saw here is everything I want to be for my nephew — his best friend, or at the very least, the person who annoys him the most.

Everything was perfect. The dialogues were beautiful. The rehearsals being read aloud as the film progressed revealed every single feeling — not just from the characters, but from all of us, both while watching and when waking up to start a new day.

I can’t keep writing or I’ll end up crying even more… Perfect.


"Jesse: [interviewer voice] Have you ever thought about the future?
[normal voice]
Jesse: Ahh yeah, whatever you plan on happening never happens. Stuff you would never think of happens. So you just have to c'mon c'mon, c'mon c'mon c'mon."

reviews.Name: Equals
reviews.Review:
I truly believe it can be hard to enjoy films with strange and unlikely premises… But I love them, when they’re well done.
As soon as I saw this film in A24’s catalogue, I was genuinely intrigued by the heavyweight cast. And the truth is, every single one of them delivered an incredible performance — especially Kristen.

Now, when it comes to the plot, the script, the premise itself — chaotic, senseless, and calling emotions a “disease” is so cliché that it just didn’t land for me. It all felt so generic, so similar to other things I’ve seen… or even read.

I honestly feel like watching another sci-fi like this — with this kind of premise — will be nearly impossible (considering there’s nothing else like it left to watch from A24).

reviews.Name: mid90s
reviews.Review:
I wouldn’t say this was a perfect film — far from it. I noticed some flaws in the script, and overall it felt a bit slow in telling the story. They could’ve explored the lives of the other characters way more, not just Stevie’s...

Not everything was bad though — the cinematography, the performances, and especially the ending really saved the experience for me. Honestly, the ending was the best I could’ve imagined.

reviews.Name: Morris from America
reviews.Review:
I don’t want people to think this is a bad film just because of the rating I gave it. It actually tackles some meaningful topics — racism, and how powerful music can be in bringing together people from vastly different cultural backgrounds.

I think everyone can say music is part of their daily lives, but my connection to it is almost ridiculous. I wake up to the radio and end the day with headaches and sore ears from having my headphones on for hours. What I feel for music aligns perfectly with Curtis’s speech to his son about authenticity and passion… I honestly can’t imagine my life without music — whether it’s Spotify or SoundCloud, guitar or ukulele, singing or just strumming strings for comfort.

The script felt a bit incomplete, some dialogue fell flat, but the cinematography was decent, and the social issues were represented really well.

reviews.Name: Opus
reviews.Review:
I was expecting worse... but I was also expecting better. I can’t really explain it. I’ve always enjoyed this type of film, but the truth is it’s starting to feel repetitive — it always follows the same formula: a group walks into the heart of a cult and witnesses strange things happening.
Don’t get me wrong, like I said, I enjoy these kinds of projects — when they’re well explained and grounded. This one, however, ended up feeling like a watered-down version of Midsommar, where none of the beliefs were properly explained. Nothing I saw was justified — why they were doing it, what for, or what the purpose behind all that madness was.
I know it might sound a bit unfair to compare it to one of the greatest horror films of the 21st century — maybe even of all time — but let’s face it: the premise is strikingly similar.

I did enjoy the characters, I was genuinely invested in understanding the cult, the ending was solid, and the cinematography was beautiful… but the lack of proper writing and depth really killed the experience for me.

reviews.Name: The Bling Ring
reviews.Review:
Based on a true story, so criticizing the script feels a bit tricky. However, I can easily call out the cinematography and editing because, honestly… terrible.
As for the case itself, it was definitely interesting to learn about. The irresponsibility—or rather, the sheer naivety—reached such an extreme that reality never even seemed to cross their minds.

reviews.Name: The Inspection
reviews.Review:
I genuinely thought it was going to be boring, but it turned out to be more interesting than I expected. Sure, it’s not the type of film I usually go for—or better yet, it’s not really a subject that catches my attention... But the truth is, I stayed fully engaged the whole time. The characters really managed to pull me in.
Of course, it falls short on a cinematic level, especially in terms of editing. It either rushed through scenes or dragged a bit too much in others.
I’m settling on 3.5 stars, mainly because I feel like the camera work, color use, and editing could’ve been a lot better.

reviews.Name: Sinners
reviews.Review:
It’s impressive how a horror film can feel like such a breath of fresh air in today’s cinema. I walked into the theatre thinking it was going to be just another generic vampire story—and walked out thinking, “What did I just watch?” And honestly, that’s exactly how you should feel leaving a movie.
There were some absolutely brilliant sound choices; it ended up being the strongest element of the whole project. The music gave me chills, and I’m sure I’ll be listening to the soundtrack on repeat like there’s no tomorrow. I’m stunned by the performances—they were extraordinary.

Bold, unafraid to show something different—and I always appreciate that.

I’m not giving it 5 stars because of the ending. It could’ve been handled better, even though I understand why they chose to go that route.

reviews.Name: 20th Century Women
reviews.Review:
It was incredible. Truly incredible.
Whenever I watch a drama, I go in wanting to get to know the characters—every single one of them—and that’s something I always take into account. This project not only portrays Dorothea’s and Jamie’s lives, but it also dives into so many other important people connected to them, and they’re all so well fleshed out…
A script that had me in tears by the end, simply because I got to know what happened to each one of them.

I have to highlight once again the essays presented throughout the film. Just like in "C’mon C’mon (2021)", it made the whole experience so much more interesting—watching the characters read those texts felt seamless within the story and helped me understand each one of them better, what they were going through, what they were feeling in that exact moment.

Oh, and by the way, I love Talking Heads.

reviews.Name: A Different Man
reviews.Review:
I started watching this film without even reading the synopsis... I had no idea what it was about, nor what genre it belonged to—and I honestly think that was the best decision I could’ve made.

Sadness, anger, stress, anxiety, joy, and pure fun—all concentrated in a deeply philosophical project. It ends where it all began, and that moment was the peak of everything I felt throughout the film.

A comedy with hints of psychological thriller, and I never thought I’d find a mix like this... I had so much fun, but at the same time, the stress kept building up until it burst in all directions the moment Oswald said: “Oh my friend, you haven’t changed a bit.”

Sebastian... Adam... You were both so incredible—I struggle to put into words just how powerful your performances were. Watching you was an absolute privilege.

reviews.Name: A Most Violent Year
reviews.Review:
Stunning cinematography, but I felt no real connection to the story. I found myself more invested in the family than the actual business... I never really cared about what they did or what was going to happen with the company, and I honestly can’t explain why. They could’ve explored Anna so much more—both personally and professionally.

There were also a few scenes that felt completely random and out of place, like the deer being hit by the car... It made no sense and, in my opinion, had no connection to the plot.

reviews.Name: A Prayer Before Dawn
reviews.Review:
As much as the character wasn’t deeply explored — and you all know how much that usually bothers me — I understand why they left some things unresolved. The film’s purpose wasn’t to dive into the character, but to expose the ongoing torture in that kind of prison. It’s a constant, daily fight, and if your eyes aren’t always open, you might end up dead in a corner of your cell.
It’s a powerful and disturbing testimony to the brutality that takes place in such dark prisons. The cinematography is stunning, with scenes that gave me chills… Yes, I would’ve done a few things differently, especially some of the more violent or unnecessary moments, but overall, it’s an important and unsettling film.

reviews.Name: After Yang
reviews.Review:
I know the theme represented in this project has been done before, but the way the story was told made everything feel special.

The cinematography is out of this world — absolutely fascinating and unafraid to explore different types of imagery and camera work. The use of color was incredibly detailed, paired with a script that had me hooked from beginning to end.

The scenes where Jake "visited" Yang’s memories left me breathless, they were just so beautiful. The conversation between the android and Kyra left me speechless, and the short dialogue between Ada and Jake in the car completely floored me — so simple, yet so powerful.

An incredible experience, and I’m sure I’ll want to rewatch this film again someday.

reviews.Name: All Dirt Roads Taste of Salt
reviews.Review:
I had a hard time with the long scenes, which kept me from giving this a higher rating. It's impossible to deny how haunting the message is or how beautiful the cinematography can be, but the transitions between scenes took far too long, and my eyes often lost focus. As impactful as the visuals were, they would linger for 5 or 6 seconds in the same spot, and once the scene changed, I found myself losing track of what I was watching.
In my opinion, the editing was poorly executed, and that made this experience less enjoyable than it could have been.
Still, I have to highlight how well the film portrayed loss and grief under certain circumstances — that part was deeply moving and thoughtfully implemented.

“Mack: You want to know a secret? It doesn't end or begin. Just changes form.
Lily: What changes?
Mack: Water. All these drops might be a river someday. Might be snow. Might be in you.”

reviews.Name: Babygirl
reviews.Review:
It’s clear that this film wasn’t made with the intention of winning any awards or anything like that. It’s a film about sex and kinks, and I’ve never really been a fan of this type of project. From the little I’ve seen in this genre, this one does stand out — though it still suffers from major script issues. I understand that the goal was to explore this kind of sexual content, but I have to evaluate the film as a whole.

Samuel didn’t even seem to have a job, Romy’s family was barely explored (and if that wasn’t the intention, they shouldn’t have included scenes with Isabel, one of her daughters), and Jacob was far from convincing... The best part was definitely the complexity of Kidman’s character, which I found genuinely interesting.

reviews.Name: Barely Lethal
reviews.Review:
I feel like when I become a dad, I’ll probably be like Mr. Marcus — and I’m not sure if that’s a good thing or not.

Either way, this feels more Disney than A24, and that’s totally fine… My problem lies in how everything unfolded — and let’s just say it wasn’t… good… not good at all.
Seeing Steinfeld in Sinners and now here, what an absurd difference — the growth and improvement in her acting is incredible.

reviews.Name: Bodies Bodies Bodies
reviews.Review:
Oh Gen Z… you’re so lost. I genuinely feel bad — and yes, I’m excluding myself from the group because self-awareness still exists somewhere.

Despite actually enjoying the film, the satire, and all those painfully on-the-nose critiques, I left the movie deeply offended... on Greg’s behalf. He was my favorite. He deserved better.

Also, Max showing up at the end? That was beyond stupid, unnecessary, and it completely ruined the experience for me.

reviews.Name: Dark Places
reviews.Review:
I read a comment that said this movie was shot similarly to The Office... Well, let me just say The Office has better cinematography than this.

I walked into this film fully aware it was going to be bad. And yet... I still left even more disappointed. It’s a rare feeling, but when it hits? Damn, it hurts.

The performances were so weak they felt robotic... I feel bad for Hoult, he was the only one who actually pulled it off here.

reviews.Name: Earth Mama
reviews.Review:
What a beautiful film. Chilling from start to finish, with cinematography that left me speechless the entire time. Images that will stay in my memory forever — not just for their impact on the story, but because of everything we know about Gia. There are moments that let you breathe once you begin to understand everything that’s happening, and that made it a lot less heavy than I was expecting — they let me breathe when I needed to.
It was an incredible experience about a sensitive, objectified, emotionally violent, and xenophobic subject.

reviews.Name: Sing Sing
reviews.Review:
A film about the corrupt justice system.
A film about the difficulty of being human.
A film about losing friends and family.
A film about the price of an act that, for many, might seem innocent.
A film about forgiveness.
But above all... a film about freedom, a film about humanity.

reviews.Name: The Zone of Interest
reviews.Review:
A film that makes the air feel heavier with each passing moment, as the sound slowly takes over our senses.
The sound becomes so disturbingly overwhelming that all that remains in my mind are the atrocities committed beyond the mundane lives of these inhuman beings.
A camera work, in my view, out of the ordinary, with haunting images...
An ending that will stay in my mind forever.

reviews.Name: The Rover
reviews.Review:
Another film completely carried by its actors. Pattinson and Pearce at their absolute best, with performances that left me speechless. If I was already a fan, after this I became a full-blown fanatic.
As for the project as a whole… it reminded me of a Manoel de Oliveira film. For those unfamiliar, he was a Portuguese filmmaker known for excessively long takes—scenes that went on for ages and could've easily been cut in half or left out entirely. This film followed that formula: it tried to build suspense but ended up just dragging every scene into boredom.
As for the ending: I loved it, genuinely. It felt ironic, but I mean it—knowing he did all of that for a single car? Brilliant.

reviews.Name: The Vanishing of Sidney Hall
reviews.Review:
I truly believe this could’ve been a brilliant film if it hadn’t included certain moments that felt, to me, emotionally manipulative and entirely unnecessary. I’m pretty sure most people watching had the same reaction I did after the opening scene: How the hell did a guy who talks like this become one of the most influential writers in the world?

That said, I don’t want to overshadow some of the genuinely well-executed parts. I think a lot of the discourse around Melody and her role in Sidney’s life is blown out of proportion. No, she’s not meant to be some ethereal “muse,” and no, she’s not annoying. She’s just a girl who falls in love with a boy—and vice versa. Their relationship was clearly built up, and I was genuinely invested in their future together as husband and wife.

Now, about those unnecessary moments: I’m mainly referring to the film’s final scenes between Sidney (Logan Lerman) and “The Searcher” (Kyle Chandler). They were incredibly dull and, frankly, a bit contradictory considering Sidney is supposed to be a genius. 
The so-called "plot twist"—if I can even call it that—completely missed the mark. It felt incredibly forced, even though it touched on sensitive topics like rape, domestic violence, and sexual harassment.

reviews.Name: Tusk
reviews.Review: From the moment I saw Lily-Rose Depp acting, I told myself, “Well, this just got interesting.” The same can't be said for Johnny Depp—by the time he showed up, I’d already seen too much. He should’ve appeared earlier, before we even knew what would happen to Wallace. And even then, his character was meant to be comic relief but only made everything more cringe-worthy. This is a film I won’t be revisiting anytime soon, for very understandable reasons: a ridiculous story with no philosophical depth.

reviews.Name: The Lovers
reviews.Review: It was hard to settle on a rating that felt right to my conscience. On one hand, three and a half stars feels too little; on the other, four stars feels like too much. The plot is incredibly simple, yet deeply symbolic and reflective of the society we live in. They both have lovers, but eventually become lovers to each other — and that’s what truly fascinated me about this whole experience. What held me back was the final act, or more precisely, the moment the son entered the picture. He was a character that rubbed me the wrong way, created a conflict of interests, and demanded to be the center of attention — it was too much, and I didn’t like that aspect. This could’ve easily been a perfect film if not for that, and for the lack of clarity about what actually happened to their relationship over the years, for it to be so broken by the time we first see them.

reviews.Name: Thunderbolts*
reviews.Review:
We’re talking about a film that caught my attention the moment I saw the “A24-style” trailer — and that already says a lot about what Marvel projects have become in recent years. The truth is, it surprised me in every possible way: from the action and battle scenes, to the visual effects, and especially the character development (which I find essential in this type of film). I’m giving it a 4.5-star rating because it stands out — in a good way — from all the Marvel films I’ve unfortunately been watching with dismay. It’s undoubtedly the best one since Endgame, and one of the strongest entries the MCU has ever brought to the big screen.

“You can’t even save yourself” - The Void

reviews.Name: Minari
reviews.Review:
I couldn’t have watched this at a better time.
An emotionally demanding film: the story seems so simple, yet every scene and every image carries deep meaning.
The ending completes the cycle—mirroring the planting season shown earlier—absolutely brilliant.
The actors turn the whole thing into a beautiful piece of art, and the soundtrack sends shivers from start to finish.

reviews.Name: While We're Young
reviews.Review: Damn it... Now I wanna get high to the Blade Runner (1982) soundtrack too.

reviews.Name: Y2K
reviews.Review: Great. Now I can say I’ve heard someone saying "Oh my God, that’s Fred Durst". And believe me, that was the best line of the movie.

reviews.Name: Woodshock
reviews.Review:
If I’d ever imagined Kirsten Dunst completely stoned, it would probably look something like this.

Started watching this with zero expectations and ended up with my jaw on the floor… Didn’t think it would be this interesting — or this visually stunning.

reviews.Name: Woman Walks Ahead
reviews.Review:
I woke up wanting to watch a film... Just finished it and now I feel like going back to bed. It made me so sleepy I could honestly take a nap.

Definitely not the kind of project I enjoy the most — I'm even the kind of guy who says he doesn’t plan on watching many more like this for the rest of his life.

reviews.Name: Waves
reviews.Review:
Please, always use protection.

On a more serious note... I'm still in shock from the sheer amount of emotions I felt throughout this project. And I honestly can't even express them properly — it was just that intense. This will stay with me forever, and I'm genuinely grateful to have come across something with so much meaning and so many important messages, especially in today’s world. I just hope more people get to watch it — it’s something special.

Ps: That scene in the garden... That moment between father and daughter, absolutely perfect.

reviews.Name: Under the Silver Lake
reviews.Review:
On a comedic note: this is basically "Beau Is Afraid", but instead of going to see his mother, he’s heading toward the girl — so they can maybe have sex in the future.

This film brings up some genuinely interesting points, especially regarding its core message: how is he more focused on finding the girl than uncovering all the twisted things going on in that city?

Extremely well-directed, with long takes that genuinely evoke classic noir films... I’m certain I’ll be rewatching this just to absorb more and connect all the dots.

By the way, the scene with the songwriter might be one of the boldest things I’ve ever seen in cinema — ever.

For now, I’m settling on 3.5 stars… I have a feeling it’s going to grow on me, little by little.

reviews.Name: Hot Summer Nights
reviews.Review: The story was so painfully predictable I found myself looking forward to the cringe-worthy love scenes — at least they made me feel something.

reviews.Name: How to Talk to Girls at Parties
reviews.Review:
Not gonna lie... there were some interesting bits. But in between, I was bombarded with such idiotic scenes I physically cringed. Another one for the "A24 type of film but make it bad" list.

It felt more like a cult than actual beings from another planet… Not sure if that was intentional, but damn, the whole thing was just poorly handled - the script felt off from start to finish.

reviews.Name: Laggies
reviews.Review:
I'm giving this rating purely for the enjoyment it gave me — definitely not because of the title or poster, because my god… awful.

It was surprisingly light, with introspective dialogue, exactly what I needed for times like this, when the anxiety tries to take control of my little head. I'm sure I'll rewatch this someday.

reviews.Name: Stars at Noon
reviews.Review:
"You really are a whore"... Wow, so romantic.

Their relationship felt so underdeveloped, so poorly grounded... I actually found most of their scenes together quite dull — I just didn’t feel anything, no real motivation that made me care about their connection.
Visually stunning, and the themes were interesting, but the execution left a lot to be desired.

reviews.Name: The Phoenician Scheme
reviews.Review:
So… yet another Wes Anderson film with Wes Anderson cinematography, Wes Anderson humor, and a script that feels very Wes Anderson.
Probably one of his less accomplished ones — especially in terms of story and how it unfolds — but still incredibly solid.

reviews.Name: Spring Breakers
reviews.Review:
James Franco seems to have an uncanny talent for playing these kinds of characters — and I’m not quite sure how to feel about that.

The cinematography was VERY well done, but the story falls short from beginning to end, and the way it wraps up is anything but satisfying.

On the bright side: Selena Gomez didn’t get much screen time.

reviews.Name: The Farewell
reviews.Review:
What a breath of fresh air, what a beautiful film, what a wonderful story... I completely fell in love with the family — I wanted to know everything, every interaction and relationship, from beginning to end.
A screenplay that gives you chills throughout, paired with a simple yet effective cinematography — I couldn’t ask for much more from a film like this.
'Based on an actual lie' — a line that will stay with me.

reviews.Name: The Monster
reviews.Review:
This was so creatively bankrupt it made me rethink my entire ambition of becoming a world-famous director. You know that delusion — "I’ll make a horror film so unforgettable it’ll haunt people forever"? Yeah, that died a slow, boring death about 15 minutes into this.
It explains nothing about "the monster", the characters are painfully predictable, and the plot is ridiculously simple and bland.

reviews.Name: Warfare
reviews.Review:
Some people are really trying to twist the purpose of this film – I feel disoriented just reading some of the reviews.
This is not a propaganda piece, nor is it trying to label anyone as a hero or a villain. It's meant to portray the cowardice and dehumanization seen throughout the Iraq War – both in the actions of Iraqi forces and in the cruelty of many American soldiers.
This is an anti-war film, with a remarkably simple plot but filled with thought-provoking elements worth discussing. I just found it unnecessary to show images of the soldiers at the end, some with blurred or covered faces.

A film with a high level of difficulty in execution that still delivers – both in cinematography and in how it conveys pain, aggression, and fear through all its actors.

reviews.Name: Tuesday
reviews.Review:
An excellent concept, with a strong first and third act. The middle section, however, felt overdone – the CGI was lacking, and the script seemed a bit lost in itself.
Even though I didn’t enjoy the second act much (I mean… she literally eats Death?), there’s still a lot of value here. The final dialogue was genuinely beautiful, and the interactions between Death and the daughter, and Death and the mother, were thoughtfully explored.
I just remained hesitant about some scenes that, in my opinion, felt completely unnecessary and detracted from the emotional weight of the story.

reviews.Name: Talk to Me
reviews.Review:
A concept with a lot to unpack, and I truly hope they explore it further in future installments (I'm definitely counting on more projects tied to this one).
The horror was well-executed — it gradually builds tension until the final crescendo of the story. I found the camera work particularly engaging, mixing familiar tricks from the genre with more innovative choices that showed real potential.
The ending genuinely gave me chills and felt completely earned, even if it caught me off guard.
It's been a while since I've seen a horror film with such decent execution. I just hope it doesn’t go down the same path as "The Conjuring" Universe and that they make smarter choices, like they did with the "X" franchise — which I honestly doubt they’ll ever top.

reviews.Name: Materialists
reviews.Review:
I’m genuinely stunned by what I just watched. I wasn’t expecting it to be this good… The trailer doesn’t even show a tenth of what’s actually addressed in the film, which made the whole experience even more surprising.
The dialogue is minimalist, yet incredibly impactful — filled with important themes that absolutely deserve to be discussed and FOUGHT.

Also, can Celine release an extended scene of Pedro Pascal in the bath? Please. For research purposes.

reviews.Name: On the Rocks
reviews.Review:
It was quite fun, even though I get the criticism. I don’t exactly feel like a 40-year-old woman who enjoys renting movies, but I can totally see this kind of vibe fitting the project.
It deals with a pretty interesting topic, especially when it comes to betrayal and over-involved parents... I think Sofia could’ve pushed Bill Murray’s character a bit further, and the final moment between husband and wife didn’t feel satisfying at all.

reviews.Name: Lady Bird
reviews.Review:
How can I put this without sounding suspicious… It kind of reminded me of Eighth Grade. Even the cinematography had some similar notes. Yes, I know I’m comparing Bo to Greta — allow me to stop this line of thought and move on to something more important:

I’ve never had any real issues with my mom, we’ve always been incredibly close, but seeing things from this point of view genuinely caught me off guard. It was such a well-portrayed relationship.

As for the teenage struggles? All well-characterized. The usual themes, sure, but paired with brilliant performances and a pacing that introduced problems gradually, never overwhelming the narrative.

And lastly, can we talk about Timothée’s range? The man goes from manipulative jerk, to junkie, to musician, to literal Messiah — and somehow makes it all work.

reviews.Name: The Souvenir
reviews.Review:
Definitely not my favorite kind of film... I found it a bit dull at times, but I get why so many people love it: it’s a well-structured story with very well-developed characters. The actors were absolutely chilling — and I have to single out Honor, what a phenomenal actress.
Am I interested in seeing the second part? Yes and no... On one hand, I want to know what happens with Julie, but on the other, I keep thinking about the extremely plain cinematography, which lacked any real spark. Still, I have to watch it.

reviews.Name: The Souvenir: Part II
reviews.Review:
Well, it really feels like I watched a completely different movie than everyone else... I don’t think this is better than the first one — which already says a lot.
I found the premise barely interesting, with a deliberately slow pace that nearly put me to sleep.

reviews.Name: Medusa Deluxe
reviews.Review:
It’s a premise we’ve seen before — and it honestly reminded me a lot of "Climax" — but at the same time, it brings something genuinely creative to the table that kept me glued to the screen for the entire hour and forty minutes.
I genuinely believe most of the people who gave this a negative review just didn’t grasp the hyperbolic, satirical concept behind everything we watched. It’s a ridiculously entertaining project, especially because the mystery becomes almost irrelevant thanks to the incredible cast of characters.
I won’t go on too long — mostly because I want anyone reading this to go watch it for themselves. It’s not a waste of time, and even if you don’t care for the story or the over-the-top sarcasm... just stay for the BRILLIANCE of the cinematography. I was blown away by the amount of wide, continuous shots throughout.

reviews.Name: Red Rocket
reviews.Review:
Sean, I’m sorry to say this, but this kind of film really isn’t for me. I do understand your essence and the message you’re trying to bring to cinema and smaller productions, but I just can’t connect much with the stories you choose to tell.
Maybe it’s simply because I’m not a fan of things that are so explicit, or of stories that feel both strange and oddly “normal” at the same time… I honestly can’t explain it.
That being said, while watching the film I could easily see why so many people are drawn to this type of project: the characters are very well developed, the story is solid — even entertaining at times — and the cinematography is surprisingly strong given the production budget.
And that’s exactly why I’m giving it the rating I am… Even if this genre doesn’t appeal to me, I can clearly recognize that you’re a remarkable director, unafraid to tell these kinds of stories.

reviews.Name: The Tragedy of Macbeth
reviews.Review:
In the end, it’s a faithful adaptation of a Shakespeare play — it doesn’t bring anything new to the story, which is honestly to be expected.
What it does offer is incredibly well-crafted cinematography (though I still question the choice of black and white and the square aspect ratio) and a cast that completely committed to their roles.
You can truly follow Macbeth’s descent into madness just by watching Denzel’s facial expressions — and that alone says everything about the quality of the cast.

reviews.Name: God's Creatures
reviews.Review:
"We are all God's creatures in the dark."
Another banger from A24 — and I say this based on everything I experienced throughout an hour and forty minutes of film. Rage, sorrow, agony, stress, and anxiety all wrapped up in a psychological drama packed with heavy symbolism and depth of character.
Unfortunately, it was poorly marketed and ended up reaching very few people.
It has that signature A24 look, with perfectly composed camera work that made the minimal dialogue between characters feel even more intense and dramatic.
Tackles urgent and relevant topics, with performances that honestly left me speechless.
I'm settling on 4 stars for now, but I have a strong feeling that a second watch could easily push it higher in my ranking.
What would you be capable of doing for your child? Everything? Or are there limits?

reviews.Name: In Fabric
reviews.Review:
And yet another banger… I’m still in shock with what I’ve just seen.
It’s a completely different kind of horror — unlike anything I’ve watched before — and that’s exactly what I loved about it.
Sure, it has its flaws, but the ever-present A24 touch, the performances, and the sharp critique of modern society make this film a true innovation on all fronts.
That’s also why I’m giving it this rating — it’s something different, proudly alternative, with psychedelic undertones and a refreshingly out-of-the-box approach to what horror has sadly become these days.

reviews.Name: Life After Beth
reviews.Review:
A decent film? I wouldn’t say that… A bad one? Yeah, I might go with that.
There’s very little worth saving here, and I could go on and on about how chaotic and pointlessly random it all felt.
Maybe this kind of project just isn’t for me — but honestly, I can’t recommend it to anyone. Just watch the trailer and read the synopsis, I promise that’s more than enough.
Were they trying to satirize horror movies? Maybe — but even if that’s the case, the result is still the same: barely anything works.
Was it meant to be a teen drama about love and loss? Could be — but again, none of those moments land.

reviews.Name: Outlaws
reviews.Review:
Honestly? I'm still trying to figure out how I didn’t switch it off halfway through.
It’s already the kind of genre I run from, but somehow they seasoned it with just the right mix of cringe and chaos to make it borderline unwatchable.
Terrible performances, cinematography flatter than a pancake, and a storyline so predictable I could’ve written it in my sleep — or worse, had ChatGPT do it.

reviews.Name: Remember
reviews.Review:
A fresh approach to the Holocaust and World War II. I wasn’t expecting a plot like this, but I always felt like something was off…
It’s really well built — no denying that — I just had a feeling about the ending, and that kind of ruined the experience for me.
Great acting, and a story I honestly found fascinating.
A few unnecessary scenes and some plot holes (as if an old man with Alzheimer’s could just leave a care home and no one would bat an eye) made me land on this rating — but don’t get me wrong, it’s definitely worth watching!

reviews.Name: The Children Act
reviews.Review:
We’re talking about a film that must’ve been incredibly hard to write and produce, given all the moral and emotional weight it carries.
We’re also talking about a film set in the UK — not in Portugal, where I was born and live… And of course, I don’t know the law well enough to comment on the specific legal matter, but I can still share my opinion: a minor should never be allowed to make that kind of decision, and religion should never be part of the equation.
Visually, it’s quite underwhelming, the performance is haunting (in the best sense), and the third act could’ve had a stronger development and conclusion.

reviews.Name: The Exception
reviews.Review:
According to A24’s website, this is a thriller… Meanwhile, Letterboxd labels it as a drama/war film. I honestly didn’t see either — at most, a bit of drama in the ROMANCE aspect.
I’m sure the goal was to focus on the relationship between the two characters and to show that love transcends any barrier (even one as stupid and inhumane as this). But I felt so little from that connection… And I swear I’m not made of stone or emotionally numb.
I just really didn’t like how their romance was portrayed, and above all, I didn’t understand their first scene alone together — completely unnecessary and totally fake.

reviews.Name: The Spectacular Now
reviews.Review:
I just can’t rate this any higher... I don’t want to ruin the "reputation" of this project because, truth be told, it’s quite entertaining, and the relationship is well-developed to the point where you genuinely care about their future.
That said, I have to admit that many of Aimee’s decisions didn’t exactly feel "human": she never questioned his behavior, never called him out, and that final scene? It turned my stomach. There’s no world in which I’d smile at someone who abandoned me — even temporarily.
Anyway… Solid overall, with plenty of positives, but the relationship felt extremely one-sided, to the point where I’m still not entirely sure what this project was trying to say.

reviews.Name: Supersonic
reviews.Review:
A documentary I find genuinely important — not just because of the band’s influence on pop music, but because of the dangerous side effects that come with fame and pop culture obsession.
We’re talking about two guys who, in my opinion, were a bit “off” from a very young age and skyrocketed to fame with ridiculously catchy and nostalgic tracks. Their childhoods weren’t exactly ideal, and they clearly didn’t get the support they needed during their so-called “glory years.”
Beautifully structured, with concert footage that left me speechless — even knowing it came from the same guys that produced "Amy".

And that phrase… “ya know what I mean” — it’ll stick with me forever.
To answer you, Liam: no mate, I never knew what you meant. Just own it.

reviews.Name: The Sea of Trees
reviews.Review:
A film where I can easily understand the criticism it received.
As extraordinary as the actors were, there’s one major point that left me a bit disappointed: pretty much every scene set in the forest. It all felt overly forced and painfully methodical — as if the film was trying too hard to make things more dramatic and “challenging” for the characters.
As for the rest of the story, nothing major to complain about aside from a few details I also found unnecessary.
A decent movie, but one with quite a few flaws... Is it worth watching? I’ll leave that to whoever feels like taking my review and rating into account.

reviews.Name: Stop Making Sense
reviews.Review:
THIS WAS SO, SO MUCH FUN.
I was peacefully applying my exfoliating cream and suddenly found myself dancing in the middle of the room like I was part of the band.
I "stopped making sense" myself — the body took over, the soul left, and the brain shut down as quickly as David Byrne puts on that giant suit.
Each song better than the one before — and those transitions? Absolutely euphoric.
The only problem? My skin is glowing but now my heart wants to live in 1984, on a stage, with dramatic lights and choreographies no one asked but we’re all thankful for.

reviews.Name: De Palma
reviews.Review:
It was extraordinarily fun to watch, mainly because he was talking directly to us the entire time.
I loved the way he broke down the concept of artistic freedom vs. the film industry, and I agree with him 100% — at least from what I can observe from the outside.

He's a director who's often misunderstood, but thankfully has unmistakable classics under his belt.
Here, he opens up about all the struggles he faced in his early years in a documentary that’s brilliantly executed and genuinely interesting.

reviews.Name: Val
reviews.Review:
I tend to cry with this kind of documentary — and this one was no exception.
Even though it wasn’t Kilmer himself narrating (due to his throat cancer), it still felt incredibly intimate, and you could tell they made a real effort to keep that tone from beginning to end.

The ‘80s and ‘90s — an actor completely misunderstood, still untouched by the molds Hollywood tends to force onto artists like him, and with so much to say.
The use of personal footage and archives really helped paint a clearer picture of Val Kilmer’s reality and inner world, and I feel like the message came through perfectly:
“I’m more than my roles. I’m more than my voice.”

reviews.Name: The Sky Is Everywhere
reviews.Review:
Another project I honestly don’t know how I made it through… The message might be important and, in some ways, well delivered — but the story itself was so dull and lacking in substance.
The cinematography was all over the place, with so many different elements thrown in that I got tired of every scene pretty quickly.

Yes, it has some interesting points, and sure, the cinematography wasn’t all bad the entire time…
But I just can’t give it a higher rating than this.

reviews.Name: Backstabbing for Beginners
reviews.Review:
As soon as I realized it was an adaptation of an autobiographical book, I immediately assumed it was going to be a boring film… And guess what? It really was.

The themes are undeniably important — I’m not here to downplay the social and ethical issues within the UN, especially regarding the aid to Iraq, as presented in the film — but I just can’t understand how they failed to make it more engaging.
Everything was well structured in theory, but the dialogues? They made me zone out completely.
The cinematography didn’t help either — dull and tedious — and the actors did what they could to keep the film alive.

reviews.Name: Boys State
reviews.Review:
WOW, what a brilliantly made piece!
It really seemed like an intense political thriller, full of twists, with behind-the-scenes and everything that happened “outside” the “political life” created by the students.

I have to highlight the incredible ability they had to create appealing and REAL characters — completely plausible in American society or any other, really.
Special mention goes to Ben Feinstein: strong and intimidating personality (unfortunately, guys like this do exist), and he irritated me from start to finish — just like the CHEGA folks in Portugal. COMPLETE DISGUST.

You can honestly learn A LOT from this documentary. It’s fascinating, concerning, and downright TERRIFYING.

reviews.Name: First Cow
reviews.Review:
I won’t lie — I was expecting a much duller film than what I actually experienced.
It’s such a simple story, but the drama is so well done that I genuinely found myself hooked on everything happening. Still, I can’t help but criticize a few scenes that, to me, felt unnecessary and really disrupted the film’s pace.

This is a feature that could easily be 40 minutes shorter — there are slow, dragging parts that momentarily made me disconnect from the subject.

That said, I won’t take away from the masterful cinematography, soundtrack, and performances, which carried a story that, while simple, holds serious historical and cultural weight.

reviews.Name: First Reformed
reviews.Review:
Exactly 27 minutes have passed since I finished watching this film…
So far, I’ve only stared at my computer screen with Letterboxd open. I don’t think I’ve even blinked, and I can feel my body frozen, covered in goosebumps.

It’s a film with such emotional, cultural, ethical, religious, and philosophical weight that I’m certain I’ll be thinking about it for weeks—maybe even months—as I recommend it to friends and family.
Every criticism aimed at the Church is delivered in a way that made my heart skip a beat whenever something out of the ordinary happened... The same goes for every time Toller read from his journal: my heart would race, my nerves would spike, and the build-up of tension exploded in that final act.

An ending that left me speechless—so spiritual, so profound, with touches of redemption. Almost imaginary... or was it real?

reviews.Name: Gloria Bell
reviews.Review:
Julianne Moore has such an absurd range that she manages to turn what I see as a bland and hollow role into something absolutely incredible… I'm giving this rating precisely because of that.
It fails in terms of camerawork, it fails with almost every character, and there are flaws in the story that make everything feel a bit uninteresting.
It was probably intentional, but they really could’ve explored Turturro’s character a lot more.

reviews.Name: Green Room
reviews.Review:
I won't lie — watching neo-Nazis be stupid, incompetent, and get killed gave me a level of satisfaction that honestly left me a bit concerned (I mean, they literally got shot in the head and that kind of violence is disturbing).
Even though I enjoyed that aspect — and seriously, fuck those little bastards — I felt like they could’ve done a better job developing the characters, especially the band.
I didn’t really feel connected to them, which made me drift off a bit… I even found myself asking who the hell some character was when they suddenly showed up.
It’s fun, I had a good time, and the cinematography is pretty solid for the kind of film this is.
But please — don’t go around calling this horror, okay?

reviews.Name: Into the Forest
reviews.Review:
What even happened here? Why did they lose power in the first place? And sorry to whoever wrote this story, but the dad’s death was completely unnecessary and just plain stupid. One more thing: why did they burn down the house? Makes zero sense.

And look, the points I just mentioned don’t even begin to cover the suffering I went through during the runtime... Mistake after mistake, a script with more holes than my grandpa’s hairline.
The only thing that slightly saved this project were the performances and the halfway decent cinematography — because other than that, it’s just not worth it.

reviews.Name: Love Lies Bleeding
reviews.Review:
Will Lou be different from her father? Is this love an escape or just another form of destruction?
These were the main questions I was left with after watching this film. It’s definitely not the kind of story I usually enjoy, and that’s also why I’m giving it this rating — but I can’t ignore the brilliance of the performances and the extraordinary use of color in the cinematography.
If they had focused more on Daisy, or if they hadn’t played so much with symbolism versus realism, it would’ve made everything even heavier — and I would’ve liked it A LOT more.
It’s a solid film, but not one I’ll be revisiting.

reviews.Name: Moonlight
reviews.Review:
A revolutionary film in every sense: cinematography, performances, screenplay.
I'm trying to write something now so I can look back and remember what I felt watching this — but the truth is, nothing will ever make me forget this feature. Nothing.
Beautiful. Haunting. Dialogues that seem simple but carry so much emotional weight. Glances that will stay with me forever.
Such important topics, portrayed in three different phases of a miserable life... Sexuality, drugs, bullying — so much symbolic weight between scenes that it all built up to constant chills and tears.

Ps and edited: I now understand why La La Land didn't win an Oscar for best picture

reviews.Name: On Becoming a Guinea Fowl
reviews.Review:
A film with somewhat disturbing messages about family traditions...
It's impossible to ignore the fact that a loved one is a criminal, and they managed to portray the worst of human nature in just an hour and a half.
It’s not a masterpiece or anything close to it, but the story itself is well thought out and nicely structured.
It’s one of those films I probably wouldn’t have watched if it weren’t an A24 project.

reviews.Name: Pi
reviews.Review:
Friends, comrades, and fellow sufferers — during my years in a Physics degree, I never quite reached this level of madness… but I’ve had to deal with plenty of lunatics in my department. I just hope I don't lose it completely once I start my Master's.
This was a rollercoaster of emotions: one moment I felt fear, the next my heart was racing from the sheer stress caused not only by the main character but also by the soundtrack and cinematography.
An extremely well-crafted project, gripping from start to finish, and a completely unconventional thriller.

reviews.Name: Priscilla
reviews.Review:
It’s not a perfect film. In my opinion, it’s actually quite far from it… But I can clearly understand the importance of a film like this and the masterful cinematography I witnessed. Delicate, with stunning color work and an aesthetic that left me speechless.
My issue was really with the film’s pace — very slow, with scenes that felt overly drawn out without much point, and others that could’ve had a much stronger impact on the viewer (like the suite scene near the end).

reviews.Name: Problemista
reviews.Review:
Don’t compare this kind of project to Wes Anderson… Wes Anderson’s style is unique to Wes Anderson films.
Yes, it’s true — I had never seen cinematography quite like this, but I couldn’t really enjoy most of the surrealist scenes. It felt like too much, a bit unnecessary.
The story itself is extremely interesting, with strong critiques of American society and immigration policies — especially relevant in today’s world — but all of that, in my view, gets drowned out by a sequence of reality-defying scenes that made me lose focus.

reviews.Name: Queer
reviews.Review:
I’m still trying to make sense of some scenes in the film, but honestly, I don’t think Luca even wants me to understand — he wants me to feel.
It’s not exactly a film with a complex premise, quite the opposite, actually. The story is simple, but the surrealism and the dreamlike concept of desire make it all the more immersive and, in my opinion, absolutely worth experiencing.
Strange as it sounds, this was the first Guadagnino film I’ve seen. I’m not particularly interested in watching Challengers — and believe me, I’m a tennis fanatic — maybe because of all the memes and the whole online circus around the project in the sports world.
But now I do have a real and specific interest in unpacking certain scenes from this film, because while it felt “weird” to my eyes, it was also deeply fascinating.
A cinematography that’s extremely challenging for general audiences… Maybe it needs a rewatch for my rating to go up or down (still torn between 3.5 and 4 stars).

reviews.Name: Revenge of the Green Dragons
reviews.Review:
What a waste of time.
Editing? I’ve seen PowerPoint transitions with more finesse.
A plot straight out of the "How to Be Clichê" handbook, violence for the sake of something, actors who seemed just as confused as I was, and a TERRIBLE cinematography
Honestly, I’m impressed — it takes effort to miss every mark this consistently.

reviews.Name: Sharper
reviews.Review:
I honestly don’t get the people who said the structure of this film was confusing — were we watching the same movie? It’s literally built to throw twists at you until the very last second, and to be fair, that was actually a lot of fun. It kept me wide awake, hanging on to every line, every suspicious glance.

That said, I do have two issues.
First — and here comes the irony — while I just defended the structure, I do think the critics had a point... just not the one they made. It’s not confusing — it’s just not very rewatchable. I can’t really see myself going back to it knowing what I know now.
The second problem: it's clear the film had a low budget. Yes, it's an A24 film, it's supposed to have cost little, but most of their catalogue is filled with films that cost even less and whose effects are a thousand times better (and yes, I'm talking about The Lighthouse, for example).

reviews.Name: Showing Up
reviews.Review:
I'll start right away with the cinematography, which in my opinion was the standout element (aside from the performances of pretty much every actor involved)... It's really simple, but well edited, with incredible colors and very well executed.
The main issue that kept me from liking it as much as I probably should have (because the project as a whole is very solid) has to do with the slow-paced scenes that popped up throughout the film: they took too long to unfold and I would lose interest easily.
The metaphor and connection with the pigeon was sensational, and I have to point out that André 3K is an incredibly underrated actor.

reviews.Name: Skin
reviews.Review:
No. Just no.
So now we’re out here distinguishing between “good” and “bad” Nazis? Really?
The only thing even remotely worth mentioning is the cinematography.

If the goal was to make me feel conflicted or emotionally torn… congratulations, I’m just confused why this even got made.

reviews.Name: Sinners
reviews.Review:
Taking a little break from the A24 catalogue just to say that this is probably going to be the best 2025 film.
Hoping that Eddington is as good as this.

reviews.Name: Slow West
reviews.Review:
I’ve said it many times and I’ll say it again: westerns are just not for me.
This one stays true to the genre, knows exactly what it’s doing — especially when it comes to cinematography (some truly gorgeous shots, I’ll admit) — but for me, the pacing didn’t work.
I found myself bored at times, and the climax didn’t hit as hard as it should have, precisely because of that.
The story is well told, and the actors did a great job, but again, the pacing kind of drags down my overall impression of the film.

reviews.Name: Son of a Gun
reviews.Review:
Predictable, dull, and flavorless — that’s all I can really take from this film.
Was I expecting much? No.
Did I think it would be this bland? Also no.

reviews.Name: The Adderall Diaries
reviews.Review:
I just couldn’t take this film seriously.
Not sure if it was Amber Heard or the story itself — maybe both — but it really didn’t work for me.
Way too many unnecessary scenes (especially around Stephen and Lana’s relationship), painfully basic cinematography, and a plot so predictable I felt like I could’ve written it in my sleep.
It honestly felt like they threw in a bunch of last-minute ideas hoping it would make things cooler… but nope, it just made everything worse.

reviews.Name: The Ballad of Lefty Brown
reviews.Review:
Yes, I’ve said it plenty of times: I don’t like westerns.
But imagine this — I’d rather win a bet and watch the entire A24 catalogue than give up halfway through because of this film. 
There are definitely some positives, mostly in the cinematography: beautiful shots, many of them simple, evoking the classic essence of the genre.
It’s a film that tries to cast heroism onto a character who was never built to be a hero.
Many scenes were just plain dull (speaking as someone who has no plans to revisit this type of story).

The actors were strong, sure, but even they couldn’t save it from being a slog.

reviews.Name: The Blackcoat's Daughter
reviews.Review:
This rating is mostly because I also gave "Saint Maud" 3.5 stars… Honestly, I think I need to rewatch both films — I genuinely feel like this score might go up.

Truth is, it’s not exactly a horror film: sure, it has elements of the genre, but I’d say it leans much more toward a psychological thriller. I’m seriously considering changing my rating to 4 stars (and "Saint Maud" too) simply because this is such a well-crafted project, with every part clearly defined and tightly written.

An eerie, spine-chilling soundtrack kicks in right when it should, and the suspense scenes are so well done. I get why people criticize the “slow burn” approach and say it takes a while for anything major to happen — but I actually think that worked in its favour and made everything even more grounded and compelling.

PS and edited: I’ve decided to change the ratings for both films… I truly think they deserve more recognition for the way they approached horror — something far from the typical style we keep seeing in adjacent works (looking at you, "The Conjuring" universe).

reviews.Name: The Death of Dick Long
reviews.Review:
I’m not gonna lie — when I found out how Richard died, I burst out laughing… Now, should I have?
I guess that’s the whole point of the film: making you question how far comedy can really go.
It’s a solid film, though not without flaws — especially on the investigative/police side of things.
The soundtrack? Absolutely perfect. And I truly hope the use of Nickelback at the end was meant as an extra comedic touch.

reviews.Name: The End of the Tour
reviews.Review:
"He wants more than he has. I want precisely what he already has." – David Lipsky
I wasn’t prepared for this, and honestly, I never thought a project like this would affect me the way it did. I really expected it to be dull or at least boring at times, but I was hooked as soon as I started to understand more about the personalities of the two characters.
What a journey. It really makes you reflect on what you want for your future, and I nearly had an existential crisis when I heard these lines:
"David thought books existed to stop you from feeling lonely. If I could, I'd say to David that living those days with him reminded me of what life is like, instead of being a relief from it. And I'd tell him it made me feel much less alone."
A simple yet effective cinematography — this is a film that places all its weight on the script and the actors, and they were absolutely breathtaking.

reviews.Name: The Florida Project
reviews.Review:
I'm going to be completely straightforward in this review because, after watching three of Sean’s projects, I still feel the same: films with brilliant cinematography but stories that never grab me from the start.
Unfortunately, I just can’t connect with the scripts he brings to his features. This one in particular felt dull for most of the runtime — except, clearly, for the final part.
The premise does have interesting elements, yes, but it ends up falling short. Honestly, it didn’t need to be such a long film to say everything it wanted to say.
I don’t want to sound insensitive — and I promise I actually cried at the end — the run to Disney World was extremely powerful and full of symbolism.

reviews.Name: The Hole in the Ground
reviews.Review:
A film with no substance, no explanations, and characters making completely irrational decisions (never in my life would I go running or exercising in the forest knowing what's out there and that things clearly aren’t safe).
I feel like I have a lot more to say about how the film was shot than anything else, because honestly, the colors and cinematography really impressed me — that’s a major positive.
But it fails in pretty much everything else.

reviews.Name: Ghost Rider
reviews.Review:
This movie was genuinely part of my childhood, but watching it again at 22 years old made me question my taste in films.
Irony aside: it’s a project that doesn’t do justice to the character — someone complex, with personal and religious struggles, and who had done deep research on the religious theme.
The editing was extremely poor, often cutting to completely unrelated scenes in such an awkward way, and the story was ridiculously simple for what Johnny actually represents.

reviews.Name: Green Lantern
reviews.Review: Why do I do this to myself?

reviews.Name: Oppenheimer
reviews.Review:
One of the greatest films of all time.
Rewatching the most terrifying story humanity has ever experienced… It leaves me devastated every single time.
It’s a story that pushed me toward Physics, that made me follow this path — not because of the political context, but because of the names involved: from Rabi to Feynman, physicists who have always crossed my path, whether through mandatory study or my own personal research.
When I watched it for the first time, already knowing the whole story (thanks to my obsession with everyone involved), I was blown away by the level of detail. And the truth is, to this day, I’m still in awe of the dialogue and depth embedded in this project.

Man… I LOVE PHYSICS. Even when they called me stupid… and honestly, it’s exactly in those moments that I felt an even stronger urge to study harder.
Masochistic or not, it was a degree that brought me genuine joy with every passing year.

reviews.Name: The Fantastic 4: First Steps
reviews.Review:
It’s likely going to make it onto my list of favorite MCU movies — but it definitely has its flaws… especially in the story.

I’m giving it 4 stars because of how it compares to other superhero films, both MCU and DCU. The setting is AMAZING, the aesthetic is sensational — like a Fantastic Four comic come to life — with each character’s personality so well defined that it makes everything more engaging. I truly felt the family dynamic here, not just “friends who went through the same accident.” They’ve always been a FAMILY.

There are some issues with the Silver Surfer (they could’ve emphasized her story more) and Galactus (they kind of made him a bit dumb), but they don’t ruin the overall film.

reviews.Name: Superman
reviews.Review:
Another James Gunn-style movie that, in my opinion, never gets boring or tiring… I FINALLY saw Superman. I FINALLY saw a man who’s vulnerable and genuinely concerned about EVERYTHING around him. They finally nailed the personality and complex character that is Clark Kent — aka the most powerful meta-human of all.

A cinematography that does justice to everything good about comic books, with incredible details both in camera work and in Clark’s relationships with the other characters. I’m excited to see this new DCU phase — please don’t ruin what is, to me, an almost perfect start.

PS: I compared this film with every superhero movie ever made, that’s why I gave this rating. It’s not the perfect movie and definitely not one of the best ever made.

reviews.Name: The clown within us
reviews.Review:
I promote this film with all my heart, just as Eduardo gave his own in making this short film. A Portuguese film, in true Portuguese fashion, with beautiful images of a city I’m honored to know and be part of, our beautiful Porto. The color, the camera, the actor, the acts, and the music — all brought together in an 18-minute session, everything perfect, with the love for his father always present.

Now in Portuguese: que maravilha Eduardo, a saudade e o amor descritos de forma tão simples mas tão especial, tão comovente, espero que vás longe❤️

reviews.Name: Weapons
reviews.Review:
When Archer said “weaponized”: PEAK CINEMA.

I really want to talk about this film… we are looking at a director who’s capable of revolutionizing horror in today’s projects. I don’t want sequels, prequels, nor do I intend to watch films made by someone who only sees money in front of them and doesn’t do it for love (yes, I’m talking to you, The Conjuring universe).

A film that will stay in my memory not only for the horror but for the SUSPENSE… And the camera work, my friends, THE CAMERA WORK, BRILLIANT.

reviews.Name: The King
reviews.Review:
I just can’t understand the criticism towards this film… It’s without a doubt one of the best projects I’ve ever seen in this genre. Perhaps it’s worth mentioning that I love Shakespeare’s plays, and seeing one of the most fervent and historic ones portrayed in a feature film suspiciously made my rating end up at this level.

A historical and vengeful weight incredibly well presented, with everything that needed to be addressed and outlined, carrying Shakespearean marks all throughout the film. An Oscar-worthy performance — Chalamet shook both soil and sea with chilling, extraordinary speeches. A powerhouse cast that came together for a story that gave me chills and happens to be one of my favorites from school. My only regret is not having watched this sooner.

reviews.Name: Ponyo
reviews.Review: Not the best Studio Ghibli project, but I can’t help but praise the incredible and sensational creativity Miyazaki has to craft something of this kind. A wonderful story and an animation made with love and care culminate in a production rich with beautiful messages for younger audiences. It’s truly a pleasure to dive deeper into these films, and they only make me want to see them all… Ponyo wanted to become human, but only love could make it happen — such a beautiful moral, one that will stay with me.

reviews.Name: Forgotten
reviews.Review: It’s not a perfect film, far from it. The plot twists felt a bit forced, and the way everything was revealed was quite predictable, never really managing to keep up the suspense (except maybe during the first half hour). I’m giving it 3.5 stars mainly because it’s clearly a low-budget film, with solid actors and a well-structured story — though it definitely falls short when it comes to mystery and shock value.

reviews.Name: Memories of Murder
reviews.Review:
I’m in complete shock.
Still thinking about the ending, still wondering if it was one person or another.
