Still processing the absurd number of suspects, all of them seemingly “normal.” I won’t go into the film’s specific message because I don’t want to spoil it — I don’t want to take away the pleasure of experiencing it for the first time, just as I did. Glued to the screen from the very first to the very last minute, ending with a final gasp and absolute shock. A masterpiece in every sense. A story that will stay with me forever.

reviews.Name: Eddington
reviews.Review:
I hope my life never takes as many turns as this, but then again, I don’t plan on becoming a politician — way too much work for a humanity that’s growing increasingly stupid.
I don’t think it was easy to deal with this script and still manage to project all the criticism Aster wanted to explore. You all know my love for this guy, especially since I’ve seen EVERYTHING he’s ever released, even back when he was still studying — which is also why I don’t consider this to be his weakest film. On the contrary.
There are SEVERAL critiques of humanity as a whole, set against one of the darkest periods of our civilization. A blind, senseless conservatism mixed with extreme activism, all culminating in 2 hours and 50 minutes of pure chaos, entertainment, and food for thought. It’s in extreme moments that humans can truly become monsters — and here, we get to witness that transformation in a way that’s transparent, deeply detailed, and striking not only in its story but also visually and philosophically.
If I was already a fan of Aster’s camerawork, this project only solidified it further — it’s what stood out the most for me.
4.5 stars? We’ll see… for now, just 4, I need to see this again.

Still one of the best filmmakers of our time.

reviews.Name: Bo Burnham: Inside
reviews.Review:
This is very  special to me. In every possible way. A safe place, a different world or some cozy spot to not be in panic.

Hope you’re doing fine Bo, we miss you…

reviews.Name: Bo Burnham: The Inside Outtakes
reviews.Review:
“I DON’T KNOW WHAT’S HAPPENING
WHAT THE FUCK IS GOING IN?” - The definition of my life.

Still perfect.

reviews.Name: Kiss the Girls
reviews.Review:
I think it nails the suspense and the thriller aspects, but it misses the mark in other areas… the story is predictable, the relationship between the serial killer and the police was barely explored, and the ending felt a bit unnecessary.
I wouldn’t say it’s a bad film, not at all. I just think they keep using the same formula, and it’s starting to get tiring… and I’m not only talking about movies from that era, I’m also referring to (and criticizing) the wave of police thrillers that keep coming out nowadays.

reviews.Name: About Schmidt
reviews.Review: It wasn’t a film that really appealed to me… I won’t lie, the premise itself is funny and extremely introspective, but I can’t help criticizing both the script and the direction. Dragged-out and unnecessary scenes that cut away from the essence and the supposed “joke” of the film. I couldn’t connect with the main character, and it made it hard to stay interested in the film.

reviews.Name: HIM
reviews.Review:
Did it manage to entertain me? Yes.
Will I ever watch it again in my life? Absolutely not.

I think a lot of people will want to see it — or already have — simply because of the monster of a producer that Jordan Peele is. For those who haven’t seen it yet, here’s a fair warning: it’s really nothing special. Don’t assume that just because Peele’s name is on the credits the film is automatically good — quite the opposite, actually.

I’d be lying if I said the film looks bad, though. Visually, it’s detailed, disturbing, and filled with bold imagery. But the story? Ridiculous. The moment I realized it was just another movie about a cult, any hope I had for it completely vanished. Sure, I get the attempt to criticize the rot behind certain sports stereotypes — but to turn that into an absurd, over-the-top final battle? Just unnecessary, and it only made me like it less.

Fun, but ridiculous. I expected so much more.

reviews.Name: Goldman v Silverman
reviews.Review:
I’m mentally preparing myself for two upcoming Safdie brothers films, because I really don’t want either of them to miss. “Uncut Gems” and “Good Time” are easily two of the best films of the 21st century — and honestly, they’ve proudly made it into my list of favorite A24 projects, and favorite films ever. Seeing the two brothers going their separate creative ways genuinely scares me. Protect them at all costs.

This short film captures perfectly the struggle of a street performer trying to showcase his art. A sharp critique of society, of the absurd “designated performance zones” in major cities (not just in New York — I see plenty of that here in Portugal, in Porto), and of the humiliation and mockery that exist even within the profession itself. Seven minutes that fly by naturally — simple, yet incredibly powerful.

reviews.Name: Daddy Longlegs
reviews.Review:
Still preparing to dive into their solo films.

The first serious project by the Safdie brothers manages to offer plenty of positives: a production that, no matter how simple it may seem, touches the soul — almost in a documentary-like format — and a story that maintains its sensibility, strength, and emotion from beginning to end.
I started watching the feature film without knowing it was inspired by the Safdie brothers’ real experiences with their father. But as I said, the production itself hinted that there was something genuine behind those scenes.
Deeply moving — at its core, it’s a portrait filled with both love and pain. The Safdies don’t paint the father as a villain, nor do they romanticize him. They show him as someone who loves his children deeply but is incapable of taking care of them in a stable way — a bittersweet blend of tenderness and irresponsibility.
Very solid for a first film, and an incredible tribute to their childhood.

reviews.Name: Heaven Knows What
reviews.Review:
Another film from the Safdie brothers — and believe me when I say I keep getting more and more amazed by the quality they showed early on in their international independent work.
A film with a “simple” premise managed to transport me into the darker side of New York, filled with social struggles and substance abuse — a project that exposes the city’s decay in an almost documentary-like way, portraying a painful path that’s incredibly hard to escape.

I’m also blown away by the performances and by the way they chose to tell the story. I honestly thought I’d find it boring, but that hour and a half just flew by.

Protect them. I’ve heard that "The Smashing Machine (2025)" isn’t selling well, and that really worries me. I just hope everything goes right for them.

reviews.Name: Primer
reviews.Review:
The goal was never to make us care emotionally about the characters — and that makes it even more fascinating to dive into and discuss some of the scenes that seem almost “random.”
We’re talking about a film that wants to confuse us, yet still gives us just enough clues so we never really get lost. I was even more amazed by the physical explanation behind the machine’s process — entropy, Feynman diagrams, and so many other concepts.
I do feel it could’ve used more time, especially in certain parts, like the party scene… or maybe even added some of those moments that are mentioned but never shown in the film.
Also, the fact that a feature-length film made on a $7,000 budget managed to earn almost $1 million in such a short time — and with a subject this complex — is wild.
I’ll definitely have to rewatch it, try to understand the timelines better… and THE EARPHONES. I’m giving it four stars — and just to be clear, I’m saying that in praise of the film, not the director.

Man, I love Physics. Gotta give credit to my younger self for choosing a degree like this.

reviews.Name: Thank You for Smoking
reviews.Review:
I have to say, this film surprised me in every possible way. A story that naturally exposes the filth of the tobacco industry and the often-hidden contradictions of so-called “sustainable” politicians.
A comedy that opened my eyes to the sin industries and ended up being a genuinely good time. There are some hilarious moments — especially at the table with those three “sinners.”

Yes, my recent watchlist has been a steady stream of 4-star films, but what can I say? I’ve been watching some seriously solid stuff. This one was actually recommended by my Project Management professor.

Lines that had me laughing like crazy:

“- You ratted us out to some reporter with tits.
- Glorious tits!”

“Michael Jordan plays ball, Charles Manson kills people... I talk.”

reviews.Name: One Battle After Another
reviews.Review:
Yeah… I know I’m late to the party with this review, but nothing’s stopping me from sharing a reaction that’s probably the same as everyone else’s on this app: WHAT A SENSATIONAL FILM.
From start to finish, absolutely captivating — with such interesting and genuinely fun characters.
Benicio del Toro, my friend, what a pleasure it is to see you on screen, as always, BRILLIANT. Sean Penn taking on such a tough role and nailing it completely. And of course… I can’t forget the one who’s about to sweep the Oscar for Best Actor — DiCaprio, who somehow manages to outdo himself yet again.

Overall? 5/5. Nothing more to add. I left the theater so hyped to talk about it that I literally looked at myself in the bathroom mirror and said, “Ocean Waves, Diogo, Ocean Waves.”

reviews.Name: After Life
reviews.Review:
"At the time I searched desperately, inside myself, for any memory of happiness. Now, fifty years later, I learned I was part of someone else's happiness. What a wonderful discovery."

I don’t remember ever thinking about the connection between a film and a memory. The truth is, after all these years, I carry incredible memories both inside and outside a movie theater — like when we watched "Megalopolis (2024)" and laughter became the only possible form of emotion, or when we felt “special” and “important” watching films like "Oppenheimer (2023)".
As time goes by and people come and go from my life — some leaving in unfair ways, showing their worst sides — I find myself returning to the same thought: when I think of my happiest memory, I always reach the same conclusion — I’m lucky to have many that I’ll be able to keep for life.

"After Life (1998)" has this incredible ability to make you feel connected to every character, even when the memory they’ve chosen (or are still choosing) means nothing to you personally. Meeting each of them was a journey that left me speechless — from someone who claimed they had no happiness in their life, to a man who simply wanted to feel the power of flying a plane again. The writing is filled with subtle beauty — from lonely lines to quiet conversations between the dead and their helpers.

I’m giving it four and a half stars for one very special reason: I want to rewatch it with the people I love most in this life, so I can finally give it five.

reviews.Name: This Is the End
reviews.Review:
Well… this was bad. Really bad.
I didn’t laugh once, and I was this close to turning it off and just aimlessly scrolling through YouTube until I fell asleep. Watching this was definitely not a good plan.

By the way, don’t ever make me hear or read “Michael Cera is dead” again — I wouldn’t be able to handle it. Don’t hurt my GOAT🐐

reviews.Name: Vincent
reviews.Review:
An absolute masterpiece. I’m speechless. How can something so short have such an impact?

Oh and by the way… Same, Vincent — Edgar Allan Poe drives me crazy too, but I love him for it.

reviews.Name: The Fall
reviews.Review:
I’m terrified of what could come out of a Jonathan Glazer horror feature. And that fear is exactly what I desperately need… I want to walk into a movie theater already scared before the film even starts.
A Glazer horror film would be everything I both want and don’t want — leaving me with psychological scars forever.

reviews.Name: Former Cult Member Hears Music for the First Time
reviews.Review:
I think I went through the same thing when I heard Geese for the first time.
Did I hit my head on a table? No, but I did feel like screaming from all the different sounds I was hearing (Cameron Winter, you magnificent beast).

Jokes aside... an EXTREMELY IMPORTANT message.

reviews.Name: The Long Walk
reviews.Review:
I was a bit undecided about what rating to give this film. Throughout the hour and forty minutes, I found myself growing attached to the characters, but at the same time, I felt like this would be one of those projects you watch once and never return to.
The truth is, the ending is so predictable that, besides leaving me disappointed, it confirmed that earlier thought — I really won’t be watching it again.

Amazing actors? Yes. Interesting premise? Also yes.
Did they manage to shape it properly and give it a fitting ending? Absolutely not.
It felt like they just wanted to wrap things up quickly so everyone could go home — even if that wasn’t actually the case.

reviews.Name: Cure
reviews.Review:
“You think there has to be a reason, a meaning behind the killings. But what if there isn’t one?”

I felt hypnotized the entire film — which only shows how powerful it is in every frame, every scene. A project that might sound cliché from its synopsis but becomes incredibly dark and philosophical as you slowly lose yourself in it.

A meticulous slow burn, with moments and lines that will stay with me forever — a masterpiece in every sense.
Every time I think about what was real or not, I get even more lost. I’ll probably keep replaying every possible moment in my head for days, maybe weeks. What a film.

Asian horror had already enchanted me with "Onibaba (1964)", but now it’s completely won me over — if that’s even possible in the truest sense of the word.

A perfect movie to watch on Halloween night.

reviews.Name: Napoleon Dynamite
reviews.Review:
I have a few negative things to say about this film, and that’s also why my rating is on the lower side…
But those 3.5 stars came from one simple reason - yes, THAT scene. The scene that made me burst out laughing and kept a huge smile on my face until the very end. It’s worth watching the whole thing just for that moment.

A film that cost around 400.000 dollars to make, filled with extremely awkward dialogues, which somehow made me feel connected to the main character.
My issues with it come from the fact that, for most of its runtime, I didn’t laugh once - and nearly every other character felt forgettable, lacking any real spark or depth.

reviews.Name: Frankenstein
reviews.Review:
“Perhaps, I thought, this was the way of the world. It would hunt you and kill you just for being who you are”

I read a review that said this was the version where Viktor tried to be a father and somehow managed to be worse than usual - I think that perfectly sums up the film.

Guillermo del Toro has already spoiled us all with his meticulous attention to detail (especially in his recent projects), next-level production, and direction that keeps the viewer hooked from start to finish - but the truth is, this is the film that brings all of that together and showcases every quality that makes him such a brilliant director, writer, and producer.

Mia Goth takes on an extremely difficult role and delivers it flawlessly; Oscar Isaac is just Oscar Isaac (there’s no other way to describe him - genius), and Jacob Elordi transcends in this role, making the creature even more iconic, a bit more unsettling, and above all: much more human.

I just want everyone - Netflix included, and whoever decided to label this as “horror” - to understand that this project will never truly be considered a horror film.
Sure, Frankenstein is a horror story (though it has much more to it: ethical philosophy, morality, and a tragic love story), but this adaptation doesn’t belong to that concept of horror.

reviews.Name: The Secret Agent
reviews.Review:
Tiago, forgive me, for I’m about to sin with this review.

I think it’s common knowledge by now how powerful and high-quality Brazilian cinema can be - the way it can move us, disturb us, and confront us, especially when dealing with dark, obscene, and painfully human subjects like this one. But I don’t think this film is for everyone, and honestly, it didn’t sit that well with me.

We’re talking about a story set during an extremely diabolical and ruthless dictatorship, where information is limited and everyone seems desperate to hide their past just to have a decent future. But when a film of this length tries to convey that idea in EVERY SINGLE SCENE, it becomes hard to stay engaged.

In the first few minutes, we already understand how hard life is for each character - they’re “refugees,” unable to build a proper life outside the city or even the country. We also quickly realize how little information existed at that time - and that’s enough to get the message across.

There were scenes so long they made me drift out of the film, and others where the camera would linger on a character meant for brief comic relief, staying there for what felt like endless seconds…

The story itself is well structured, but it bothered me not to see any real ending for the main character. Did it get the message about lack of information across? Yes - but I had already understood that moral two hours earlier.

Overall: an important message, a solid story, and an extremely well-executed production - but one that fails in editing and in truly communicating with its audience.

This is Brazil’s pick for the Oscars, so… let’s see how that turns out. 🇧🇷🎬

Oh, and by the way… that scene with the leg? Horrendous.

reviews.Name: Look Back
reviews.Review:
Everyone has an artistic side that can be expressed in countless ways. Writing is part of my routine - I write for myself, but sometimes I think about the possibility of someone reading my poetry and feeling connected to me. Writing belongs to me, but reading also shares connections and emotions; reading also makes us human.

Anyone even slightly interested in manga knows how inhumane the industry can be... stories of artists who admit to sleeping only two or three hours a day just to release a new chapter. The love for art surpasses any kind of hard work - and it’s easy to see that in our own lives: when students love a subject, they’re far more likely to succeed in it than in one they dislike but force themselves through just to keep up. Working on something you love always carries an immense weight in your success.

A story that gives you chills even before you realize it’s semi-autobiographical. Something that will stay with me forever. 

No more words - perfect.

reviews.Name: Society of the Snow
reviews.Review:
"Hay que regresar al pasado sabiendo que el pasado es lo que más cambia."

Don’t ask me why I had never watched this before - I definitely dropped the ball when it came to the international film category that year, so much so that I also only watched The Zone of Interest (2023) this year…

I genuinely feel like I became dehydrated while watching the final minutes of this film. The writing is so introspective, philosophical, hopeful, that I almost forgot who was saying those words… And when I finally came back down to Earth and fully realized it, I cried even more.

The strongest aspects of this film lie in the narrator and in the sheer realism of every scene. They almost transported me directly into that world, and it deeply unsettled me from beginning to end. The actors gave everything in roles that are as disturbing as they are emotionally demanding, and the direction, in my view, has a crucial role in keeping us fully engaged all the way through.

reviews.Name: Irreversible
reviews.Review:
This is a five-star film, but I'm only giving it four because I never intend to watch it again.
Gaspar Noé, you're one of the very few directors capable of making me feel like this: helpless, uncomfortable, on the verge of jumping out of my seat, wanting to look away because I physically cannot sit through certain scenes. The most radical, explicit, raw, cruel, barbaric scene I have ever seen in my entire life. Incredibly controversial (how on earth did they allow this in a film?) and profoundly morbid. Nine minutes. Nine.

A film shot in five weeks, with most of the script created by the actors themselves (improvised scenes) and a party that was actually happening, with people genuinely drunk interfering with the shoot - all executed flawlessly. And the way the camera follows the characters is itself drunk - amazing. The fact that it's told in reverse only makes it hurt more: getting to know the characters' details in a way that builds a connection at the very end is devastating.

I felt powerless the whole time. It's a film I don’t ever want to watch again.

reviews.Name: Bugonia
reviews.Review:
I think it’s completely understandable if people don’t consider this Yorgos’s best film - and, in my view, it doesn’t reach the madness of The Lobster or the visual and technical fascination of Poor Things. But let’s be honest: this was the most fun I’ve ever had with one of his films.

A movie capable of showing just how ridiculous a human being can be, where the project itself embraces that ridiculousness and pulls us into a universe that really isn’t that far from our own. It doesn’t take long for the insanity to escalate, but the last 30 minutes shine above everything that came before.

Yorgos… you always manage to surprise me, you know that.

Human beings have no solution. We are our own colonizers — one of the few species capable of destroying itself.

Ps: Jesse Plemons has been taking on roles I could easily see Dafoe playing… I kept watching and thinking: “Dafoe, is that you?”

reviews.Name: The Metamorphosis of Birds
reviews.Review:
“A distância daqueles que nós amamos é tão difícil. Porém ela é também muito construtiva. Constrói quem eu sou.”

Poetry and cinema joined forces to create this film.
During this short and chaotic journey of mine through cinema, there has always been something that bothered me: not knowing and not having truly felt Portuguese film and Portuguese cinema. I’m here to change that, and I feel like I started in the best way possible - what an unbelievable project.
Unsettling dialogues, meaningful from start to finish, with metaphors that follow us as we get to know the family, all culminating in that final scene. Chilling from beginning to end, a poem that came straight from the heart.

I feel an enormous pride in being Portuguese and in seeing my culture represented in such a beautiful way. A pride in our Latin roots, a pride in our mixture, a pride in the Portuguese saudade of being who we are.

“Deixa-me sempre muito espantado como é que uma coisa tão infinita consegue entrar por nós a dentro” - e agora em português, o oceano é assustador, mas será sempre a nossa casa.

reviews.Name: On Falling
reviews.Review:
Precarious work, no chance of moving up, a job that turns you into a prisoner and makes all your personal hobbies disappear - a fear I’ve always had since I started my degree. There were moments when I studied so much that every form of escape from stress just vanished, consumed by the anxiety of finishing a certain class. It scared me, tore me apart, and above all made me rethink my future. Even with financial struggles, I don’t want to let go of the things that make me who I am - whether it’s writing, watching films, or listening to music on my record player. I don’t want to lose my identity.
This film addresses this topic beautifully, this ability we have to dehumanize ourselves just to survive. Simple, but with a powerful message. Joana, I always knew you were a great actress, but here you truly stood out. Portuguese cinema gains so much from projects like this, and it only makes me feel proud of our artistic and cultural growth - beyond our four walls (Portugal), sharing it with the world. This film can make that connection possible, and I’m eager to see it win more awards.

reviews.Name: The Banshees of Inisherin
reviews.Review:
"He was a dull man, to be sure, but a dull man is better than a sharp man who takes off his own fingers."

This film will always have a special place in my heart. Forever. Rewatching this project gives me moments of inner reflection, moments where I think about my future, and it makes me feel alive in every single frame. Perfect.

reviews.Name: Train Dreams
reviews.Review:
So: nominated for Best Actor at the Oscars, right?

I absolutely have to talk about Joel Edgerton, who delivered a staggering performance, hit with an emotional weight that’s impossible to ignore - watching him embody something like this gave me chills from beginning to end.

I struggled to settle on a rating for this project, because as visually stunning as it is, with a strong narrator and a compelling story, there are still a few things I would change - mainly in the middle section. It’s a feature that gets almost everything right, but by the end it left me interpreting the story in different ways and wishing certain scenes had been approached differently.
The way grief was portrayed was masterfully executed: it made me angry, it made me sad, it made me think about all the moments in my life where I felt like the main character… uncomfortable, melancholic - and absolutely beautiful.

reviews.Name: My Neighbor Totoro
reviews.Review:
A project so simple, yet with a magic that never goes unnoticed - capable of enchanting even adults like me.
Beautiful from start to finish. Studio Ghibli’s animations have never failed me: it’s always time wonderfully spent.
I’m amazed by the love they pour into their drawings, and even more amazed by how clearly that love shines through on screen.

reviews.Name: Frances Ha
reviews.Review:
It’s an autobiographical film that tries a bit too hard to be autobiographical.

The message is genuinely good, and I believe many people will relate to the characters or to the authenticity of being childlike while navigating adulthood. I felt connected to Frances throughout the entire movie, and everything she experienced was conveyed - just not in the most impactful way. I wish the film had leaned more into its dramatic moments.

It’s definitely not a bad film, which is why I’m giving it this rating, and it’s certainly not a waste of time. You follow a journey that could very well be your own - the beginning of adult life, full of highs and lows. Frances had plenty of lows, but the child within her managed to stay alive, genuine in every moment. Hold on to that moral; it’s worth having moments like these.

reviews.Name: Porco Rosso
reviews.Review:
"I'd rather be a pig than a fascist"

The best Miyazaki film I've seen so far, and without a doubt one of Studio Ghibli's finest. What a wonderful film to experience - not only for its animation and story, but for the entire philosophy behind the pig and the post–First World War setting.
What impressed me the most was how simply the main character is introduced: few words, no over-explaining, and yet an entire life and a quiet tragedy are immediately felt. The "curse" of his pig form is presented with such restraint that it becomes even more powerful - a symbol that speaks for itself without ever needing to be spelled out.
It's fun, genuinely funny, with BRILLIANT touches of classic romanticism in the main character, and it carries the best message I could ever ask for in a film: FASCISM NEVER AGAIN, or, in Portuguese, FASCISMO NUNCA MAIS.

reviews.Name: Black Bag
reviews.Review:
This film disappointed me far too much - in every possible way. For me, it was the exact opposite of what a thriller should be: it didn’t captivate me, it didn’t make me eager to reach the end, and it made me lose interest in the case incredibly fast. Above all, it delivered a final twist so underwhelming that I don’t think I’ve ever felt this kind of let-down in a film that CLAIMS TO BELONG to this genre. Incredibly predictable, desperately trying to hold on to suspense until the very last shot.
Lines like “See who picks up the gun” landed painfully badly, making me roll my eyes repeatedly while trying to focus on the film’s few positive aspects: incredible actors and an editing job that was… acceptable.

reviews.Name: A Charlie Brown Christmas
reviews.Review:
Charlie Brown: "I think there must be something wrong with me, Linus. Christmas is coming, but I'm not happy. I don't feel the way I'm supposed to feel."

This short film is truly beautiful. It’s been far too long since I last watched it - I only vaguely remembered a few scenes, and yet it feels special in every possible way. A Christmas tree can be the most beautiful one we've ever seen, but if it isn't made with care, attention, and affection, it ends up feeling almost empty.
The way Charlie is portrayed is striking, because of everything he represents: depression, fear of how others will react, and the belief that he's worth nothing… It's such an important message, especially for this time of year.

reviews.Name: Angst
reviews.Review:
Another film I never intend to watch again in my life. And it's all because this project is deeply disturbing… The only film that ever gave me this kind of feeling was "Irréversible (2002)", and I think that alone says a lot about the experience I had watching this.
What I really want to highlight is the way the story is told and the camerawork… The entire narrative is built through the psychopath's perspective, and the storyline is so chillingly uncomfortable that you genuinely hope everything ends as quickly as possible… The camera work only amplified every feeling I mentioned above: claustrophobia, the constant tracking of the character, even the rhythm of his breathing - all of it culminating in a first-person life story that forces you to confront just how horrifying a human being can be.
I won't lie… Part of me wishes that anyone reading this review would gain enough curiosity to try watching it, because believe me… you will never see anything like this in your life. But I also can’t convince you to do it, because everything I say about this film will sound bad and unsettling - AND THAT'S EXACTLY THE POINT.

reviews.Name: Sick of Myself
reviews.Review:
This kind of feeling - where the pleasure of watching a project blends with the discomfort of being fully aware of everything I just witnessed - has only happened with a few directors during my journey through the world of cinema: Robert Eggers with his fantastical and gothic worlds, Ari Aster with his incredibly satirical, fertile, and challenging imagination, and… Kristoffer Borgli, who keeps winning my heart as I explore more of his short and feature-length work.
A director who challenges the very way a moral should unfold within a story, someone unafraid of delivering the purest message in an eccentric, uncomfortable, yet entertaining, didactic, and provocative manner.

"Sick of Myself" brings an IMPORTANT message about the idealization of people on social media and the obsessive hunger for attention and existential jealousy. Modern narcissism, shattered identity, and a sexual tension that, beyond awkward, escalated into extreme levels of horror I never expected to see on screen.
An ending that demands the viewer to imagine their own closure, set within a narrative where identity is pushed so far into question that you lose track of what is real, what is imagined, and what is exaggerated.

reviews.Name: Softcore
reviews.Review:
A short film that shows just how ridiculous blind dates can be and how unbearably cringe outings become when the connection between two people doesn't spark in the very first moments.

Who in their right mind invites someone over to their place on a first date, without even knowing the other person's musical tastes? An incredibly ironic question - perfectly suited to describe a short film that is astonishingly detailed, with surprising, almost horror-like camerawork, sprinkled with romantic-comedic touches that made me laugh and feel scared at the same time.

reviews.Name: Whateverest
reviews.Review:
Even though this is a mockumentary, I genuinely need to talk about something that was present throughout those 15 minutes of film…
Love for music can appear in the strangest possible ways, but the moment it enters your life, it stays with you forever. Even if you do stupid things or stray from what's considered "normal", what truly matters is that the love remains - always there - whenever you put your headphones on or let a vinyl spin in a room filled with more memories than your own head.

That's exactly how I feel about music. I'm just like Todd: eccentric, constantly thinking about music, unable to get it out of my head. I've always imagined myself in that world, performing or doing something with my voice.

Being addicted to music is probably the best drug one can take, and I'm glad I'm on the addicts' side. I want to keep feeling it, living it, and breathing music until the end of my days.

reviews.Name: The Usual Suspects
reviews.Review:
I won't lie: for a moment I thought it was one character, but in the end it turned out to be another… It ended up being somewhat predictable and yet completely unpredictable at the same time, I honestly can't explain this properly without spoiling certain scenes.

The truth is, it entertained me from start to finish, and the actors put on an absolute show when it comes to presenting their characters. We understand every action, we understand their mindset, even without knowing their pasts - not only because the story is incredibly well structured to allow that, but also thanks to the extraordinary performances.

I think the film works perfectly as a whole, and I genuinely feel like I'll revisit it with great pleasure.

The initial interrogation scene with the suspects is actually a blooper, but it works incredibly well, it feels so natural, so genuine. The way Benicio del Toro handled the moment made it even more special. What an amazing scene.

reviews.Name: Eer
reviews.Review:
If I had an ear like that? I'd still keep listening to music on the beach, during that sunset, and if necessary, I'd even stand right next to that guy's wife. I'd stay glued to them just so they could witness the bloody collapse happening inside my eardrum.

It's a work that carries many of the psychological and moral elements of "Sick of Myself", and it already shows how much I liked what I saw. "Liked" is a strong word here, because the goal is to leave you completely uncomfortable while watching this. What's funniest is how you end up focusing on the strangest things in each scene, which makes everything feel even more immersive.

reviews.Name: Eddington
reviews.Review:
Watching it a second time made my rating change, and honestly, I wasn’t expecting that to happen at all. The final 40 minutes put any human being to the test - especially anyone aware of the sheer stupidity of what they’re witnessing. The fact that everything takes place in a single county, where the homeless man may be insane but the community can be even more so… it’s deeply satirical, and chilling to see a Sheriff portrayed this way. It’s almost unbelievable to experience this film from start to finish and take in the sheer number of extremes it represents.

Ari Aster, you already know everything I think about you and your films. But it never hurts to say it again: you’re one of my favorite directors, and that’s going to be very hard to change.

reviews.Name: Jay Kelly
reviews.Review:
An incredibly dry film, though I can’t deny the quality of the performances. The truth is, I only finished it because Clooney was the lead and because I have a soft spot for Adam Sandler, like everyone else.

A film with no real substance, extremely vague, built around a premise that feels somewhat randomly thrown together, and populated by characters it’s hard to connect with on an emotional level.

reviews.Name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 1
reviews.Review:
It’s a comfort film for me… Even though I didn’t have the time this year to do a full marathon, I didn’t want to let Christmas go by without watching at least one film.

I’m genuinely excited about the series. The people closest to me know that I have a few issues with the films and the direction of almost all of them (except Azkaban), mainly because they fail to deliver even half of what the books offer - and that really bothers me.

Still, it remains a tradition in my household, and I always watch it with great pleasure. This one in particular is among the best, even though it falls short with some characters. Seeing Hermione cast “Obliviate” on the Death Eaters without us ever getting inside her head hurts. Seeing Bathilda Bagshot’s story completely cut hurts even more. But… it’s Harry Potter. It defined my childhood. And revisiting it always comes with a sense of nostalgia that leaves my heart feeling warm.

reviews.Name: Ricky Gervais: Mortality
reviews.Review:
I don’t think this is his best material, and I actually think it’s far from it. This rating ends up being a comparison with all the specials I’ve watched over the past few months, even years: they’re all incredibly “safe” politically, and it gets a bit boring to hear jokes that never stray beyond the usual boundaries of what’s considered acceptable.

That’s exactly why I like this guy. He isn’t afraid to go straight for dark jokes, with that sharp British sarcasm that, even if it feels like a continuation of his older material, still makes me laugh uncontrollably.

I get tired of hearing that there are limits to humor, which is precisely why I enjoy when he pushes back against that idea, even if he does it repeatedly. Some people say it gets tiring… I also find it tiring when someone gets canceled over a JOKE that stepped outside the so-called “normal.” It reaches a point where, beyond being exhausted from trying to explain that it’s just a JOKE, I start feeling genuinely uncomfortable with modern society’s inability to take anything as a comedic moment or a form of satirical relief.

If it hits too close to home and people get sensitive? Get some perspective and laugh, because life is life, and we’re all going to die anyway.

reviews.Name: One Battle After Another
reviews.Review:
It was a great idea to rewatch this film on the last day of the year. If I already thought it was incredible the first time I saw it in theaters, now… I don’t know, it feels like it grew on me even more, and I wasn’t expecting that at all. I genuinely thought my rating might go down for reasons that, looking back, feel simply stupid to even consider.

To me, this is a project with absolutely no flaws. Everything fits perfectly: everything is well executed, well detailed, and so, SO WELL directed. Perfect from start to finish. Funny and unsettling at the same time.

Even knowing exactly what was going to happen, the final chase still managed to surprise me again in terms of stress, emotion, and anxiety - PEAK cinema.

reviews.Name: Isle of Dogs
reviews.Review:
Every reason is a good enough reason to be deported. (contains irony)

This man’s creativity fascinates me. It leaves me at a loss for words. This is clearly a project that delivers harsh criticism of society and the way politics are conducted, but what surprised me the most was the way he chose to express those ideas (many of them as axioms).

It’s not a perfect film, and I don’t think it’s his best, but at the same time I feel a strong need to talk about it. It’s an incredible film, both in terms of animation and storytelling. I’ve seen negative reviews complaining about the lack of “substance” in the story, and while watching the film I couldn’t help but notice where those people stand on the political spectrum. At the same time that they fail to understand the content, they throw stones at the characters as if they were to blame for the so-called “lack of substance.”

It’s absolutely worth watching - it has incredible historical and political elements, and a direction that left me speechless.

reviews.Name: Stranger Things 5: The Finale
reviews.Review:
I’ll be honest: this rating and this review don’t truly reflect what I think about the final season. Some serious mistakes were made, especially in the second volume.

The first part was solid, with a fourth episode that genuinely gave me chills, and I expected things to keep improving more and more. The truth is that from the fifth episode onward, I tried to keep my feet firmly on the ground because I could feel the quality starting to decline.

When I began watching the final episode, my expectations were incredibly low, and maybe that’s exactly why this rating ended up being so high.

Do I understand why the final battle, that climactic moment, lasted 15 minutes? No. But I do understand the desire to give each character a dignified ending and to show their future, even if only for a single scene. Do I agree with the decision not to kill anyone in order to avoid upsetting sensitivities? No, I don’t. As a screenwriter, I would have gone for an even bigger shock, but that’s just me. At the same time, I accept that choice: these characters have suffered enough over five seasons, and they deserved a happy ending, or at least a stable one.

I don’t know what happened before this final episode, and I also don’t know what went on behind the scenes - especially between the Duffer Brothers and Netflix, but I feel like things were cut that shouldn’t have been. Either that, or nothing more was filmed and this is simply all we were given. I prefer to believe the first option: that they had to cut material to fit everything into eight episodes. One of them being two hours long, which made me think we were about to get a heavy backstory episode, crammed with plot resolutions in such a limited time. They did it in one hour. ONE hour.

These four stars are partly because I started the final episode with very low expectations, and partly because this show was a huge part of my childhood. I was 13 or 14 when I first started watching it. I grew up with these characters… and just as they grew up, so did I, watching my own childhood slowly fade away. That’s why I loved the final scene: it was so well structured, so carefully planned, with clear references to the very first scene of the first episode, both in its direction and in its use of the camera.

Oh and btw: STEVE HARRINGTON MY GOAT, YOU SURVIVED.

reviews.Name: Nowhere
reviews.Review:
I’m too European for this shit.

I’m only giving it one and a half stars because of THAT scene - which is actually split into three or four parts… Because honestly, I still don’t know if I watched a film or a Linkin Park music video. A music video that wouldn’t even make it onto MTV for pornographic reasons.

I truly hope I’m not the only one with a negative opinion about this project. I couldn’t enjoy the direction, the story, the performances - nothing. It’s ridiculous to think that I could make a better film than this, but I genuinely believe it’s true. I don’t know if it was the editing, but the actors delivered what is probably the worst performance I’ve ever seen in a feature-length film.

reviews.Name: Uncut Gems
reviews.Review:
Is it strange to say that I had forgotten just how insane the final 40 minutes of this film are? Maybe. But at the same time, it feels natural, because I can’t remember any other film capable of making me feel this way - in such a dirty, relentless manner. Adam Sandler is so charismatic that you can’t help but root for his character until the very end, which makes everything more gripping and much harder to digest once it’s over.
Everything in this film is, in my view, executed extremely well, and the direction does almost all the magic - whether through the camerawork or the near-psychotic, stress-inducing editing. It’s a modern classic, and revisiting it only reinforces my stance on the film and further solidifies everything I have to say about this feature and about the Safdie brothers.

reviews.Name: Die My Love
reviews.Review:
It was difficult to settle on a rating for this film… On my way home, I couldn’t stop thinking about it, and that alone made me raise my score. This isn’t a perfect feature film, far from it, but there are moments that left me completely speechless.
I have to start with Jennifer Lawrence’s performance. Within the first twenty minutes, I realized this wasn’t an awards-bait performance aimed at the Golden Globes - this is Oscar-level work, and I wouldn’t be surprised at all if it ends up there. As for the rest of the cast, they do solid work; Pattinson remains incredibly reliable, delivering a performance that carries a chilling restraint in a situation that is ridiculously hard to navigate.
I have a lot of positive things to say about the cinematography, especially the photography. There’s one image in particular that left me stunned, part of a scene capable of provoking both tears and deep unease.
Some negatives: the editing feels a bit all over the place, and the screenplay falls short in a few scenes.

reviews.Name: Stand by Me
reviews.Review:
When the narrator started speaking, I genuinely thought it was going to break the film’s mood a bit. In fact, during the first half hour it bothered me slightly, because every scene with the kids was cut with him older, speaking directly to us. In the end, that’s my only truly relevant criticism of this project.
By the time I reached the end, I was quite emotional, not only because I realized this wasn’t a film about a dead boy, but about something much deeper, with an even more compelling meaning. The final line completely wrecked me, and the truth is that the hour and a half flew by. I was fully committed to the characters, felt connected to each and every one of them, and that was achieved in a brilliant way, especially considering we’re talking about a relatively short film when compared to other dramas.

reviews.Name: Two People Exchanging Saliva
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I think there’s something worth taking away from this short film, especially in how it portrays a dystopian world completely oppressed by human actions that are almost indispensable in our everyday lives.
It surprised me in a positive way, mainly because of the strong performances and the breathtaking cinematography. At the same time, I have to criticize a few things that weren’t explained in the film. Yes, we’re dealing with a deeply inhumane world, and sometimes we don’t even need to fully understand it to feel it, but this one needed more context. It needed more explanations, more nuances that would allow us to reach a clear conclusion in our own minds, and that didn’t quite happen for me.
That said, I still come away with a positive impression, and I’m curious to see what projects they might take on next.

reviews.Name: Butterfly
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I don’t think I’ve ever been this shocked by transitions in my life. The way moments of life flowed into scenes was so beautifully executed that I found myself staring in awe from start to finish. A life story told through an artistic lens, paying tribute to difficult times in sports.

The butterfly stroke is my favorite, and I’m sure that every time I swim, I’ll think of this film. At least for a while… Swimming makes me forget things, but it also brings back memories, moments that were meaningful in my life, or simply striking to my own perspective. This project firmly belongs to the latter.

reviews.Name: Forevergreen
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I don’t really know what to say about this short film… The bear was incredibly insensitive, and I felt a bit lost when it came to the message, what they were actually trying to communicate to us. The animation is well done, it almost feels like stop-motion, and I genuinely thought that was brilliant.

At the same time, the story fails in almost every way, at least from my perspective. I think they could have approached the subject differently and given far less screen time to the damn fries.

reviews.Name: Blue Moon
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I won’t lie… it was hard to make it through the entire film. I have a few suspicions as to why, but I’ll start with what was, for me, the best thing about this project: the casting. Watching Ethan Hawke perform is one of the best feelings anyone who truly loves cinema can experience. At the same time, Margaret Qualley is brilliant, and Andrew Scott remains incredibly solid.

That said, I do have some criticisms, especially regarding the cinematography. I think it’s easy to recognize the importance of a script like this, and the fact that it’s long didn’t bother me at all, quite the opposite. It managed to keep me engaged with the build-up (even if it was minimal) just enough to feel something in the final moments. However, the camera work and the settings could have been handled much better.

Even the script itself, which kept me invested for a good portion of the film, falters, especially at the beginning. The first 20 minutes felt dull and incredibly tedious, to the point where I almost turned it off to watch something else.

I’m considering 3.5 stars mainly because of Ethan Hawke. Not because of the experience itself, in that case, the rating would drop considerably, but because I got to watch an actor who deserves respect and recognition. Even if this isn’t the year for an Oscar, and I don’t think he’ll win, I still think the consideration matters, especially in a year filled with so many extraordinary performances.

reviews.Name: The Girl Who Cried Pearls
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I’ve never been very good at reviewing animated films, so I’ll try to keep this as concise as possible while still conveying everything I felt watching this: we’re talking about a short project that tells a beautiful story in such a captivating way that I honestly don’t remember blinking during those 16 minutes. I was enchanted by the animation and impressed by how much they managed to express in such a short amount of time. I genuinely feel honored to have watched something like this: the love poured into the project is visible from start to finish.

I wasn’t expecting an ending like this, and maybe I wouldn’t have chosen it myself, but it left me thinking, and that made the whole experience even more engaging, exceptional, and deeply touching.

reviews.Name: A Friend of Dorothy
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

The fact that it felt a bit generic and had such a short runtime made it hard for me to properly connect with the characters. I didn’t feel the film’s climax in the way the director probably intended.
The "Awww" moment was there at the end when I looked at the pages of the book, but it could have been far more emotional if the scenes had more depth. That said, at that point it would no longer be a short film, it would start turning into something bigger.
Still, I have to mention the quality of the actors, because they were incredibly solid.

reviews.Name: Retirement Plan
reviews.Review: 🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆 "I will be present. So aggressively present." Wow… We forget so easily that this is what we live for. A day filled with so much worry, whether at work or, in my case, at university, where stress and anxiety take center stage and end up driving all our decisions. And it all starts to feel a bit pointless… pointless in the sense that one problem can easily turn out to be the least of our problems, if that makes sense. We’re here to live, not to suffer. I have a similar list, but with even more items on it. I just hope I’ll have the time to do everything I’ve imagined doing, seeing, or experiencing, whether during retirement or in the limited vacations I’ll have in my professional future. This animated short film made me feel something I’ve never felt with films from this genre before, which is why I gave it this rating. How can seven minutes be so introspective? How can such a simple list make me feel "saudade" for things I haven’t even done yet?

reviews.Name: Elio
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I don’t think this is Pixar’s project with the most potential. Everything I saw felt a bit generic to me, built on familiar premises, even if set within a universe separate from everything that has already been done in animated films. At the same time, it didn’t feel like a waste of time at all, quite the opposite. I managed to find positive and genuinely interesting elements that could have been expanded or better explained.
It’s a film that tackles sensitive topics and resolves them in a very solid way, whether through well-structured dialogue or beautiful imagery. A solid project overall, with a fun, different, imaginative animation that isn’t afraid to show itself. But I still felt like something was missing… something that would allow me to say "sensational",  something that would make me cry in the moments where I was supposed to. Maybe I just didn’t fully connect with the characters for that to happen. And saying this might make it sound like I felt nothing, but trust me, I was completely engaged from start to finish.

reviews.Name: It Was Just an Accident
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆 

I have to say I wasn’t expecting this film to even make it into the Oscar nominations. At the same time, the moment I saw it listed in the International Feature category, I thought it might show up somewhere else as well, even before having seen it. As soon as I noticed it in Best Original Screenplay, I immediately felt I had to watch it as soon as possible. And I did. I don’t regret it at all.
A completely ironic, sarcastic title that carries an overwhelming weight once the story unfolds and the reality lived by these characters is deconstructed. It’s impressive how the film managed to keep me hooked from beginning to end, constantly thinking about a possible resolution to everything, and then… completely speechless. I did not expect a final act like this, especially given such a heavy subject, so historically grounded and painfully plausible.
Brilliant performances, an incredibly detailed and well-crafted final confession, and a message that needs to be carried forward: we must never forget these authoritarian regimes, where everything seems to be… just… an accident.

reviews.Name: The Voice of Hind Rajab
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

“Do you really think the voice of a terrified little girl will spark their empathy?”

I can’t write a review.
The film speaks for itself.

Free Palestine.

reviews.Name: Zootopia 2
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

Was I the only one who wasn’t shocked by the plot twist? I found it incredibly predictable. I even said right at the beginning that the two of them were going to split in the middle of the film. Well… I was absolutely right.

Even though I found the story quite predictable, I have to look at the audience this film had and will continue to have. And yes, it’s a great movie for kids. It’s a project that talks about abuse of power and minorities in an incredibly elegant, fun, and educational way.

Not to mention the animation, which we already knew from the first film was spectacular, but I still have to highlight it again because of the quality of the universe that was created and the very well-designed action sequences.

reviews.Name: Little Amélie or the Character of Rain
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I’m a bit hesitant about the rating I’ll give this film, because the truth is it left me with more questions than answers. At the same time, I feel like that was entirely intentional, since we experience the whole project through Amélie’s perspective: a child trying to deal with far too many emotions at once. She even has to face an identity crisis… at three years old? I had to deal with that at 21/22, I can’t even imagine what that feels like for a child.

I waited a few minutes before settling on a final verdict for my rating, and now, with a clearer head, I can talk about everything I saw and felt during the film. Beyond the stunning animation, with eccentric touches in the way nature is portrayed (much like how a child might experience it), I was also struck by the maturity of the screenplay, the way the story unfolds, and a moral that left me almost emotionally wrecked by how beautiful it is.

I still have two films left to watch in the animation category, but it’s hard to imagine either of them convincing me that this isn’t the best of the five. Everything is incredibly well done, the love for the story is palpable, and the animation itself is… I honestly run out of words trying to describe just how beautiful these scenes are.

reviews.Name: KPop Demon Hunters
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

The conversations between Rumi and Jinu felt like they were pulled straight out of High School Musical, and I’m really not into that. 

Some scenes were incredibly cringe, but the truth is the animation and the story are genuinely impressive. Even though it’s not my favorite music style, I have to highlight the fact that I started the film feeling very skeptical and finished it with several positive takeaways. Beyond the animation and the story, the voice performances themselves are very strong and do justice to the genre throughout the entire project.

Do I think it’s the best film in the category? Hell no - I still believe Little Amélie is better in every possible way. Do I think it’s going to win? Maybe, especially for Original Song… I’m actually more confident about the music category than animation itself. “Golden” was a huge moment in 2025, and as much as I’d love to see “I Lied To You” from Sinners win, I find it very hard to believe that the award won’t go to the single in this long feature.

reviews.Name: Butcher's Stain
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

At the same time that I understand the story and feel that the atmosphere is very well portrayed, I was confused by the storyline involving the son: it felt like it didn’t really add anything to the film. If they had explored the relationship between him and the custody of his child a bit more, it might have given the screenplay more weight, especially considering how sensitive the subject matter is.

The cinematography is decent, nothing particularly out of the ordinary, it does what it’s supposed to do. The story itself, especially the environment among coworkers, social media, and the way misinformation spreads, is well presented overall.

reviews.Name: Jane Austen's Period Drama
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

We’re literally talking about a period drama. Did I enjoy the comedy? I found it entertaining, at least, but I didn’t burst out laughing the way I probably was supposed to. I found the character names funnier than anything else, but I’m not sure that qualifies as “the joke of the century.”

Those 13 minutes felt like a sketch straight out of SNL, and I guarantee that’s not a criticism of the project, far from it. I just think that if the goal was to make a sketch, they should’ve pushed the ridicule of the characters even further, especially Dickley. I can easily imagine Dickley fainting the moment he hears the meaning of “menstruation,” only for him to be the one needing a doctor by the end. Or even more ridiculous: Dickley cancels the wedding, marries someone else, and then discovers menstruation the hard way… that would be PEAK cinema.

I think I’ve realized that the short film category isn’t exactly stacked this year. I’m still missing one film, “The Singers,” and from what I’ve heard, it’s the best of the five. So far, my pick is a short I gave 3.5 stars to, and that says a lot. I’m really hoping "The Singers" blows me away.

reviews.Name: The Three Sisters
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I really loved what I saw. A simple animation, with spot-on comedic touches, ridiculously well-structured characters, and an extremely fun ending.

I’ve been thinking about my ratings for the animated shorts and I honestly don’t even know how to rank them… First place goes to “Retirement Plan”, and fifth place is definitely “Forevergreen.” From there on, I genuinely have no idea how to order the rest: they were all incredibly fun to watch, each one with strong animation in its own way.

We’ll see, but this was undoubtedly a great year for this category. From a retirement plan to three sisters enchanted by a sailor… incredible.

reviews.Name: The Smashing Machine
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

Also in preparation for Marty Supreme. These guys never fail to surprise me. The Safdie brothers manage to make films with premises that aren’t really my thing, yet still completely hook me from start to finish the moment I hit the spacebar on my keyboard. This one is no exception.

And if I’ve seen people criticizing the film’s quality, I’m here to push back against that and say this: if there’s cinematography I absolutely LOVE, it’s the kind this film delivers. If there’s editing that gets me completely addicted, barely blinking, it’s this. Benny, my friend, you genuinely surprised me. I thought I was about to watch something fairly standard, but instead I ended up seeing a project with an image and a use of camera that left me speechless, especially for a film like this, where it makes perfect sense.

Great performances all around, fully had me engaged with the script from the very beginning. Emily Blunt is at her best here, genuinely chilling, and this might honestly be Dwayne Johnson’s strongest role to date.

No, it’s not a perfect film. I also don’t plan on revisiting it anytime soon, and that’s probably why I’m settling on 4 stars. But one thing I’ll keep doing is defending the Safdie brothers, because they’ve already proven they can make incredible films with or without each other. And when I say I won’t be rewatching it soon, that has more to do with the story than anything else - simply put, I’m not really a fan of wrestling, combat sports, or anything that revolves around people beating each other up.

reviews.Name: Song Sung Blue
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I’ll start by saying this right away… Jennifer Lawrence was absolutely robbed. Sorry, but everything I saw from Kate Hudson felt way too generic to even be considered Oscar-worthy.

This is pure Oscar bait, and I think that already says a lot about what this project is. Is it bad? Overall, no, it has some solid musical moments. But the screenplay seriously struggles to keep you engaged until the very end. The final scene didn’t hit as hard for me, precisely because I never fully connected with the characters as a whole, and that’s entirely on the writing.

The cinematography is decent, the editing is serviceable, and the atmosphere does a good job of immersing you in the period. It was tolerable, but I can easily imagine this kind of film working much better with a shorter runtime and stronger, sharper dialogue.

reviews.Name: Marty Supreme
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

More than 60 minutes have passed since I finished watching this film. I got home and I’m now listening to the sound of rain falling while trying to calm myself down, if that’s even possible. Hardly.

I think everyone close to me knows that the Safdie Brothers have been climbing higher and higher in my personal ranking. 2025/2026 was no exception. Did I have extremely high expectations and was I scared because of that? Absolutely. My heart was racing and the film hadn’t even started yet. The moment I saw that very Safdie-esque transition at the beginning, I immediately realized the importance and the influence this project could have on the future of cinema - whether in editing, performances, cinematography, or even marketing campaigns. The ability Josh and the rest of the team (from producers to marketing directors) had to bring so many people into theaters to watch a film where the main character is obsessed with playing table tennis… I honestly have no words.

Give Chalamet the Oscar. What an actor, what a beast. He’s reached a level where he can completely erase every role he’s done or is currently doing from your memory and introduce a brand-new embodiment: one capable of putting us through stress, anxiety, and a complete inability to distinguish reality from fiction.

Once again, we find ourselves rooting for a narcissistic character who only cares about himself… where have I seen this before?

A modern classic. And my pick for Film of the Year 2025. I still haven’t seen Hamnet or Sentimental Value, but I honestly find it impossible for this opinion to change.

reviews.Name: Sirāt
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

It’s an extremely uncomfortable film from start to finish, and that purpose is perfectly fulfilled. When it ended, my first thought was: “How did this even end up with two Oscar nominations?” - not because of the film’s quality, but because of the Academy’s nomination history.

This is the kind of project that’s incredibly hard to please everyone, and for that alone it deserves my respect. Its boldness is, in my view, its biggest strength. At the same time, I feel like I’m part of the group of people who didn’t see much beyond the outstanding sound design and cinematography. A lot of important narrative content was missing for the story to properly unfold, and the weakest point is definitely the screenplay itself. Even the ending wasn’t at all what I expected or wanted for something like this.

Overall, a solid film, with phenomenal cinematography and sound editing, but one that falls short when it comes to storytelling.

reviews.Name: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

Rose Byrne easily deserves the Best Actress nomination. This is one of those films where the actress has to be exceptional, otherwise the entire essence and interest of the project collapses. And I honestly can’t find enough adjectives to describe her performance, because truly? It’s chilling, a bad trip from start to finish.

This is where the direction and cinematography come in: extremely bold. Bronstein brings forward ideas that are incredibly hard to digest, and that’s precisely what makes the film so engaging and so claustrophobic. This is not a film for everyone, so for those who struggle with claustrophobia, motherhood-related fears, or are going through some kind of mental breakdown - DO NOT WATCH IT.

I haven’t lived through what the character goes through, but I’ve had moments where stress, anxiety, and the fear of owning my identity made me feel genuinely unwell. This film is a representation of that (just like the hole in the apartment ceiling, anyone who saw that as a plot hole clearly hasn’t experienced the same thing).

It’s not a perfect film, but it’s yet another reminder that a good film doesn’t need to end well or badly, doesn’t need to make you feel safe or happy. You can have a powerful viewing experience while sitting with the worst feelings a mother can possibly face.

Oh and by the way: my mom is a superhero.

reviews.Name: Sentimental Value
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

Sorry, Brazil, but I have my pick for Best International Feature. And there’s honestly no debate.

I was rereading my Marty Supreme review and it actually made me laugh - at a moment when I truly don’t know what to say about this film, a moment where the tears feel bigger than some lakes in Portugal. I laughed because in that review I said it would be hard for any film to surpass Marty Supreme for me. Turns out… I just hadn’t seen this yet.

It’s incredibly difficult to explain what I felt, and what I’m still feeling. The truth is that as I write this review, I’m constantly sniffling and pretending the tears aren’t falling. This film touched me in ways I never thought I could experience. There were moments where I was crying so much I couldn’t even read the subtitles.

It’s a slow project, and I understand the criticism regarding the length of some scenes, but those were precisely the moments where I completely broke down - whether because of their emotional weight or simply because of the actors’ performances. Rose, Stellan, Inga, and Elle made even stone cry. I keep thinking about every scene, every moment where they step in and completely dominate the space.

I think it’s clear that I could spend hours talking about this film, but the truth is that I’m psychologically exhausted and can’t write much more. So I’ll leave it with just one final moment, one that will stay with me forever. And when I say this, I mean it sincerely, I’m not trying to hyperbolize what I felt. The final scene between Nora and Agnes pierced my soul and made me relive moments where I didn’t feel at peace with myself, moments I had to fight through, and ones I’m proud to have overcome. That scene was special to me in so many ways, and I’m deeply grateful to have experienced something like this.

reviews.Name: Sentimental Value
reviews.Review:
Over the last two days, I haven’t been able to stop thinking about this film, and I don’t think it’s leaving my head anytime soon.
A second watch made the opening scenes feel even heavier, even more meaningful.

Special in every sense, forever burned into my memory.

reviews.Name: Hamnet
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

This review may be influenced by another film that is simply perfect, which I also watched twice this week. Consider this a disclaimer.

This is an incredibly well-crafted project, and from the middle of the film onward I started crying and didn’t stop until the final minutes. It left me thinking about what might possibly be the greatest theatrical play ever made in the history of humanity.

My Oscar prediction for Best Actress goes to Jessie Buckley, I find it very hard to imagine it going to anyone else. I do have my personal favorite, my pick remains unchanged (one love, Renate Reinsve), but I wouldn’t be upset at all if it went to Buckley. Quite the opposite, she absolutely deserves it too.

As for Mescal: I felt he was a bit lost at times, especially in the early scenes, but he found an incredible rhythm and ended up being part of my favorite scene in the entire film, right near the end.

I don’t think this is a perfect film, that should be clear by now. There are a few things I would easily change, mainly in the opening section, where we’re introduced to their relationship, or even when we first meet the children.

IMPORTANT NOTE: Another thing that slightly affected how seriously I took some scenes: since when does a baby get born without an umbilical cord attached? Since when is there no blood? And since when does the baby come out that quickly?

reviews.Name: The Singers
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

I wasn’t expecting this to warm my heart the way it did, and I’m really glad I went in knowing absolutely nothing about the project.

I think I’ve found my favorite in the Live Action Short Film category. Everything I saw reflects my addiction, my caffeine, what makes me wake up with a smile on my face, what makes me cry for hours on end, what makes me feel every word and every note. It reflects my love for music.

Because any performer, any song, can reach our soul in different ways, yet move us all the same - a unique moment shared between people who listen and experience what they’re hearing together.

What I saw reflects a love for art: an addictive love, sometimes toxic, but ultimately beautiful, precious, something we simply cannot live without.

Very solid cinematography, characters we know nothing about and yet somehow understand, a blend of emotions carried through the music and the stories behind it, and an incredibly well-crafted edit.

reviews.Name: The Worst Person in the World
reviews.Review:
Never has a director made me feel so completely desolate and emotionally shaken. I’ve been thinking for a long time about what I’ve just watched, and the truth is: I don’t even know where to begin.

This is yet another film that challenges genres and tries to explore the complexity of the human mind in the rawest way possible. I know many people don’t like that approach, and that’s a fair opinion, but I’m definitely not part of that group. Quite the opposite.

I feel that if I start going too deep into this project, everything might collapse inside me psychologically, and I already feel fragile. So I’ll try to stay afloat and talk about it in broader terms. Once again, I connected very easily with the main character, perhaps because of everything she goes through across those two intense hours. It’s a clear portrayal of the disappearance of one’s own identity, where everything feels either missing or unbearably dull, even the act of simply being.

It’s a film that pushes the boundaries of what is considered “normal” for a woman in her 30s, depicting a crisis that fits perfectly into the kind of anxiety and stress so many of us young people experience day after day.

Renate Reinsve continues to rise higher and higher in my estimation. She is a phenomenal actress, chilling at times, and she gives even more weight to everything we see simply by being on screen.

I’ll end by saying that I’m absolutely loving discovering Joachim Trier’s filmography, and I’m increasingly committed to exploring all of his work.

reviews.Name: Angel's Egg
reviews.Review:
The only truly obvious element while watching this film is its connection to Christianity. Don’t take that as a criticism, it merely serves as context for anyone looking to experience something incredibly abstract, with a message that can only be interpreted by those who watch it. Different perspectives inevitably lead to different moral conclusions.

It’s difficult to assign a rating to this project, simply because it fits into something I’ve never experienced before. I’ve never come across an animated film this surreal and abstract.

There are several ideas I took away from the story. The reinterpretation of Noah’s Ark, turned inside out, reflecting an inability to restore faith, arguably the film’s core theme. The bird as a symbol of freedom of thought and action, the only animal capable of surviving such an atrocity, such condemnation of humankind. A God who forgets humanity, as if people were pawns left behind in a cardboard box or abandoned among the wreckage of a wooden ship. A cross present throughout the entire project, and the figure of Jesus Christ defined ambiguously as either savior or sinner.

I could continue listing interpretations and messages, but the truth is I’d be writing for countless minutes, and I’m sure no one has the patience to read all of that.

So I’ll leave you with my rating, and with the suggestion that experiencing something like this is genuinely worthwhile - especially coming from someone who is an atheist.

reviews.Name: Oslo, August 31st
reviews.Review:
“I just want you to understand. If that’s how it ends, it’s a choice I’ve made.”

I swear, this man leaves me completely devastated, speechless, thinking about his films for hours on end. It’s incredible how raw a film can be, how powerful it can become by being purely human, simple, and truthful. I usually gravitate toward something more dramatic: more adrenaline, more horror, more psychedelia. But Joachim has brought me calmness and depth in storytelling, and discovering this side of cinema is making me completely captivated by Scandinavian filmmaking.

Beyond the raw and deeply human stories (so human they can feel almost desolating), what has truly held me hostage are the lead actors who speak to us through their eyes. This project had a lot of dialogue, especially at the beginning, giving us detailed context about Anders’ situation. But halfway through the film, he was communicating with us without saying a word.

I noticed Anders’ brilliance in “The Worst Person in the World (2021)”, even in a supporting role, but seeing him carry a film as the protagonist only confirmed what I already felt back then: extraordinary, from beginning to end. Whether in the way he delivers his lines or in the way his gaze drifts toward places unknown to us, yet makes us uneasy, as if we’re desperate to understand what his character is thinking.

Joachim is rising higher and higher in my personal ranking of directors. And soon enough, he’ll be among my very favorites: fully deserved.

reviews.Name: A Place We Call Reality
reviews.Review:
There’s one truth behind every Borgli project: he’s weird, and I’m absolutely here for it.

At the same time, I can’t help but feel that this short doesn’t quite do justice to his beautifully twisted mind. We’re talking about a complete satire of reality and imagination - about how the two coexist simultaneously, about the toxicity of a society trapped in its own version of the Matrix. It attempts to expand on themes like narcissism, emotional fragility, fear of acceptance, and an obsessive critique of cultural norms.

The problem? It feels like too much for something this short.

I genuinely loved the concept, the themes, and the completely unhinged yet disturbingly hilarious approach. But it ends up feeling rushed. The soundtrack is overbearing at times, and the film doesn’t quite manage to bring its ideas together into a cohesive moral statement. Even if the intention was to leave everything open-ended, I’m not entirely convinced that was the strongest choice.

Kristoffer Borgli, remember the name. I’m incredibly curious to see where his cinema goes next. Very excited to see "The Drama (2026)".

reviews.Name: The Devil All the Time
reviews.Review:
“Some people were born just so they could be buried.”

I first watched this film with my mother a few years ago - if I’m not mistaken, it was the year it was released. Back then, I didn’t feel completely captivated by it. The truth is, I hadn’t fully understood its message, nor had I managed to connect with the characters the way I probably should have.

Even so, I felt it deserved a second chance. The story was far too interesting, the cinematography incredibly well executed, and the performances on another level. I’m very glad I gave it that second viewing.

It grew on me immensely, mainly because I felt much more connected to the story this time. The actors deliver extraordinary performances in roles that are incredibly difficult to digest, portraying characters with a deeply psychotic and emotionally unstable edge.

It’s a slow film, very slow. It tries to fully introduce every character, and I feel that’s where it stumbles slightly. We’re dealing with many people, many family and emotional connections, which become difficult to keep track of within a two-hour runtime.
At the same time, this second experience resonated with me much more. It’s a story that ultimately ties everything together in a powerful way, leading to an almost breathless ending, one that feels only partially rewarding, but intentionally so. And that, in itself, felt brilliant.

reviews.Name: The Royal Tenenbaums
reviews.Review:
