I was a bit disappointed. I wasn’t expecting to encounter characters in a Wes Anderson film that I simply couldn’t connect with emotionally, but unfortunately, that’s what happened.

The cinematography is unmistakably his, and I love it. The editing is also very well executed. Still, I couldn’t fully engage with the story itself, even though the actors delivered extraordinary performances.

I went in expecting to have a lot of fun, yet I didn’t laugh once,  which is unusual for a Wes Anderson film, at least compared to my previous experiences with his work.

I feel like I might be being a little harsh on the film as a whole, so let me emphasize this again: Wes Anderson has a remarkable ability to immerse us in a story and keep us engaged all the way to the end. That alone deserves praise, especially considering I didn’t connect with the narrative as much as I had hoped.

reviews.Name: Thelma
reviews.Review:
I went into this film without reading the synopsis. As I’ve mentioned in another review, I’ve been trying to explore more of Trier’s work, and that’s exactly what I’ve been doing. Watching this project made the differences between it and the three previous films I’ve seen from him very clear - and no, that’s not a negative. There’s a lot to say about this one.

It’s impressive. He continues to surprise me, whether through overwhelming melancholy or by venturing into something closer to psychological thriller territory, everything feels perfectly placed.

This is one of the most symbolic and emotionally devastating messages I’ve encountered within this kind of genre. As always, I try to avoid spoilers in my reviews, so don’t assume the story is generic, it’s anything but that. It’s incredibly complex to describe, yet it held me from beginning to end. Not only because of the thriller elements, but also because of the way he introduces and develops the protagonist within the religious constraints of her family.

The way he succinctly portrays the struggles of deeply conservative families, especially when imposed on young people with promising futures but painful memories, felt nothing short of brilliant.

There are stunning scenes, meticulously haunting moments that will stay with me for a long time. The seizure scene in the swimming pool left me psychologically shaken, and the moment I fully understood what the story was building toward made me cry uncontrollably. Not to mention the final scene, executed in such a powerful way that it leaves you questioning what truly happened.

Joachim Trier, now I can finally say this with certainty: you’ve entered my list of favorite directors.

reviews.Name: Autumn Sonata
reviews.Review:
I knew this was going to be a heavy film, but I wasn’t expecting it to be this dense in terms of dialogue.

After my journey through Joachim Trier’s filmography, I started asking myself, “Should I explore more Scandinavian cinema?” I found my answer when I saw someone comparing Trier’s cinematography to Bergman’s.

I’m not here to compare the two directors - only to say that I’ll definitely continue exploring this kind of cinema, because it leaves me deeply interested and emotionally shaken.

The story in this film is so compelling that I found myself wanting to know more about the characters’ lives, even though the dialogue already provides extensive insight into Eva’s, Helena’s, and of course Charlotte’s childhood and emotional past.

This is another film that’s difficult to digest. We’re talking about a feature built on piercing dialogue, mesmerizing performances, and cinematography that works beautifully in harmony with the story itself. I found it brilliant that the film is set during autumn - the metaphor feels intentional. The emotional “harvest” Eva carries within her reaches a breaking point, as if she can no longer bear the weight of what has grown inside her.

An introduction to Ingmar Bergman, and I’m eager to discover more.

reviews.Name: The Humans
reviews.Review:
Throughout the film, I kept telling myself that something was missing, something that would truly anchor me to the story, and especially to the characters. Unfortunately, that assumption never evolved into anything else, as I couldn’t connect with the dialogue in the way I was supposed to.

"The Humans (2021)" attempts to inject touches of horror into what already feels like a culmination of catastrophes no one would ever wish to experience in real life. That might have been the film’s strongest aspect, particularly because it chooses the right moments to make us grip our seats. Beyond that, however, I felt very little connection to the characters.

At times, the script felt slightly disjointed, which caused me to lose interest in certain conversations or revelations. I’m aware that I likely missed important character details precisely because I struggled to stay engaged with the dialogue. Many of those moments felt poorly timed to me, especially when exposition was delivered while the camera was focused on exploring the house, for instance.

Strong editing, bold camera work, and solid performances. But ultimately, a screenplay that didn’t quite reach the level I was expecting.

reviews.Name: Hamnet
reviews.Review:
I had to honor a deal I made with my mother and so… I went back to watch this film. 

Such a short time later, but it had to be done… I’m not lying when I say that when I walked out of the cinema the first time, I felt like cancelling that agreement - exactly because I had suffered too much with it, just like all of us. 
Going back to a heavy film after what, 2/3 weeks? I did that with Sentimental Value and it wasn’t even weeks, I watched it twice within the space of two days.

There were things that grew on me, especially Paul Mescal’s overall performance: the opening scenes didn’t fully win me over, but from the moment William returns to London after the trauma, Paul’s acting rose greatly in my consideration.

I don’t have much more to say, the rest is in the first review I wrote about the film :)

reviews.Name: The Backrooms (Found Footage)
reviews.Review:
I became incredibly curious after watching the trailer and noticing that one of my favorite actresses, Renate Reinsve, is part of the film’s cast. Only now did I realize that he had made this - a short horror film that already hints at a connection with the upcoming project from A24.

It’s suffocating to watch. At times I almost didn’t want to look at the screen, and achieving that feeling in such a short runtime already shows signs of the potential quality his future projects might have. A large set that somehow still feels claustrophobic, with subtle glimpses of the monster that make everything even more immersive, and even more terrifying.

reviews.Name: Real Life Exp.
reviews.Review:
I’m not sure what to say about this short… I’m a bit lost when it comes to the message Borgli was trying to convey, and that’s why my rating doesn’t go beyond 2.5 stars. It doesn’t make good use of the camera, nor does it make the characters truly interesting. I know that ten minutes isn’t a lot of time, but it could still have offered some background to help us connect with them.

Instead, we just watch two girls dying of thirst, with no real concern about leaving that place. That’s it.

reviews.Name: It's Such a Beautiful Day
reviews.Review:
Well… I genuinely thought I was going to like this project more, but the truth is that everything felt so confusing and so disruptive that I never managed to fully connect with the character. It has beautiful moments and the humor is definitely on point, but I felt like it needed more context and fewer distorted episodes of reality.

At the same time, I couldn’t really connect with the animation style either. The combination of drawings with real-life elements made me lose track of what I was actually watching at times.

It’s not my intention to give this project a bad rating, because it genuinely has some incredible touches… maybe it deserves a second chance - although I doubt I’ll return to it anytime soon.

reviews.Name: Sentimental Value
reviews.Review:
They called me a masochist for watching this film in the cinema twice within a week… they’ll probably call me the same again for having watched it a third time in such a short period of time, and honestly? I don’t care.

It continues, and will continue, to be impossible to describe the importance this project has in my life. It may sound strange, but the truth is that I will never forget the first time I watched this, alone, on the big screen. I’ll never forget the fear I felt when I walked into the theater with extremely high expectations, without even having watched a trailer or knowing what the film was about (on purpose, because of the impact and surprise it had on audiences).

What’s even more interesting is catching details that I missed the first times, like the monologue delivered by Elle Fanning and Stellan’s gaze, which says more than a thousand-page script ever could.

As I said in my previous reviews, it’s simply a perfect film, kept in a special corner of my heart for everything it represents, for everything it tells through dialogue and through distant, silent looks. A project that marked me for life.

“It's like her sadness is such an overwhelming part of her.”

reviews.Name: Arco
reviews.Review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

Okay, I'll admit it right away: we’re talking about the best-looking animation in the category. Before watching this project, I had read an article highlighting the fact that it was hand-drawn, and when I finally saw it I was left speechless.

A very strong animated film in every sense, even if the story, in my opinion, wasn’t explored as deeply as I would have liked. I was completely blown away by some scenes and environments, incredibly well crafted and filled with very meaningful messages. A journey full of bumps, with well-timed touches of humor (especially through the characters Frankie, Dougie, and Stewie) and a very consistent final message.

I don’t consider it the best in the category, and I honestly thought it would be. At the same time, I don’t want to take away from its achievements, so it will easily land in second place in my ranking for Best Animated Feature at the Academy Awards:

1 – Little Amélie or the Character of Rain
2 – Arco
3 – Zootopia
4 – KPop Demon Hunters
5 – Elio

reviews.Name: Project Hail Mary
reviews.Review: PLEASE DON'T COMPARE THIS TO INTERSTELLAR. IT'S SIMPLY NOT COMPARABLE. After my humble and friendly request, I can move on to my thoughts about the project. I want to make it clear that I haven’t read the book yet (but I promise I will), so I’m speaking purely from what I saw on screen: funny, entertaining the whole time, charismatic, everything a sci-fi film should have in its personality. The cinematography is outstanding, with breathtaking images, extremely intelligent camera work, editing that kept me glued to my seat, and a soundtrack that was incredibly well chosen. The truth is that the two and a half hours flew by and it ended up being one of the best surprises I’ve had in a movie theater. Ryan Gosling delivers a captivating performance, supported by a very well-written story and dialogues that balance humor with dramatic touches. The way the film plays with its emotional beats is very well executed. I don’t want to go into details because I’d rather avoid spoilers, so I’ll just leave a recommendation: watch it in the cinema, big screen, Dolby Atmos, comfortable seats, popcorn from the supermarket (the cinema bar is ridiculously expensive, be smart), and a smile on your face.

reviews.Name: Dune
reviews.Review:
Preparing for the absolute PEAK.

I’m so excited, the best trilogy of all time? Debatable, but no one can deny the spectacular nature of these films. The landmark they represent.

Perfect.

reviews.Name: Ad Astra
reviews.Review:
Is this a film about space? Or is it... not?

It’s hard to digest a film like this, but I think it’s even harder to read some of the reviews I came across when I opened Letterboxd to write mine.

Yes, it’s a slow film. Yes, the character is very complex and almost feels “dead” at times, in the sense that we struggle to understand his emotions throughout the film… But… isn’t that the point? This is a film about depression told through the stars, and there’s nothing more poetic than that.

It’s unsettling to realize that Roy had everything he needed to live a fulfilling life without constantly looking up. Even more unsettling is understanding that his life could only move forward if he put an end to his story with his father.

We’re talking about a project that isn’t afraid to confront you with one of the most cruel states of mind imaginable: depression. A film that deconstructs its main character to such an extent that, by the end, you no longer care about the fate of humanity or its survival: you only care about his psychological state. About Roy.

Because this is a sci-fi film that isn’t just about space. It’s about dreams, failure, family, sadness, and the hope of finding something that gives us… life.

Don’t try to find something up there if you don’t first have everything grounded down here.

reviews.Name: Project Hail Mary
reviews.Review:
I hope people start to realize just how important this film is for the future of sci-fi.

What impressed me the most on this second trip to the cinema was the camera work, especially in the transitions between scenes, combined with the editing, where the camera follows movement so fluidly. Brilliant in every sense of the word.

We all know The Academy doesn’t usually favor sci-fi films, but no one is going to forget the sheer brilliance and the breath of fresh air that this film brought to the genre and to cinema itself.

Amaze amaze amaze.

reviews.Name: Toback Vs. Mailer: The Incident
reviews.Review:
Why did I watch this?

Picture this: I’m tired, I’ve spent the whole day studying and working on projects, it’s already late, and I’m not about to start a full-length film now… So I decided to stick with something short. I wanted to watch "Two Ships (2025)", but I couldn’t find it anywhere, so I went digging through the filmography of directors I love, and ended up finding this Safdie short.

Are there positives? Sure. You can very easily tell that James Toback and Norman Mailer are complete pieces of shit (trust me, it’s good to know that). At the same time, the way the café scenes blend with the animated segments was brilliant, and the animation itself is genuinely fun, well made, with a style that feels very different from anything I’ve seen before.

It killed some time, and honestly, I’m glad I didn’t end up on TikTok just to fall asleep, because at least I watched something… educational:

GAMBLING GAMBLING GAMBLING.
ALCOHOL ALCOHOL ALCOHOL.

reviews.Name: Soul
reviews.Review:
“You can't crush a soul here, that's what life on Earth is for.” - 22

Sometimes we need to reconnect with our inner peace, especially in moments when stress, anxiety, and discouragement show up uninvited. I found this film during one of those moments, and I promised myself I would come back to it whenever I felt “lost” in things I still had to do: deadlines, responsibilities, times when I spend more time stuck in my room than outside, actually living life.

Don’t get me wrong, I love studying. I love working on projects that challenge me, that push me to my limits. But I reach a point where stopping feels like pressing a button covered in dust somewhere deep in my brain.

I used to be a lot like Joe… focused exclusively on what I could achieve academically and professionally. I forgot there was more to life than that.

This is a very special film to me. It reminds me of where I came from, what I’ve been through, and who I am now.

A project that… makes me stop. Think. Feel nostalgic for what I’ve lived and plan everything I still want to discover and experience.

Oh and by the way... Music is a big part of me, and without it... I would probably be one of those damned souls.

reviews.Name: Project Hail Mary
reviews.Review:
“You’ve reviewed this film twice” - make it three, Letterboxd.

Good, good, good
Great, great, great
Amaze, amaze, amaze.

reviews.Name: The Squid and the Whale
reviews.Review:
FINALLY A BREAK FROM UNI TO WATCH FILMS.

As soon as the film ended and I saw the credits, I thought, “of course this was produced by Wes Anderson.”

To be completely honest, it’s been hard for me to find something in Noah Baumbach’s work that truly delivers memorable moments. That might sound a bit harsh considering what I’ve experienced so far, but I feel like his dramas are missing something. Even with extraordinary actors, not everything can be saved, and some moments were genuinely dull.

I don’t think this justifies a runtime of an hour and twenty, something closer to 50 minutes would’ve been enough to convey everything, and might have even improved my opinion of the project.

Yes, it’s based on real events from his life, and I understand that it can be difficult to build a gripping script from that kind of premise. At the same time, I feel like the relationship between the children and the mother could have been explored more deeply, and especially the parents’ affairs with other people. It was presented way too quickly, almost in an ironic tone that felt a bit off to me. Was it meant to be comic relief? It didn’t work. Could it have leaned more into drama and carried a heavier tone to keep us more engaged? Definitely.

reviews.Name: Lessons of Darkness
reviews.Review:
To be honest, I’m not sure what to think about this project. It was incredibly heavy… At the same time, many of the images that filled the screen lingered for too long, and that made me lose focus at times. They’re shocking, yes, but I couldn’t fully engage with everything because the camera kept circling and circling, trying to suggest something that wasn’t being conveyed by the narrator. I can’t really explain it any better than that…

It’s another project that shows just how awful we can be, and in a way it’s interesting to watch, especially in times like these, with so much political, social, and economic uncertainty.

But one thing is clear: we’re all going to die if we keep making decisions that make absolutely no sense.

reviews.Name: The Movement of Things
reviews.Review:
I wasn’t expecting such a slow film to captivate me this much. Maybe it’s because I saw my country represented, a poem written through cameras pointed at a way of life.

I’m not entirely sure how I found this film. It had been sitting on my watchlist (not here, on one collecting dust in my Google Keep), but I know it was a great decision to put it there, and to finally have the courage to watch it.

I’ll say it now: this is not a film for everyone. Many will probably find it boring, but I wish more Portuguese people would watch it with pride in their chest and a sense of longing for what we still have yet to live.

It’s a tribute to the Portuguese spirit, raw and unfiltered, someone who lives a life simply by living it, one day at a time, and ends up living a happy life close to the people they love.

There’s nothing quite as beautiful as being Portuguese.

Vivam a vida, um dia de cada vez, com orgulho em ser a melhor mistura que podíamos ser: Português.

reviews.Name: Urchin
reviews.Review:
It’s an incredibly interesting film with a perspective that moves away from the usual clichés often found in stories like this.

I won’t lie: I added this project to my watchlist solely because a well-known actor was directing it. Is that a bit “strange” or maybe even rude as a reason? Maybe, but the truth is, I watched this film without even reading the synopsis, and honestly, it was the best decision I could’ve made. If I had known anything about the project beforehand, I might not have watched it at all. I’ve seen a lot of films dealing with drugs, addiction, second chances, and the search for purpose.

What surprised me here is how those themes are approached in a much more “subtle” way, making the character feel incredibly raw, almost like he’s confiding in us. And it’s not just the writing: the camera work, the editing, and… the COLORS in every scene make everything more immersive, more emotional, more abstract.

There is, however, one negative point I can’t ignore: I loved the way we see Mike daydreaming, those almost psychedelic moments are brilliant, but I wanted more of them. It feels like Harris held back a little, as if he was afraid to go further in that direction. Maybe stepping away from the drama too much felt like a risk. Maybe.

reviews.Name: The Drama
reviews.Review:
Kristoffer Borgli, you keep surprising me and climbing higher and higher in my personal ranking.
I genuinely don’t understand some of the negative opinions… sure, everyone has their own taste, but you can’t walk into a cinema expecting a rom-com just because the story revolves around a couple. Especially when we’re talking about Borgli, an extraordinary mind, delivering sharp social commentary in his films, chaotic editing that somehow holds together an avalanche of emotions, and camera work that keeps getting more refined.
Borgli expands his eccentricity almost like a funnel, but in reverse: he starts with something intimate and calm, builds a connection with the characters, and then gradually increases the suspense, the tension, and those awkward moments until you reach a point where you don’t even know if you should laugh, cry, or just look away from the screen because of how uncomfortable everything feels. And that’s exactly why I wanted to see this in a cinema: until now, I hadn’t had the chance to experience one of his films on the big screen.
A24 really has some brilliant minds in their hands, people capable of creating projects that stay with you for a long time. And this one even had Ari Aster as a producer (I swear, those two must love working together).

reviews.Name: The Assistant
reviews.Review:
I think it’s easy to understand how important this film is, both in its premise and in the way the story unfolds. Still, it wasn’t a project that kept me engaged until the end. Quite the opposite.

I understand its influence. I understand why the script had to be this way, why it needed to be slow, so we could fully grasp the disturbing reality the character is subjected to… But this just isn’t a film for me. It’s not something that calls me back for a rewatch.

I’m giving this rating not because of the film’s message - because, again, it’s incredibly important, hauntingly real, and unfortunately truthful - but because of how little it managed to hold my attention in several aspects. Even though I connected with the main character, I never felt fully immersed in the story.

reviews.Name: DEAD GRANDMA
reviews.Review: That was quick. Literally.

reviews.Name: The Loser
reviews.Review:
It’s truly a privilege to watch Kristoffer Borgli’s work.

I just watched a short film that has more substance than many feature-length films released throughout the year, and that alone says a lot about my experience in just a few minutes. Being able to see such a well-defined “character” in so little time is extraordinary.

The editing is IMPECCABLE. It adds comedic moments and allows us to escape the cringe just enough: they never drag, they hit at exactly the right moment, cutting to another scene or image so we can catch our breath, even if only briefly.

MAN I LOVE NORWEGIAN FILMMAKERS (AND MAN I LOVE KRISTOFFER BORGLI).

reviews.Name: It's Not a Phase
reviews.Review: Excuse me? I actually feel sorry for you, don't tell me not to.

reviews.Name: The Moment
reviews.Review:
I found myself a bit unsure about where this project was trying to go. At times it feels like a documentary, but then it leans into drama/thriller influences and even… mockumentary? I was left confused, and honestly, that started to bother me more as the film went on.

I also have to point out the questionable quality of some scenes: not just in terms of editing (colors, cuts, etc.), but also the excessive use of that low-budget, handheld documentary-style camera work.

It’s not all negative, though, I am giving it 3.5 stars, after all. It’s a film about Charli XCX, so of course I was going to enjoy it overall. Especially because it gives us a deeper look into her mindset, the pressure behind the scenes, and a personality clearly built for big stages. Every scene she’s in fills the frame and elevates the story, making everything instantly more engaging.

reviews.Name: Willem Dafoe
reviews.Review: YES, I NEED TO KNOW THE NAME OF THE ACTOR WITHOUT HAVING TO SEARCH IT IN AN AI OR ON GOOGLE. YES, it does my ego good.

reviews.Name: The Altruist
reviews.Review: "Nobody invited me", that brand of FOMO that plagues us all and sent me spiraling during COVID. But hey, Borgli, rest easy: you’re the first person I’m inviting to the anonymous mentally ill group chat, there's nothing wrong with that.

reviews.Name: Lee Cronin's The Mummy
reviews.Review:
I genuinely think Lee Cronin tried to inject British humor into a film about Egypt and FAMILY, and James Wan thought it was peak cinema. I can literally picture a scenario where Cronin presents the line “I’m her Daddy now” to the entire production team and everyone just stands up and starts clapping.

Yes, it’s a Blumhouse Productions film from 2026, so don’t walk into the cinema with high expectations. But there are definitely some positives to take from this project: a fairly interesting reboot of The Mummy, with body horror executed at just the right level. The entire mummification sequence is GENIUS (and I mean that sincerely), to the point where, if they made a whole film just about that, I’d give it 5 stars.
Of course, it’s not all great. The film lacks in several areas, especially in a sometimes childish script and lines that completely kill the mood. The ending felt rushed, and some unnecessary scenes were chosen to fill space that could have been used for something far more impactful and meaningful.

As for the funeral scene: for a moment, I genuinely thought I was watching "Death at a Funeral" (2010)… and trust me, I don’t mean that as a criticism.

reviews.Name: Syndromes
reviews.Review:
The idea isn’t bad, and the direction is definitely on a solid level. I mean, we’re talking about Kristoffer Borgli, of course the editing and direction were going to be good.

Anyway… I’m going to have trauma from this new X-Men.

reviews.Name: Michael
reviews.Review:
I have to thank Ms. Maria (the lady cleaning the theater during the credits) for the moment,  singing and dancing together in an already empty cinema: was genuinely so much fun.

I went to see this film alone. I wish I could’ve watched it with my mom, but unfortunately she had work the next day and it was impossible. She was the one who introduced me to Michael Jackson through my grandfather’s record player, with a tiny 45 RPM vinyl filled with his biggest hits: unfortunately, they couldn’t afford more at the time, so that’s what they had. I still remember telling my mom that I wanted to dance as well as Michael, and trying to do the moonwalk in front of her. It went badly. Very badly. She laughed at me, and honestly, I’m completely okay with that.

I didn’t walk into the cinema expecting the greatest film of all time. I didn’t expect the best CGI either (some moments looked decent, others somehow looked worse than every episode of She-Hulk). I went in hoping for a haunting performance (it was almost uncomfortable how accurate it felt), an overwhelming urge to stand up, sing, and dance, and sound design from another world.

That’s exactly what I got, and I’m happy with that.

I’m giving this rating because of everything this project represents to me, because of the experience I had (shout out to Ms. Maria, an incredible dancer with a broom), and because the casting was absolutely insane.

reviews.Name: Michael Jackson's Thriller
reviews.Review: WAIT. I DIDN’T KNOW I COULD LOG THIS. PERFECT.

reviews.Name: Michael Jackson: Bad
reviews.Review: THIS ONE I KNEW I COULD LOG, MISTER SCORCESE😋😫

reviews.Name: The Punisher: One Last Kill
reviews.Review:
I think we seriously need to start talking about how weak the connections between characters have become in Marvel projects. The CW genuinely handled that better with DC characters, and trust me, aside from the first season of The Flash and the first two seasons of Arrow, I thought most of it was a complete mess.

The moment I saw Jessica Jones randomly spawning into a season of Daredevil: Born Again, I started getting worried. I get it, New York is huge, different neighborhoods and all that, but when Wilson Fisk is the mayor… I don’t know.

Anyway, I’m not here to talk about Born Again, but about what felt, to me, like a post-credit scene for a film that somehow released between Daredevil and Spider-Man: Brand New Day. Everything I watched in this special felt like black screen text saying: “The Punisher will return in Spider-Man: Brand New Day.” And honestly? I’m not sure I liked that.

It’s true that I went into this with high expectations, something that could work as a bridge between the events of Born Again and Spider-Man’s street-level crime world. We didn’t get that. We got a revenge story (and yes, I know we’re talking about Punisher / Frank Castle) with very little substance.

Yes, Jon Bernthal was incredibly good. Yes, Frank’s psychological discomfort was explored surprisingly well in such a short runtime. But no… there’s not a single moment here that feels worthy of being remembered.

And by the way… that CGI somehow looked worse than Crisis on Infinite Earths during the Arrowverse era, and I think that already says a lot about the level of effort put into this special by the production team.

reviews.Name: Still
reviews.Review:
One of the reasons I love watching early career projects (short films) of my favorite filmmakers is because they open my eyes to everything a director went through while evolving and discovering more about their own cinematic identity. This short makes it very clear that Joachim Trier was still struggling to find himself… and I don’t mean that as a negative criticism at all. I loved how deliberately confusing it was, with extreme shifts in camera work. Those abrupt stylistic changes are exactly what make this project feel even more unique, even more interesting.

In just 20 minutes, Joachim manages to tell an incredibly touching story filled with fascinating touches of Nordic cinema.

God, Trier… I love you man. You keep surprising me so much.

reviews.Name: Pieta
reviews.Review:
“I sometimes have memories of things that could never have happened. Maybe they belong to somebody else.”

I’m genuinely not okay… was this really Joachim Trier’s first project? What an absolute banger. So much suffering, such radical emotional shifts, such a raw depiction of loss…

Joachim would later revisit these themes throughout his career, and honestly, I know very few directors capable of expressing this level of emotion in stories like these. I was going to say that this short film explores the deepest sides of loneliness, suicide, aging, and memory, but the truth is that all of Trier’s projects approach these subjects in such a raw and beautiful way…

reviews.Name: Mother Mary
reviews.Review:
I’m not going to lie, it took me some time to find the courage to write a review for this film.

It genuinely felt like me and Tiago were the only people in that cinema more excited about a new David Lowery film than a new Anne Hathaway film. And don’t get me wrong: of course I was curious to see her in what is, without a doubt, the most difficult role of her career so far. We were also shocked that nobody walked out halfway through the movie… The moment I realized where the story was heading, I immediately thought someone would find it incredibly weird. “Wait… this isn’t just a film about a pop star?”

No. It’s not just a film about a pop star, it has far more substance than that. If you dislike slow, psychologically destructive metaphors and analogies, do yourselves a favor and never watch a Lowery film. You can’t walk into one of his films expecting to watch “just that.” Knowing the director matters, The Lord is speaking.

As for the film itself: during the credits, I also turned to Tiago and said, “Well… it’s a David Lowery film.” I somehow have both a lot and very little to say about this project. It’s a beautiful analogy (and usually not my type of film), one that deliberately avoids exploring the fashion world in depth because it’s much more interested in exploring the relationship between the two central characters.

Michaela Coel was cast PERFECTLY, and finally, we got to see and hear mommy Hathaway sing.

It’s an uncomfortable film, with that very specific low-budget A24-type atmosphere, so don’t expect groundbreaking CGI… Even so, the concert scenes disappointed me a little. There are also one or two scenes I personally wouldn’t have handled the same way.

Ps: I LOVE YOU A24, PLEASE NEVER LOSE YOUR ESSENCE.

reviews.Name: Backrooms
reviews.Review:
And then… Renate Reinsve said “I am The Backrooms.”

This is probably going to be a completely different opinion from most people, and that’s perfectly fine, they’re the ones who are wrong, not me.

Horror is ALIVE, with so much still left to offer and entirely new subgenres continuing to emerge.

I want to start by saying that this rating doesn’t just represent what I felt while watching this on the big screen… it also exists to tell something to everyone who won’t find this film decent or substantial enough: you’re completely wrong. This is not an easy film to digest. As I said, it belongs to a completely different type of horror than anything we’ve seen before (and yes, we already survived Ari Aster). It isn’t slow, it simply wants to immerse us in that world, paying attention to detail and characters who clearly have much more left to say. Just feel it. Think about what you’re seeing and imagine everything the character must be thinking in that moment: the actors help enormously with that imaginative process.

I also need to thank Kane Parsons for not abandoning his original idea. It’s difficult to express how much admiration I have for people who hold onto their vision and try to show the best possible version of it. It’s even harder to take an idea that started small and project it onto the biggest screen possible. Kane, at 20 years old, managed to get two Oscar nominated actors working together. Kane, at 20 years old, managed to experiment with what a camera could or couldn’t do inside a space that feels simultaneously claustrophobic, familiar, and endless.

I also have to address the criticism I’ve been seeing in reviews, reels, and TikToks. This is not a conventional film. It’s not a film that places the entire story wide open right in front of you. People forget that a lot already existed before it reached cinemas. People forget this universe didn’t begin now, the story was already well established, structured, and explored. This film is only a small representation of everything that had already been created.

Renate Reinsve is simply perfect. And yes, I’m biased now, she’s my favorite actress. But believe me: she completely commands your attention from beginning to end, and her eyes say everything.

All that’s left for me to say is: give Parsons every possible budget imaginable so he can keep doing this. Not as a series (because that already happened on YouTube). I want something huge, just like this film.

reviews.Name: Louder Than Bombs
reviews.Review:
"There are days I'm invisible, I can do whatever I want. I must be careful not to lose that ability." - Conrad

(I’m leaving a few quotes that resonated with me at the top and bottom of this review...)

It feels like reading poetry late in the afternoon, watching the sunset over the mountains with a view that is... somehow deeply nostalgic. That is the best way I can describe what it felt like to watch this movie.

To the TikTokers and consumers of short, algorithm-processed content, I’ll say this: don’t watch it. Nothing will sink in, and you’ll just end up quitting halfway through. But to those who want to experience the very best that cinema has to offer... believe me, it’s worth it. Not just for the story itself, but for how they chose to tell it. Yes, it has a few under-explored subplots, but that was precisely the point: the emptiness of something (or someone) they lost.

The direction is rock-solid, featuring performances that will give you goosebumps down to your fingertips, and moments that will stay with me for a very long time. Moments where I felt... represented? Maybe that’s not the perfect word, but seeing Conrad go through all of that while dreaming of a girl he "can't have". I didn't have a similar family experience, but I’ve had times when I didn't believe in myself, and that doubt consumed my dreams. It consumed the plans I made, not just with my friends, but with my family. A bedroom that mirrored who I was, a passion for writing that haunted me (and still does), and a "dream girl who lives only in my dreams and nowhere else".

Joachim and Eskil, this won’t be the last time I sing your praises. Delivering a story that is so simple yet so complex is hard... incredibly hard. Thank you.

Ps: I'm still crying over that scene... Yes... That look...

"He could still, many years from now, recall the scene in all its detail. The lock of hair she placed behind her ear. The way the washing label stuck out from the neck of her tanktop. The streetlights that went out as they passed Kevin Anderson's house. That strangely familiar smell of damp earth that he couldn't quite place." - Melanie

reviews.Name: The Affected
reviews.Review: An incredibly timely short film, considering it’s 6 years old... I wonder why it hasn’t aged badly... The answer to that question is easy: human beings, forever condemned to their own stupidity.

reviews.Name: Obsession
reviews.Review:
I’m going to reinforce something I already said in my Backrooms review: horror is truly alive, my friends.

I honestly can’t remember the last time I got this many chills in a movie theater. REAL chills. Physical chills. Goosebumps. The kind that stay with you for minutes and leave a mark both physically and psychologically.

This is an incredibly solid film, with brilliant touches of drama, horror, and comedy. All three come together in scenes that feel simultaneously cringe and hilarious. The truth is that many moments are so tonally varied that they blend genres in a genuinely impressive way.

It has one or two flaws, but we can’t forget that this is Curry’s first film on this scale. I was a little disappointed that they never explained where the boxes containing the wish breaking object came from. I know there wasn’t much time for that, and they clearly wanted to focus on this particular wish, but still... not even a tiny bit of lore?

Either way, I loved every detail, every joke, every analogy, metaphor, and every exchange of blame. Because yes, in the end, it was always his fault...
"You used to be normal.", was probably one of the lines that made me the most uncomfortable, and it came from Bear himself.
Then again, I might be biased. If Nikki asked me to do something, I’d do it without blinking. Inde Navarrette, you are a goddess. 😭

reviews.Name: Procter
reviews.Review:
Honestly, I don’t know what to think about this short... The truth is, I wasn’t expecting something so dark and meticulous from Trier. I went into it blind without reading the premise, and I’m so glad I did... because I was expecting something much more sentimental and ended up with an incredibly unsettling story.

Curry Barker is right: YouTube is a massive gateway for discovering the work of up-and-coming creators, or even exploring the past projects of our favorite directors. To the person who uploaded this short with English subtitles: Thank you.

reviews.Name: Sexy Beast
reviews.Review:
I honestly wasn’t expecting this kind of movie. I started watching it the way I always do with directors who are starting to take center stage on my watchlist: I didn't read the synopsis. I knew absolutely nothing about the feature film—not the story, not the actors...

I won’t lie, I thought it was going to be incredibly philosophical and not so straightforward (the first movie I watched by Glazer was "Under the Skin (2013)"). The vibe is incredibly well-established, and you shouldn't expect to see many of the crime tropes typically associated with this movie's genres. Expect a slow start, with characters who are extremely simple yet have complicated premises that eventually trigger an avalanche of events, which, in my opinion, were very well handled.

It’s a movie that hasn’t aged particularly well in terms of camera effects, color, and CGI (the little it has), but honestly? It didn’t bother me (except maybe for that boulder scene behind Gal at the beginning of the film). The camerawork is solid for what a thriller demands, and it doesn't lack personality and atmosphere.

"But quite frankly your attitude appeals me. It's not what you're saying. It's all this stuff you're not saying. Insinnuendos." - I'm going to save this quote; I feel like I'm going to turn this expression into a meme in my everyday life.

reviews.Name: A Serious Man
reviews.Review:
This is definitely not the movie for me. I can’t quite explain it; maybe I just wasn’t in the right mood for it. Either way, I’m giving it this rating because of some truly incredible details I picked up on, especially the camerawork and the editing. The editing, with its extraordinary flow between scenes, was what kept me more invested in the feature film.

At the same time, I have to point out that the story is very simple but tends to get needlessly overcomplicated. That drastic shift halfway through the movie wasn't necessary, and I feel like it kind of killed my vibe because I went into it expecting a comedy-drama. I ended up feeling like it has more thriller elements, and the comedic beats weren't well executed. In fact, the comedy itself was, in my eyes, pretty childish and didn’t add much to the story or the mood.

Joel Coen and Ethan Coen: you can tell they have immense imaginative power and, above all, know exactly how to use the camera and color to emphasize certain scenes. I give them credit for that. I also applaud the production and the actors, because if it had been any other cast, I might not have made it to the end of the movie.

Are these feelings just because it's my birthday and I wasn't that interested in watching a movie about a physicist caught in an avalanche of events? Maybe.

reviews.Name: Chime
reviews.Review:
I have to say something: as crazy as it sounds, I can't seem to find Kurosawa's movies anywhere, but somehow I stumbled upon "Chime (2024)" lost on the internet... and I FINALLY found a second film of his to watch, since "Cure (1997)" is one of my all-time favorites.

My expectations were sky-high, and I feel like they were actually met. We're talking about a 45-minute project that tries to tackle a sound capable of altering people's personalities, or even changing their body temperature. That is incredibly difficult to pull off in such a short runtime, but Kurosawa manages to deliver moments of suspense and horror that keep you hooked until the very end. Because let's be honest: there are very few directors capable of creating such uncomfortable atmospheres in such a limited amount of time.

I also need to highlight how simplicity took over most of the scenes and how that made everything so much more immersive, psychotic, and dramatic. I got goosebumps watching the shift in the characters' tones, and it didn't take anything overly elaborate to give us those moments.

I NEED TO EXPLORE MORE OF KUROSAWA'S WORK. HELP ME FIND GOOD STREAMING PLATFORMS BECAUSE I WANT TO WATCH EVERYTHING HE'S EVER DONE.

loved_more.Name: Former Cult Member Hears Music for the First Time

watchlist_cosine.Name: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind

discoveries_cosine.Name: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
The Art of Racing in the Rain

watchlist_semantic.Name: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty

discoveries_semantic.Name: Maquia: When the Promised Flower Blooms
Believe Me: The Abduction of Lisa McVey
Josee, the Tiger and the Fish
Kill Bill: The Whole Bloody Affair

five_star.Name: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban
El Camino: A Breaking Bad Movie
If Anything Happens I Love You
Harry Potter 20th Anniversary: Return to Hogwarts
Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse
five_star.directors: Kemp Powers|Justin K. Thompson|Joaquim Dos Santos

five_star.Name: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar

half_star.Name: Big Mommas: Like Father, Like Son
A Glimpse Inside the Mind of Charles Swan III

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Sentimental Value
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
They called me a masochist for watching this film in the cinema twice within a week… they’ll probably call me the same again for having watched it a third time in such a short period of time, and honestly? I don’t care.

It continues, and will continue, to be impossible to describe the importance this project has in my life. It may sound strange, but the truth is that I will never forget the first time I watched this, alone, on the big screen. I’ll never forget the fear I felt when I walked into the theater with extremely high expectations, without even having watched a trailer or knowing what the film was about (on purpose, because of the impact and surprise it had on audiences).

What’s even more interesting is catching details that I missed the first times, like the monologue delivered by Elle Fanning and Stellan’s gaze, which says more than a thousand-page script ever could.

As I said in my previous reviews, it’s simply a perfect film, kept in a special corner of my heart for everything it represents, for everything it tells through dialogue and through distant, silent looks. A project that marked me for life.

“It's like her sadness is such an overwhelming part of her.”

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Dream Scenario
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
While watching this project, I kept thinking, "This is exactly the kind of movie Ari Aster would make, from the editing to the script itself"... When the credits started rolling, I read "Produced by Ari Aster," and my jaw dropped: one of the best plot twists ever. The man is haunting me... Or am I the one haunting him? Either way, great movie.

Edit: Another one from A24, let’s keep the momentum going!

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Good Time
Directors Peak Cuts.review: "Beau is Afraid" but indie style, with less steroids, and in my opinion: much better.

Directors Peak Cuts.name: The Lighthouse
Directors Peak Cuts.review: Moral of the story: don't EVER spill yer' beans.

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Oppenheimer
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
One of the greatest films of all time.
Rewatching the most terrifying story humanity has ever experienced… It leaves me devastated every single time.
It’s a story that pushed me toward Physics, that made me follow this path — not because of the political context, but because of the names involved: from Rabi to Feynman, physicists who have always crossed my path, whether through mandatory study or my own personal research.
When I watched it for the first time, already knowing the whole story (thanks to my obsession with everyone involved), I was blown away by the level of detail. And the truth is, to this day, I’m still in awe of the dialogue and depth embedded in this project.

Man… I LOVE PHYSICS. Even when they called me stupid… and honestly, it’s exactly in those moments that I felt an even stronger urge to study harder.
Masochistic or not, it was a degree that brought me genuine joy with every passing year.

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Hereditary
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
This film is the culmination of everything I want in a horror film. Probably one of the best scripts I've ever seen on a project of this kind. So many symbolisms, so many precedents for everything that will happen, everything so well planned. We see it from Annie's perspective but we get to understand much more than she does and that was simply GENIUS. 
Chilling music, jumpscares that left my heart in my mouth, simply horrific and chilling content, it's truly something "a la Ari Aster"... You're crazy, I sincerely hope you never change, because we need more things like this.

Directors Peak Cuts.name: Under the Skin
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
What I took from this film: the plot isn’t what matters, it’s the perspective. 

What a sensational experience... So simple yet so abstract — visually haunting in a way that contrasts beautifully with the human experience, the experience of just being... human.
Scarlett’s character slowly begins to understand the meaning we, as humans, give to life — things like kindness, solidarity, care for others… or even monstrous instincts (like the man in the forest).
This was without a doubt a cinematic experience that will stay with me forever, in the best way possible. I also know this isn’t a film for everyone, and many might not enjoy the heavy abstraction… But me? I LOVED it — and I’m still considering the 5 stars.

Directors Peak Cuts.name: The Lobster
Directors Peak Cuts.review:
Yorgos, és um maníaco, insanamente marado, louco... Deixas-me sempre sem palavras quando acabo de ver um filme teu.
Tanto simbolismo, tanta metáfora e coisas tão hiperbolizadas mas que fazem TANTO sentido dentro da sociedade atual... Um espelho completamente exagerado do nosso mundo, deixou-me tão desconfortável e tão pensativo ao mesmo tempo - genial.
Cinematografia arrepiante, diálogos super estranhos, às vezes quase que nem parece bem representado pelo ator em si... E no fim chegas à conclusão que era tudo de propósito para pensares fora da caixa, para te deixar fora da tua zona de conforto.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Sentimental Value
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
They called me a masochist for watching this film in the cinema twice within a week… they’ll probably call me the same again for having watched it a third time in such a short period of time, and honestly? I don’t care.

It continues, and will continue, to be impossible to describe the importance this project has in my life. It may sound strange, but the truth is that I will never forget the first time I watched this, alone, on the big screen. I’ll never forget the fear I felt when I walked into the theater with extremely high expectations, without even having watched a trailer or knowing what the film was about (on purpose, because of the impact and surprise it had on audiences).

What’s even more interesting is catching details that I missed the first times, like the monologue delivered by Elle Fanning and Stellan’s gaze, which says more than a thousand-page script ever could.

As I said in my previous reviews, it’s simply a perfect film, kept in a special corner of my heart for everything it represents, for everything it tells through dialogue and through distant, silent looks. A project that marked me for life.

“It's like her sadness is such an overwhelming part of her.”

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Grave of the Fireflies
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
I have few words to describe this project.
The theme may be quite “common” in cinema in general, but this movie managed to exceed all my expectations.

Perfect. That’s the word that defines everything I just watched.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: I'm Still Here
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
Now with my parents… And I'll keep watching, to never forget how it makes me feel. I'll show this to my sons/daughters... 

Even though I’m not Brazilian, I feel proud to have been born speaking this beautiful language.

The ice cream scene gets me every time.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Soul
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
“You can't crush a soul here, that's what life on Earth is for.” - 22

Sometimes we need to reconnect with our inner peace, especially in moments when stress, anxiety, and discouragement show up uninvited. I found this film during one of those moments, and I promised myself I would come back to it whenever I felt “lost” in things I still had to do: deadlines, responsibilities, times when I spend more time stuck in my room than outside, actually living life.

Don’t get me wrong, I love studying. I love working on projects that challenge me, that push me to my limits. But I reach a point where stopping feels like pressing a button covered in dust somewhere deep in my brain.

I used to be a lot like Joe… focused exclusively on what I could achieve academically and professionally. I forgot there was more to life than that.

This is a very special film to me. It reminds me of where I came from, what I’ve been through, and who I am now.

A project that… makes me stop. Think. Feel nostalgic for what I’ve lived and plan everything I still want to discover and experience.

Oh and by the way... Music is a big part of me, and without it... I would probably be one of those damned souls.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: A Ghost Story
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
I’m still thinking about the rating I should give this project (I’m seriously considering 5 stars)… I’ve been trying to write something about what I just watched for 30 minutes, but the shock is so intense that I’m speechless. How can something so simple be so powerful… How did a Halloween costume manage to leave me in tears? I have a headache from crying so much.  
Suffering, loss — words that were very clearly expressed throughout the film… But "saudade" prevailed in my interpretation of it all. Absolutely beautiful.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Manchester by the Sea
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
Isto é uma masterpiece ok? É o culminar do melhor que o drama pode dar: raiva, tristeza, choro, melancolia, pena. A história é tão simples mas tão cativante, tão dramática. 
Junta atores extraordinários, visuais simplistas mas credíveis, todas as imagens são importantes para o mood que querem incutir. 
Casey Affleck… És um monstro. Chorei, demasiado, e foste o maior culpado desse sucedido.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Causeway
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
I don't care what anyone else thinks about this… I've read so many comments saying it's boring — enough. This is hauntingly beautiful and chilling. The pacing was nearly perfect, I was completely drawn to the characters, I wanted to know more about the two of them, and I honestly don't think it was just because of the actors: the script is chilling, simple, and effective.

The ending left me in pieces, and the final line perfectly captured what both of them were looking for: “I'm trying to make a friend.” Sometimes, just a quiet cup of coffee in the morning with the right company can mean the world.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Beautiful Boy
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
Sem pestanejar. 5 estrelas. Foram capazes de usar um dos meus poemas preferidos de sempre. Sempre li ofegantemente, a pensar na minha vida e colocam assim, a meio e no final do filme, para impactar ainda mais. Nunca mais vou ser o mesmo. Bukowski escreveu um dos poemas mais simples, mais diretos - sem medos ou receios - tal como os pais deste miúdo, que viveram sem medo de enfrentar a situação. 
Chalamet, Carrell, sinto-me honrado... Ver-vos a representar estes papeis incomodou-me, deixou-me sem ar, fez-me chorar de forma insólita. Foi tudo tão bem planeado, tão expressivo, tão... especial.
Sampha na banda sonora e a última imagem... os dois, sentados em bancos de jardim... Levou-me ao pico da loucura, e ao pico do choro.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: The Metamorphosis of Birds
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
“A distância daqueles que nós amamos é tão difícil. Porém ela é também muito construtiva. Constrói quem eu sou.”

Poetry and cinema joined forces to create this film.
During this short and chaotic journey of mine through cinema, there has always been something that bothered me: not knowing and not having truly felt Portuguese film and Portuguese cinema. I’m here to change that, and I feel like I started in the best way possible - what an unbelievable project.
Unsettling dialogues, meaningful from start to finish, with metaphors that follow us as we get to know the family, all culminating in that final scene. Chilling from beginning to end, a poem that came straight from the heart.

I feel an enormous pride in being Portuguese and in seeing my culture represented in such a beautiful way. A pride in our Latin roots, a pride in our mixture, a pride in the Portuguese saudade of being who we are.

“Deixa-me sempre muito espantado como é que uma coisa tão infinita consegue entrar por nós a dentro” - e agora em português, o oceano é assustador, mas será sempre a nossa casa.

Emotional Damage Cinema.name: Dead Poets Society
Emotional Damage Cinema.review:
Não queria ver algo novo, decidi ficar no aconchego de um filme preferido meu. 
Preferido porque tudo aquilo que me ensinaram sobre poesia, na escola, valeu zero para mim. E agora é uma das coisas mais importantes da minha vida. Aprendi sozinho, aprendi a entender versos à minha maneira e não à maneira dos outros.
Estamos aqui para viver, para quê ser realista? Prefiro sonhar e usar a poesia para enaltecer o facto de não ser uma máquina. Apenas um ser humano...

"Two roads diverged in a wood, and I—
I took the one less traveled by,
And that has made all the difference." - Robert Frost

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: 20th Century Women
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
It was incredible. Truly incredible.
Whenever I watch a drama, I go in wanting to get to know the characters—every single one of them—and that’s something I always take into account. This project not only portrays Dorothea’s and Jamie’s lives, but it also dives into so many other important people connected to them, and they’re all so well fleshed out…
A script that had me in tears by the end, simply because I got to know what happened to each one of them.

I have to highlight once again the essays presented throughout the film. Just like in "C’mon C’mon (2021)", it made the whole experience so much more interesting—watching the characters read those texts felt seamless within the story and helped me understand each one of them better, what they were going through, what they were feeling in that exact moment.

Oh, and by the way, I love Talking Heads.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Adderall Diaries
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I just couldn’t take this film seriously.
Not sure if it was Amber Heard or the story itself — maybe both — but it really didn’t work for me.
Way too many unnecessary scenes (especially around Stephen and Lana’s relationship), painfully basic cinematography, and a plot so predictable I felt like I could’ve written it in my sleep.
It honestly felt like they threw in a bunch of last-minute ideas hoping it would make things cooler… but nope, it just made everything worse.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: After Yang
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I know the theme represented in this project has been done before, but the way the story was told made everything feel special.

The cinematography is out of this world — absolutely fascinating and unafraid to explore different types of imagery and camera work. The use of color was incredibly detailed, paired with a script that had me hooked from beginning to end.

The scenes where Jake "visited" Yang’s memories left me breathless, they were just so beautiful. The conversation between the android and Kyra left me speechless, and the short dialogue between Ada and Jake in the car completely floored me — so simple, yet so powerful.

An incredible experience, and I’m sure I’ll want to rewatch this film again someday.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Aftersun
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Deixou-me despedaçado. Deixou-me parado, a olhar para o ecrã, no final do filme. Durante uns 10 minutos. Apenas a pensar em todos os detalhes que foram dando ao longo do filme sobre a depressão do Calum.
Um tópico tão importante e explicado de forma simplesmente… Brilhante. Não há outra palavra para definir, todos os momentos reais ou imaginados pela Sophie, todas as imagens e cinematografia, todos os diálogos, simples mas impactantes, todas as pequenas cenas que mostravam aquilo que o Calum era quando não estava com ela. Foi tudo tão bem representado que sou capaz de me lembrar de todos os pequenos momentos em que vias o quão o Calum não gostava de si mesmo… Cenas tão pequenas como ele cuspir a pasta de dentes para o espelho onde se via.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: All Dirt Roads Taste of Salt
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I had a hard time with the long scenes, which kept me from giving this a higher rating. It's impossible to deny how haunting the message is or how beautiful the cinematography can be, but the transitions between scenes took far too long, and my eyes often lost focus. As impactful as the visuals were, they would linger for 5 or 6 seconds in the same spot, and once the scene changed, I found myself losing track of what I was watching.
In my opinion, the editing was poorly executed, and that made this experience less enjoyable than it could have been.
Still, I have to highlight how well the film portrayed loss and grief under certain circumstances — that part was deeply moving and thoughtfully implemented.

“Mack: You want to know a secret? It doesn't end or begin. Just changes form.
Lily: What changes?
Mack: Water. All these drops might be a river someday. Might be snow. Might be in you.”

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Amy
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
This was one of the hardest projects to watch. Amy had such a huge impact on the love I have for music that, while watching it, I felt pieces of myself falling apart.  
Seeing and hearing everything she did—not just in the studio but also on tour—hurt, even knowing everything that had happened. In fact, I feel like knowing her full story only intensified my experience even more.  

She was—and will always be—one of my favorite artists. She still inspires me to do the little, silly things I do with my guitar and my voice.  

My favorite part, without a doubt, was when "Back to Black" started playing with just her voice… then transitioned to the studio version, and finally ended again with her singing “black” at the mic. It completely broke me.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Babygirl
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
It’s clear that this film wasn’t made with the intention of winning any awards or anything like that. It’s a film about sex and kinks, and I’ve never really been a fan of this type of project. From the little I’ve seen in this genre, this one does stand out — though it still suffers from major script issues. I understand that the goal was to explore this kind of sexual content, but I have to evaluate the film as a whole.

Samuel didn’t even seem to have a job, Romy’s family was barely explored (and if that wasn’t the intention, they shouldn’t have included scenes with Isabel, one of her daughters), and Jacob was far from convincing... The best part was definitely the complexity of Kidman’s character, which I found genuinely interesting.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Backrooms
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
And then… Renate Reinsve said “I am The Backrooms.”

This is probably going to be a completely different opinion from most people, and that’s perfectly fine, they’re the ones who are wrong, not me.

Horror is ALIVE, with so much still left to offer and entirely new subgenres continuing to emerge.

I want to start by saying that this rating doesn’t just represent what I felt while watching this on the big screen… it also exists to tell something to everyone who won’t find this film decent or substantial enough: you’re completely wrong. This is not an easy film to digest. As I said, it belongs to a completely different type of horror than anything we’ve seen before (and yes, we already survived Ari Aster). It isn’t slow, it simply wants to immerse us in that world, paying attention to detail and characters who clearly have much more left to say. Just feel it. Think about what you’re seeing and imagine everything the character must be thinking in that moment: the actors help enormously with that imaginative process.

I also need to thank Kane Parsons for not abandoning his original idea. It’s difficult to express how much admiration I have for people who hold onto their vision and try to show the best possible version of it. It’s even harder to take an idea that started small and project it onto the biggest screen possible. Kane, at 20 years old, managed to get two Oscar nominated actors working together. Kane, at 20 years old, managed to experiment with what a camera could or couldn’t do inside a space that feels simultaneously claustrophobic, familiar, and endless.

I also have to address the criticism I’ve been seeing in reviews, reels, and TikToks. This is not a conventional film. It’s not a film that places the entire story wide open right in front of you. People forget that a lot already existed before it reached cinemas. People forget this universe didn’t begin now, the story was already well established, structured, and explored. This film is only a small representation of everything that had already been created.

Renate Reinsve is simply perfect. And yes, I’m biased now, she’s my favorite actress. But believe me: she completely commands your attention from beginning to end, and her eyes say everything.

All that’s left for me to say is: give Parsons every possible budget imaginable so he can keep doing this. Not as a series (because that already happened on YouTube). I want something huge, just like this film.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Backstabbing for Beginners
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
As soon as I realized it was an adaptation of an autobiographical book, I immediately assumed it was going to be a boring film… And guess what? It really was.

The themes are undeniably important — I’m not here to downplay the social and ethical issues within the UN, especially regarding the aid to Iraq, as presented in the film — but I just can’t understand how they failed to make it more engaging.
Everything was well structured in theory, but the dialogues? They made me zone out completely.
The cinematography didn’t help either — dull and tedious — and the actors did what they could to keep the film alive.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Ballad of Lefty Brown
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Yes, I’ve said it plenty of times: I don’t like westerns.
But imagine this — I’d rather win a bet and watch the entire A24 catalogue than give up halfway through because of this film. 
There are definitely some positives, mostly in the cinematography: beautiful shots, many of them simple, evoking the classic essence of the genre.
It’s a film that tries to cast heroism onto a character who was never built to be a hero.
Many scenes were just plain dull (speaking as someone who has no plans to revisit this type of story).

The actors were strong, sure, but even they couldn’t save it from being a slog.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Barely Lethal
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I feel like when I become a dad, I’ll probably be like Mr. Marcus — and I’m not sure if that’s a good thing or not.

Either way, this feels more Disney than A24, and that’s totally fine… My problem lies in how everything unfolded — and let’s just say it wasn’t… good… not good at all.
Seeing Steinfeld in Sinners and now here, what an absurd difference — the growth and improvement in her acting is incredible.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Blackcoat's Daughter
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
This rating is mostly because I also gave "Saint Maud" 3.5 stars… Honestly, I think I need to rewatch both films — I genuinely feel like this score might go up.

Truth is, it’s not exactly a horror film: sure, it has elements of the genre, but I’d say it leans much more toward a psychological thriller. I’m seriously considering changing my rating to 4 stars (and "Saint Maud" too) simply because this is such a well-crafted project, with every part clearly defined and tightly written.

An eerie, spine-chilling soundtrack kicks in right when it should, and the suspense scenes are so well done. I get why people criticize the “slow burn” approach and say it takes a while for anything major to happen — but I actually think that worked in its favour and made everything even more grounded and compelling.

PS and edited: I’ve decided to change the ratings for both films… I truly think they deserve more recognition for the way they approached horror — something far from the typical style we keep seeing in adjacent works (looking at you, "The Conjuring" universe).

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Bling Ring
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Based on a true story, so criticizing the script feels a bit tricky. However, I can easily call out the cinematography and editing because, honestly… terrible.
As for the case itself, it was definitely interesting to learn about. The irresponsibility—or rather, the sheer naivety—reached such an extreme that reality never even seemed to cross their minds.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Bodies Bodies Bodies
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Oh Gen Z… you’re so lost. I genuinely feel bad — and yes, I’m excluding myself from the group because self-awareness still exists somewhere.

Despite actually enjoying the film, the satire, and all those painfully on-the-nose critiques, I left the movie deeply offended... on Greg’s behalf. He was my favorite. He deserved better.

Also, Max showing up at the end? That was beyond stupid, unnecessary, and it completely ruined the experience for me.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Boys State
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
WOW, what a brilliantly made piece!
It really seemed like an intense political thriller, full of twists, with behind-the-scenes and everything that happened “outside” the “political life” created by the students.

I have to highlight the incredible ability they had to create appealing and REAL characters — completely plausible in American society or any other, really.
Special mention goes to Ben Feinstein: strong and intimidating personality (unfortunately, guys like this do exist), and he irritated me from start to finish — just like the CHEGA folks in Portugal. COMPLETE DISGUST.

You can honestly learn A LOT from this documentary. It’s fascinating, concerning, and downright TERRIFYING.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Brutalist
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I really spent a long time figuring out what score I should give to this project. I'm somewhere in the middle, between the 3.5 and 4.5 stars I've planned since the credits started rolling. I can easily say that it was one of my favorite cinematic experiences... But I can also say that I will hardly see this film again: I feel like it won't have the same impact at home.
Brutal actors, ending that really left me thinking and soundtrack 10/10.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Captive
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Some script flaws keep my rating from being higher. The direction itself feels a bit amateurish. The actors might be fantastic, but they can’t exactly save a story like this — especially with such a terrible, unsatisfying ending.  

Not everything is awful, though… Like I said, the actors were incredible, and the actress who played Cassie really stood out. Ryan Reynolds himself delivered a solid performance too.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Causeway
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I don't care what anyone else thinks about this… I've read so many comments saying it's boring — enough. This is hauntingly beautiful and chilling. The pacing was nearly perfect, I was completely drawn to the characters, I wanted to know more about the two of them, and I honestly don't think it was just because of the actors: the script is chilling, simple, and effective.

The ending left me in pieces, and the final line perfectly captured what both of them were looking for: “I'm trying to make a friend.” Sometimes, just a quiet cup of coffee in the morning with the right company can mean the world.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Children Act
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
We’re talking about a film that must’ve been incredibly hard to write and produce, given all the moral and emotional weight it carries.
We’re also talking about a film set in the UK — not in Portugal, where I was born and live… And of course, I don’t know the law well enough to comment on the specific legal matter, but I can still share my opinion: a minor should never be allowed to make that kind of decision, and religion should never be part of the equation.
Visually, it’s quite underwhelming, the performance is haunting (in the best sense), and the third act could’ve had a stronger development and conclusion.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Civil War
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Complicado descrever o que senti ao ver isto. Vou mais além e digo que é mesmo impossível. Houveram cenas em que me apetecia saltar da cadeira de tão arrepiado que me sentia. E não falo em específico, refiro-me sim ao geral do filme. 
Frases e diálogos extremamente bem conseguidos, com duplos significados e de compreensão profunda.
Mas o que mais me impressionou foi mesmo o rumo que deram: não é um filme sobre guerra, é um filme sobre jornalismo de guerra. Tudo o que sofrem, toda a adrenalina que sentem no momento… Simplesmente TUDO, bem representado por cada personagem apresentada.
Não minto que dava um final diferente à história, com toques mais satíricos e hiperbólicos, mas overall, muito bem conseguido.
Sobre esta crítica? Não consigo descrever melhor… “Yeah, that'll do.”

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Climax
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Não vou mentir: dói-me não dar 5 estrelas. Tenho uma razão que me leva a crer que esta é a melhor pontuação que posso dar ao que assisti e acreditem, não é do filme, é de mim. 
É assustador ler pessoal a referir que este é o projeto menos "louco" do Noé... Pôs-me stressado, ansioso e desgastado psicologicamente. E a cena final  colocou-me num estado pior: degradado. 
Desde cinematografia, onde chegamos quase a perceber aquilo que o camera man está a sentir (coisa que nunca pensei dizer sobre um filme), passando por toda uma peça teatral (diria várias divididas em fases), indo para a capacidade que os atores tiveram para expressar a loucura e a sinistralidade da droga acabando no som. O som, do início ao fim é constrangedor, faz-te querer saltar da cadeira, subir uma parede e fugir só para não sentir a evolução da música. A própria forma como o som é projetado pela câmara, aqueles momentos em que o camera man seguia as personagens para fora do espaço "central", o tempo que foi acelerando à medida que tudo ficava ainda mais insano...
Acabo tentando realçar o porquê de não ter dado 5 estrelas: não sei se algum dia vou voltar a ver isto, tenho medo de voltar a sentir tudo o que senti.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Close
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
An incredibly emotional journey (and it frustrates me to read comments casually tossing around the word "manipulative") that simply left me… silent. Just staring at the credits for a good few minutes.

I may not have experienced what Léo went through, but I felt connected to him throughout the entire film. The way he dealt with loss—barely speaking about it, going through everything almost "alone" —reminded me of a version of myself that always did the same. Is it a mistake? Yes, but sometimes silence can be the loudest scream.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: C'mon C'mon
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
This was beautiful. I can’t find another word to define what I just experienced… I might be biased when I call this film a masterpiece: I just became an uncle (well, a year and a few — I’d even say brief — months ago). What I saw here is everything I want to be for my nephew — his best friend, or at the very least, the person who annoys him the most.

Everything was perfect. The dialogues were beautiful. The rehearsals being read aloud as the film progressed revealed every single feeling — not just from the characters, but from all of us, both while watching and when waking up to start a new day.

I can’t keep writing or I’ll end up crying even more… Perfect.


"Jesse: [interviewer voice] Have you ever thought about the future?
[normal voice]
Jesse: Ahh yeah, whatever you plan on happening never happens. Stuff you would never think of happens. So you just have to c'mon c'mon, c'mon c'mon c'mon."

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Cut Bank
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
Big-name actors only to end up watching Liam Hemsworth deliver a downright mediocre performance. I wasn’t ready for something so poorly explained, so nonsensical, and above all, so passionless.

The only decent takeaway from this film is a handful of visuals — and especially Michael Stuhlbarg, who completely sold me. He did his absolute best with the script he was given. I’ve always said Michael is a brilliant actor, and this just reinforces my point.
As for the rest… Terrible.

“That, indeed, is the taking of a human life” — NO SHIT SHERLOCK.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: Dark Places
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I read a comment that said this movie was shot similarly to The Office... Well, let me just say The Office has better cinematography than this.

I walked into this film fully aware it was going to be bad. And yet... I still left even more disappointed. It’s a rare feeling, but when it hits? Damn, it hurts.

The performances were so weak they felt robotic... I feel bad for Hoult, he was the only one who actually pulled it off here.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: De Palma
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
It was extraordinarily fun to watch, mainly because he was talking directly to us the entire time.
I loved the way he broke down the concept of artistic freedom vs. the film industry, and I agree with him 100% — at least from what I can observe from the outside.

He's a director who's often misunderstood, but thankfully has unmistakable classics under his belt.
Here, he opens up about all the struggles he faced in his early years in a documentary that’s brilliantly executed and genuinely interesting.

Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.name: The Death of Dick Long
Every A24 Film That I Have Ever Seen.review:
I’m not gonna lie — when I found out how Richard died, I burst out laughing… Now, should I have?
