After studying English literature at University College London, he made several short films before his feature film debut with Following (1998). Nolan gained international recognition with his second film, Memento (2000), and transitioned into studio filmmaking with Insomnia (2002). He became a high-profile director with The Dark Knight trilogy (2005–2012). He found further success with The Prestige (2006), Inception (2010), Interstellar (2014) and Dunkirk (2017). After the release of Tenet (2020), Nolan parted ways with longtime distributor Warner Bros. Pictures. He signed with Universal Pictures for Oppenheimer (2023), which won him Academy Awards for Best Director and Best Picture.

Three of Nolan's films have been inducted into the National Film Registry. Infused with a metaphysical outlook, his works thematise epistemology, existentialism, ethics, the construction of time and the malleable nature of memory and personal identity. They feature mathematically inspired images and concepts, unconventional narrative structures, practical special effects, experimental soundscapes, large-format film photography and materialistic perspectives. His enthusiasm for the use and preservation of traditional film stock has also garnered significant attention. He has co-written several of his films with his brother, Jonathan. He runs the production company Syncopy Inc. with his wife, Emma Thomas.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Christopher Nolan, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Interstellar
films.review: I'm so scared of the future. The IMAX vibes just make everything worse.

films.name: Oppenheimer
films.review:
One of the greatest films of all time.
Rewatching the most terrifying story humanity has ever experienced… It leaves me devastated every single time.
It’s a story that pushed me toward Physics, that made me follow this path — not because of the political context, but because of the names involved: from Rabi to Feynman, physicists who have always crossed my path, whether through mandatory study or my own personal research.
When I watched it for the first time, already knowing the whole story (thanks to my obsession with everyone involved), I was blown away by the level of detail. And the truth is, to this day, I’m still in awe of the dialogue and depth embedded in this project.

Man… I LOVE PHYSICS. Even when they called me stupid… and honestly, it’s exactly in those moments that I felt an even stronger urge to study harder.
Masochistic or not, it was a degree that brought me genuine joy with every passing year.

films.name: Following
films.review:
To be completely honest, and I promise I was impartial in my rating... 4 stars because I would never in my life have thought of a plot like this. Something that at first seems simple, but the timeline and everything having a purpose makes it even deeper. I also give this rating because a film that cost 6000 pounds, that was filmed on weekends? It's for very few.

I can only finish this review with some advice: never fall for strangers' schemes. It's easier to say now, since ingenuity has decided not to appear, at least for now.

films.name: Doodlebug
films.review: Nolan smoked some things before turning on the camera for this project.

films.name: Quay
films.review: This was very boring… but the camera work was very solid.

Damien Chazelle.name: Damien Chazelle
Damien Chazelle.bio:
Damien Sayre Chazelle (/ʃəˈzɛl/; born January 19, 1985) is a French-American filmmaker. He directed the psychological drama Whiplash (2014), the musical romance La La Land (2016), the biographical drama First Man (2018), and the period black comedy Babylon (2022).

For Whiplash, he was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay. His biggest commercial success came with La La Land, nominated for 14 Academy Awards, winning six, including Best Director, making him the youngest person to win the award at age 32. He has also directed two episodes of the Netflix limited series The Eddy (2020).

Description above from the Wikipedia article Damien Chazelle, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: La La Land
films.review:
Tive a oportunidade de rever este filme limitando pontos de escárnio ou sarcasmo. A realidade é que era demasiado puto quando vi pela primeira vez e decidi dar uma segunda oportunidade. Ainda bem que dei. É um filme quase perfeito. Um musical requintado, com letras incríveis, toques teatrais esplêndidos e interpretações emotivas, sensacionais.
É definitivamente um daqueles filmes que me vão ficar na memória. Porque quando era mais novo não tinha esta ligação com a cinematografia, não conseguia nem percebia o quão importante isto é para as pessoas, o poder de influenciar e fazer sentir coisas fora da caixa.
Ryan, Emma, que prazer de vos ver a encenar.

films.name: Babylon
films.review:
How to tell the story of cinema? Easy: hire a group of extraordinary actors, create beautiful sets, write an odyssey, launch some criticisms of Hollywood, the kind that are over a hundred years old and create scenes that could remain forever in the viewer's memory. Maybe it's not that easy...

I just wish the movie was 20 minutes shorter :)

Daniel Scheinert.name: Daniel Scheinert
Daniel Scheinert.bio:
Daniel Scheinert (born June 7, 1987) is an American filmmaker and one-half of the filmmaking duo known as the Daniels, alongside Daniel Kwan. He began his career as a director of music videos, including ones for "Houdini" (2012) by Foster the People and "Turn Down for What" (2013) by DJ Snake and Lil Jon, both of which earned Grammy Award nominations. Scheinert co-wrote and co-directed the absurdist comedy dramas Swiss Army Man (2016) and Everything Everywhere All at Once (2022). The latter became A24's highest-grossing film and earned him several accolades, including Academy Awards for Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Original Screenplay.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Daniels (directors), licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.review:
É um crime só ter visto isto agora. Ter desistido da Netflix e passado para a HBO Max começa a mostrar sinais de positivismo e bom presságio.
E como começar esta crítica? Eu nem sei o que dizer… Porque tudo o que vi foi tão bem conseguido, não encontro defeito algum, e ficava horas a escrever sobre tudo o que há de genial neste filme. Tenho a certeza absoluta que um outro “eu”, de outro universo, talvez uma árvore, talvez uma pedra, assistiu a este filme com o maior prazer possível. Everything… Perfeito. Everywhere… terei sempre a mesma opinião. All at once… com todas as emoções assoberbadas num único momento: colado à televisão a assistir um grande filme.

films.name: Swiss Army Man
films.review:
This was so chaotic and fun... It honestly felt strange watching the man who defeated Voldemort fart his way through an hour and a half.  
But the truth is, beneath all the chaos and weirdness, the moral of the story hits surprisingly hard: the ending even made me shed a tear — I wasn’t expecting it to leave me so lost in thought.

Honestly, I'll never forget most of this movie, hilarious, funny and extremely introspective.

PS: I loved the holy bible joke.

films.name: The Death of Dick Long
films.review:
I’m not gonna lie — when I found out how Richard died, I burst out laughing… Now, should I have?
I guess that’s the whole point of the film: making you question how far comedy can really go.
It’s a solid film, though not without flaws — especially on the investigative/police side of things.
The soundtrack? Absolutely perfect. And I truly hope the use of Nickelback at the end was meant as an extra comedic touch.

David Fincher.name: David Fincher
David Fincher.bio:
David Andrew Leo Fincher (born August 28, 1962) is an American film director. His films, mostly thrillers, have received 40 nominations at the Academy Awards, including three for him as Best Director.

Born in Denver, Colorado, Fincher was interested in filmmaking at an early age. He directed numerous music videos, most notably Madonna's "Express Yourself" in 1989 and "Vogue" in 1990, both of which won him the MTV Video Music Award for Best Direction. He made his feature film debut with Alien 3 (1992), which garnered mixed reviews, followed by the thriller Seven (1995), which was better received. Fincher found lukewarm success with The Game (1997) and Fight Club (1999), but the latter eventually became a cult classic. In 2002, he returned to prominence with the thriller Panic Room starring Jodie Foster.

Fincher also directed Zodiac (2007), The Social Network (2010), The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo (2011), and Mank (2020). For The Social Network, he won the Golden Globe Award for Best Director and BAFTA Award for Best Direction. His biggest commercial successes are The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (2008) and Gone Girl (2014), both of which grossed more than $300 million worldwide, with the former earning thirteen nominations at the Academy Awards, and eleven at the British Academy Film Awards.

He also served as an executive producer and director for the Netflix series House of Cards (2013–2018) and Mindhunter (2017–2019), winning the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Drama Series for the pilot episode of House of Cards. Fincher was the co-founder of Propaganda Films, a film, and music.

films.name: Se7en
films.review: The 30th birthday. In IMAX. One of my favourite films… always leaves me in shock.

films.name: Zodiac
films.review: Oh well… I think you can see why I'm such a Fincher fanatic.

films.name: The Game
films.review:
Sem querer dar spoilers vou só dizer o seguinte: tudo aquilo que o David Fincher vos pode proporcionar num projeto está presente neste filme. Cinematografia arrepiante, imagens lindíssimas e plot twist que me deixou “para além de bom diretor és um grande cabrãozinho”. 
Andei o filme todo a tentar descobrir a manha, “Seven” fez me ser assim com filmes deste homem.

O que se pode dar a um homem rico quando ele já tem tudo? Eu sei a resposta, venham falar comigo depois de verem isto.

films.name: The Killer
films.review:
É mais um daqueles cenários em que me aparece algo adequado a uma boa sessão de cinema mas eventualmente me apercebo do inevitável. E engraçado usar este adjetivo porque concordar com críticos nunca foi a minha onda.
Considerar fracasso é algo bastante abusivo, sarcástico diria. Mas não fica longe de uma decepção daquelas fervorosas. Quem diria que alguém como o Fincher fosse fazer um filme com tão pouco sumo, e que me fizesse ligar tão pouco à personagem. 
Fassbender com uma prestação bastante sólida, não há muita coisa a apontar.
Mas se achas, Fincher, que só os psicopatas e só os assassinos maníacos atrás de vingança é que ouvem The Smiths, estás muito enganado. Sou apologista de boa música e não sou de grandes espetáculos. Apenas ouço e segrego tudo o que me soa nos ouvidos. Talvez devesses fazer uma melhor apropriação da mesma para soar melhor num filme e numa personagem.

David Leitch.name: David Leitch
David Leitch.bio:
David Leitch (/liːtʃ/; born November 16, 1975) is an American filmmaker, stunt performer, coordinator, and actor. He made his directorial debut in the action film John Wick (2014) with Chad Stahelski, though only Stahelski was credited. He later also directed the films Atomic Blonde (2017), Deadpool 2 (2018), Hobbs & Shaw (2019), Bullet Train (2022), and The Fall Guy (2024).

Description above from the Wikipedia article David Leitch, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: The Fall Guy
films.review:
Fez-me lembrar demais Deadpool, and I love it. 
Como sempre faria algumas mudanças mas foi extremamente divertido.

I want what they have. Is it too much to ask?😭😭

David Yates.name: David Yates
David Yates.bio:
David Yates (born 8 October 1963) is an English filmmaker who has directed feature films, short films, and television productions. He is best known for directing the final four films in the Harry Potter series and the three films of its prequel series, Fantastic Beasts. His work on the Harry Potter series brought him critical and commercial success, along with accolades such as the British Academy Britannia Award for Excellence in Directing.

Yates directed various short films and became a television director early in his career. His credits include the six-part political thriller State of Play (2003), for which he won the Directors Guild of Great Britain Award for Outstanding Directorial Achievement, the adult two-part documentary drama Sex Traffic (2004) and the Emmy Award-winning television film The Girl in the Café (2005).

Yates is a founding member of Directors UK and has had a close partnership with Warner Bros. as a director and producer. Around 2022, he began working through his own production company, Wychwood Media.

Description above from the Wikipedia article David Yates, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 2
Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince
Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows: Part 1
films.review:
It’s a comfort film for me… Even though I didn’t have the time this year to do a full marathon, I didn’t want to let Christmas go by without watching at least one film.

I’m genuinely excited about the series. The people closest to me know that I have a few issues with the films and the direction of almost all of them (except Azkaban), mainly because they fail to deliver even half of what the books offer - and that really bothers me.

Still, it remains a tradition in my household, and I always watch it with great pleasure. This one in particular is among the best, even though it falls short with some characters. Seeing Hermione cast “Obliviate” on the Death Eaters without us ever getting inside her head hurts. Seeing Bathilda Bagshot’s story completely cut hurts even more. But… it’s Harry Potter. It defined my childhood. And revisiting it always comes with a sense of nostalgia that leaves my heart feeling warm.

films.name: Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them
Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix
Fantastic Beasts: The Crimes of Grindelwald
Fantastic Beasts: The Secrets of Dumbledore

Denis Villeneuve.name: Denis Villeneuve
Denis Villeneuve.bio:
Denis Villeneuve (born October 3, 1967) is a Canadian filmmaker. He has received seven Canadian Screen Awards as well as nominations for three Academy Awards, five BAFTA Awards, and two Golden Globe Awards. Villeneuve's films have grossed more than $1.8 billion worldwide.

Villeneuve began his career in his home country, directing four French-language dramas: August 32nd on Earth (1998); Maelström (2000); Polytechnique (2009), a dramatisation of the 1989 École Polytechnique massacre; and Incendies (2010). The last of these gained him international prominence and earned a nomination for the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film. He expanded to English-language films by directing the thrillers Prisoners (2013), Enemy (2013), and Sicario (2015).

Villeneuve gained wider recognition for directing science fiction films. His work on Arrival (2016) earned him a nomination for the Academy Award for Best Director. This was followed by Blade Runner 2049 (2017), which was critically lauded but financially unsuccessful. His next projects were Dune (2021) and Dune: Part Two (2024), a two-part adaptation of Frank Herbert's novel of the same name. Both films were critically and commercially successful, with the former earning him Academy Award nominations for Best Adapted Screenplay and Best Picture.

films.name: Dune
films.review:
Preparing for the absolute PEAK.

I’m so excited, the best trilogy of all time? Debatable, but no one can deny the spectacular nature of these films. The landmark they represent.

Perfect.

films.name: Dune: Part Two
films.review:
Não consigo descrever o quão perfeito é este filme. Sim, eu já usei esta frase antes… E acreditem: tenho tentado não usar a palavra “perfeição” por ser forte demais para descrever algo. Mas aqui entramos noutro nível de cinematografia e de história. Edições lindas, passagens de tempo fenomenais, imagens de cortar a respiração, assuntos como fé, religião, esperança e guerra abordados de forma inimaginável… E a soundtrack… Posso soar, para aqueles que me conhecem, demasiado suspeito mas… Aquilo que o Zimmer fez com este filme tornou tudo ainda mais arrepiante. Parecia que quando entrava um som mais forte tudo à minha volta ficava ofuscado no sentido de me deixar ainda mais cativado, emerso na história.
A realidade é que esta obra é a real definição de cinema. Por tudo aquilo que representa, por tudo o que retrata… e por tudo o que foi feito atrás e à frente das câmaras.

films.name: Arrival
films.review:
After so long without seeing this...
How is it possible that I gave it 4 stars when I downloaded Letterboxd?

"You know I've had my head tilted up to the stars for as long as I can remember. You know what surprised me the most? It wasn't meeting them. It was meeting you."

This is the peak of cinema... This is what Martin Scorcese wants: absolute cinema.

films.name: Incendies
films.review:
I saw this the day before International Women's Day, and it shook me even more.

My only criticism is that it's a bit slow in some scenes.

Spoiler alert: The moment when the bus was set on fire... I had to stop, take it all in, and move on. I think I stood there for 5 minutes, not knowing where to look.

films.name: Next Floor
films.review:
What the hell did I just watch?
Huge criticism of human consumerism. It also has a humorous touch, I wish it were more explicit... As if the content wasn't already completely satirical. Yup, I'm very skeptical of comedies.

films.name: 120 Seconds to Get Elected
films.review:
“Poverty will be illegal”… 
Well, that took a quick turn. Tries to explain all the nonsense in political debates. I wanted to see more.

Eric Darnell.name: Eric Darnell
Eric Darnell.bio: Eric Darnell (born August 21, 1961) is an American animator, storyboard artist, director, writer, songwriter and occasional voice actor best known for the Madagascar saga. He now works for Baobab Studio.

Francis Ford Coppola.name: Francis Ford Coppola
Francis Ford Coppola.bio:
Francis Ford Coppola (born April 7, 1939) is an American filmmaker. He is widely acclaimed as one of Hollywood's most celebrated and influential film directors. He epitomized the group of filmmakers known as the New Hollywood, which included George Lucas, Martin Scorsese, Robert Altman, Woody Allen and William Friedkin, who emerged in the early 1970s with unconventional ideas that challenged contemporary filmmaking.

He co-authored the script for Patton, winning the Academy Award in 1970. His directorial fame escalated with the release of The Godfather in 1972. The film revolutionized movie-making in the gangster genre, garnering universal laurels from critics and public alike. It went on to win three Academy Awards, including his second, which he won for Best Adapted Screenplay, and it was instrumental in cementing his position as one of the prominent American film directors. Coppola followed it with an equally successful sequel The Godfather Part II, which became the first ever sequel to win the Academy Award for Best Picture. The film received yet higher praises than its predecessor, and gave him three Academy Awards—for Best Adapted Screenplay, Best Director and Best Picture. In the same year was released The Conversation, which he directed, produced and wrote. The film went on to win the Palme d'Or at the 1974 Cannes Film Festival. His next directorial venture was Apocalypse Now in 1979, and it was as notorious for its lengthy and troubled production as it was critically acclaimed for its vivid and stark depiction of the Vietnam War. It won his second Palme d'Or at the 1979 Cannes Film Festival.

Although some of Coppola's ventures in the 1980s and early 1990s were critically lauded, Coppola's later work has not met the same level of critical and commercial success as his '70s films.

films.name: Megalopolis
films.review:
Tive mesmo de dormir sobre o assunto… e perceber realmente o quão mau isto foi… 
Estão a ver o que é pensar REALMENTE que tudo isto foi feito no maior sarcasmo possível? Estive por momentos a considerar que seria o filme mais satírico que já vi. Mas falando a sério: não há um ponto a favor, não existe, foi tudo simplesmente terrível.

Francis Lawrence.name: Francis Lawrence
Francis Lawrence.bio:
Francis Lawrence (born March 26, 1971) is an American filmmaker and producer. After establishing himself as a director of music videos and commercials, Lawrence made his feature-length directorial debut with the superhero thriller Constantine (2005) and has since directed the post-apocalyptic horror film I Am Legend (2007), the romantic drama Water for Elephants (2011), four of the five films in The Hunger Games film series, and the spy thriller Red Sparrow (2018).

Description above from the Wikipedia article Francis Lawrence, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: The Long Walk
films.review:
I was a bit undecided about what rating to give this film. Throughout the hour and forty minutes, I found myself growing attached to the characters, but at the same time, I felt like this would be one of those projects you watch once and never return to.
The truth is, the ending is so predictable that, besides leaving me disappointed, it confirmed that earlier thought — I really won’t be watching it again.

Amazing actors? Yes. Interesting premise? Also yes.
Did they manage to shape it properly and give it a fitting ending? Absolutely not.
It felt like they just wanted to wrap things up quickly so everyone could go home — even if that wasn’t actually the case.

Gore Verbinski.name: Gore Verbinski
Gore Verbinski.bio: Gregor Justin "Gore" Verbinski (born March 16, 1964) is an American film director, screenwriter, and producer. He is best known for directing Mouse Hunt, The Ring, the first three Pirates of the Caribbean films, and Rango. For Rango, Verbinski won both the Academy Award and BAFTA Award for Best Animated Film.

films.name: Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl
Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End

Hayao Miyazaki.name: Hayao Miyazaki
Hayao Miyazaki.bio:
Hayao Miyazaki (Miyazaki Hayao, born January 5, 1941) is a Japanese manga artist and prominent film director and animator of many popular anime feature films. Through a career that has spanned nearly five decades, Miyazaki has attained international acclaim as a maker of animated feature films and, along with Isao Takahata, co-founded Studio Ghibli, an animation studio and production company. The success of Miyazaki's films has invited comparisons with American animator Walt Disney, British animator Nick Park as well as Robert Zemeckis, who pioneered Motion Capture animation, and he has been named one of the most influential people by Time Magazine.

Miyazaki began his career at Toei Animation as an in-between artist for Gulliver's Travels Beyond the Moon where he pitched his own ideas that eventually became the movie's ending. He continued to work in various roles in the animation industry over the decade until he was able to direct his first feature film Lupin III: The Castle of Cagliostro which was published in 1979. After the success of his next film, Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind, he co-founded Studio Ghibli where he continued to produce many feature films until Princess Mononoke whereafter he temporarily retired.

While Miyazaki's films have long enjoyed both commercial and critical success in Japan, he remained largely unknown to the West until Miramax released his 1997 film, Princess Mononoke. Princess Mononoke was the highest-grossing film in Japan—until it was eclipsed by another 1997 film, Titanic—and the first animated film to win Picture of the Year at the Japanese Academy Awards. Miyazaki returned to animation with Spirited Away. The film topped Titanic's sales at the Japanese box office, also won Picture of the Year at the Japanese Academy Awards and was the first anime film to win an American Academy Award.

Miyazaki's films often incorporate recurrent themes, such as humanity's relationship to nature and technology, and the difficulty of maintaining a pacifist ethic. Reflecting Miyazaki's feminism, the protagonists of his films are often strong, independent girls or young women. Miyazaki is a vocal critic of capitalism and globalization. While two of his films, The Castle of Cagliostro and Castle in the Sky, involve traditional villains, his other films such as Nausicaa or Princess Mononoke present morally ambiguous antagonists with redeeming qualities.

films.name: Porco Rosso
films.review:
"I'd rather be a pig than a fascist"

The best Miyazaki film I've seen so far, and without a doubt one of Studio Ghibli's finest. What a wonderful film to experience - not only for its animation and story, but for the entire philosophy behind the pig and the post–First World War setting.
What impressed me the most was how simply the main character is introduced: few words, no over-explaining, and yet an entire life and a quiet tragedy are immediately felt. The "curse" of his pig form is presented with such restraint that it becomes even more powerful - a symbol that speaks for itself without ever needing to be spelled out.
It's fun, genuinely funny, with BRILLIANT touches of classic romanticism in the main character, and it carries the best message I could ever ask for in a film: FASCISM NEVER AGAIN, or, in Portuguese, FASCISMO NUNCA MAIS.

films.name: Spirited Away
films.review:
Yes, it's a mistake to have only seen this now.
As I write this, I realize more and more how beautiful this film truly is — completely immersive, layered with multiple critiques of society, whether represented through pigs or gold.  
The soundtrack is perfect; I feel like I'll be listening to it obsessively for the next few months.  

And I feel like this won’t be my final rating, so don’t be upset with the 4.5 stars.

films.name: My Neighbor Totoro
films.review:
A project so simple, yet with a magic that never goes unnoticed - capable of enchanting even adults like me.
Beautiful from start to finish. Studio Ghibli’s animations have never failed me: it’s always time wonderfully spent.
I’m amazed by the love they pour into their drawings, and even more amazed by how clearly that love shines through on screen.

films.name: Ponyo
films.review: Not the best Studio Ghibli project, but I can’t help but praise the incredible and sensational creativity Miyazaki has to craft something of this kind. A wonderful story and an animation made with love and care culminate in a production rich with beautiful messages for younger audiences. It’s truly a pleasure to dive deeper into these films, and they only make me want to see them all… Ponyo wanted to become human, but only love could make it happen — such a beautiful moral, one that will stay with me.

watchlist.name: Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind

James Cameron.name: James Cameron
James Cameron.bio:
James Francis Cameron (born August 16, 1954) is a Canadian filmmaker. He is a major figure in the post-New Hollywood era and often uses novel technologies with a classical filmmaking style. Cameron first gained recognition for writing and directing The Terminator (1984), and found further success with Aliens (1986), The Abyss (1989), Terminator 2: Judgment Day (1991), and True Lies (1994), as well as Avatar (2009) and its sequels. He directed, wrote, co-produced, and co-edited Titanic (1997), winning Academy Awards for Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Film Editing. He is a recipient of various other industry accolades, and three of his films have been selected for preservation in the National Film Registry by the Library of Congress.

Cameron co-founded the production companies Lightstorm Entertainment, Digital Domain, and Earthship Productions. In addition to filmmaking, he is a National Geographic explorer-in-residence. He has produced many documentaries on deep-ocean exploration, including Ghosts of the Abyss (2003) and Aliens of the Deep (2005). Cameron has also contributed to underwater filming and remote vehicle technologies and helped create the digital 3D Fusion Camera System. In 2012, Cameron became the first person to solo descend the Mariana Trench, the deepest part of the Earth's ocean, in the Deepsea Challenger submersible.

Cameron's films have grossed over $8 billion worldwide, making him the second-highest-grossing film director. Three of Cameron's films are among the top four highest-grossing films of all time; Avatar (2009), Avatar: The Way of Water (2022) and Titanic (1997) are the highest, third-highest and fourth-highest-grossing films of all time, respectively. Cameron directed the first film to gross over $1 billion, the first two films to gross over $2 billion, and is the only director to have had three films grossing over $2 billion. In 2010, Time named Cameron one of the 100 most influential people in the world. Cameron is also an environmentalist and runs several sustainability businesses.

Description above from the Wikipedia article James Cameron, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Superman
films.review:
Another James Gunn-style movie that, in my opinion, never gets boring or tiring… I FINALLY saw Superman. I FINALLY saw a man who’s vulnerable and genuinely concerned about EVERYTHING around him. They finally nailed the personality and complex character that is Clark Kent — aka the most powerful meta-human of all.

A cinematography that does justice to everything good about comic books, with incredible details both in camera work and in Clark’s relationships with the other characters. I’m excited to see this new DCU phase — please don’t ruin what is, to me, an almost perfect start.

PS: I compared this film with every superhero movie ever made, that’s why I gave this rating. It’s not the perfect movie and definitely not one of the best ever made.

James Wan.name: James Wan
James Wan.bio:
James Wan (born 26 February 1977) is an Australian filmmaker. He has primarily worked in the horror genre as the co-creator of the Saw and Insidious franchises and the creator of The Conjuring Universe. The lattermost is the highest-grossing horror franchise at over $2 billion. Wan is also the founder of the film and television production company Atomic Monster.

Beginning his career with the Saw franchise, Wan made his feature directorial debut with his first film in 2004. The franchise became commercially successful and grossed more than $1 billion globally. Following a period of setbacks, Wan found new success with the Insidious series, in which he directed the first film in 2010 and its 2013 sequel. The same year as the second Insidious, Wan directed the first Conjuring film to critical and commercial success. He served as the director of the second installment in 2016 and produced subsequent films in the franchise.

Outside of horror, Wan directed Furious 7 (2015), the seventh installment of the Fast & Furious franchise, and the DC Extended Universe superhero films Aquaman (2018) and its sequel Aquaman and the Lost Kingdom (2023). Both Furious 7 and Aquaman grossed over $1 billion, making Wan the eighth director with two films to reach the milestone. He is the 16th highest-grossing director of all time as of 2021, with his films having grossed over $3.7 billion worldwide.

Description above from the Wikipedia article James Wan, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

Jaume Collet-Serra.name: Jaume Collet-Serra
Jaume Collet-Serra.bio: Jaume Collet-Serra (born March 23, 1974) is a Spanish filmmaker. He is best known for directing the films House of Wax (2005), Orphan (2009), Unknown (2011), The Shallows (2016), Jungle Cruise (2021) and Black Adam (2022). He is the co-founder of Ombra Films.
Jaume Collet-Serra.birthplace: Sant Iscle de Vallalta, Barcelona, Spain

Joachim Trier.name: Joachim Trier
Joachim Trier.bio: Joachim Trier (born 1 March 1974) is a Danish-born Norwegian filmmaker. He is best known for his Oslo trilogy which comprises the films Reprise (2006), Oslo, August 31st (2011), The Worst Person in the World (2021). For the latter film, he was nominated for the Best Original Screenplay at the 94th Academy Awards, with the film also nominated for Best International Feature. He is also known for directing Louder Than Bombs (2015), Thelma (2017), and the documentary The Other Munch (2018). In addition to an Academy Award, he has also been nominated for a BAFTA Award, two Cesar Awards, and three Cannes Film Festival Awards.

films.name: Sentimental Value
films.review:
They called me a masochist for watching this film in the cinema twice within a week… they’ll probably call me the same again for having watched it a third time in such a short period of time, and honestly? I don’t care.

It continues, and will continue, to be impossible to describe the importance this project has in my life. It may sound strange, but the truth is that I will never forget the first time I watched this, alone, on the big screen. I’ll never forget the fear I felt when I walked into the theater with extremely high expectations, without even having watched a trailer or knowing what the film was about (on purpose, because of the impact and surprise it had on audiences).

What’s even more interesting is catching details that I missed the first times, like the monologue delivered by Elle Fanning and Stellan’s gaze, which says more than a thousand-page script ever could.

As I said in my previous reviews, it’s simply a perfect film, kept in a special corner of my heart for everything it represents, for everything it tells through dialogue and through distant, silent looks. A project that marked me for life.

“It's like her sadness is such an overwhelming part of her.”

films.name: Pieta
films.review:
“I sometimes have memories of things that could never have happened. Maybe they belong to somebody else.”

I’m genuinely not okay… was this really Joachim Trier’s first project? What an absolute banger. So much suffering, such radical emotional shifts, such a raw depiction of loss…

Joachim would later revisit these themes throughout his career, and honestly, I know very few directors capable of expressing this level of emotion in stories like these. I was going to say that this short film explores the deepest sides of loneliness, suicide, aging, and memory, but the truth is that all of Trier’s projects approach these subjects in such a raw and beautiful way…

films.name: The Worst Person in the World
films.review:
Never has a director made me feel so completely desolate and emotionally shaken. I’ve been thinking for a long time about what I’ve just watched, and the truth is: I don’t even know where to begin.

This is yet another film that challenges genres and tries to explore the complexity of the human mind in the rawest way possible. I know many people don’t like that approach, and that’s a fair opinion, but I’m definitely not part of that group. Quite the opposite.

I feel that if I start going too deep into this project, everything might collapse inside me psychologically, and I already feel fragile. So I’ll try to stay afloat and talk about it in broader terms. Once again, I connected very easily with the main character, perhaps because of everything she goes through across those two intense hours. It’s a clear portrayal of the disappearance of one’s own identity, where everything feels either missing or unbearably dull, even the act of simply being.

It’s a film that pushes the boundaries of what is considered “normal” for a woman in her 30s, depicting a crisis that fits perfectly into the kind of anxiety and stress so many of us young people experience day after day.

Renate Reinsve continues to rise higher and higher in my estimation. She is a phenomenal actress, chilling at times, and she gives even more weight to everything we see simply by being on screen.

I’ll end by saying that I’m absolutely loving discovering Joachim Trier’s filmography, and I’m increasingly committed to exploring all of his work.

films.name: Oslo, August 31st
films.review:
“I just want you to understand. If that’s how it ends, it’s a choice I’ve made.”

I swear, this man leaves me completely devastated, speechless, thinking about his films for hours on end. It’s incredible how raw a film can be, how powerful it can become by being purely human, simple, and truthful. I usually gravitate toward something more dramatic: more adrenaline, more horror, more psychedelia. But Joachim has brought me calmness and depth in storytelling, and discovering this side of cinema is making me completely captivated by Scandinavian filmmaking.

Beyond the raw and deeply human stories (so human they can feel almost desolating), what has truly held me hostage are the lead actors who speak to us through their eyes. This project had a lot of dialogue, especially at the beginning, giving us detailed context about Anders’ situation. But halfway through the film, he was communicating with us without saying a word.

I noticed Anders’ brilliance in “The Worst Person in the World (2021)”, even in a supporting role, but seeing him carry a film as the protagonist only confirmed what I already felt back then: extraordinary, from beginning to end. Whether in the way he delivers his lines or in the way his gaze drifts toward places unknown to us, yet makes us uneasy, as if we’re desperate to understand what his character is thinking.

Joachim is rising higher and higher in my personal ranking of directors. And soon enough, he’ll be among my very favorites: fully deserved.

films.name: Still
films.review:
One of the reasons I love watching early career projects (short films) of my favorite filmmakers is because they open my eyes to everything a director went through while evolving and discovering more about their own cinematic identity. This short makes it very clear that Joachim Trier was still struggling to find himself… and I don’t mean that as a negative criticism at all. I loved how deliberately confusing it was, with extreme shifts in camera work. Those abrupt stylistic changes are exactly what make this project feel even more unique, even more interesting.

In just 20 minutes, Joachim manages to tell an incredibly touching story filled with fascinating touches of Nordic cinema.

God, Trier… I love you man. You keep surprising me so much.

films.name: Louder Than Bombs
films.review:
"There are days I'm invisible, I can do whatever I want. I must be careful not to lose that ability." - Conrad

(I’m leaving a few quotes that resonated with me at the top and bottom of this review...)

It feels like reading poetry late in the afternoon, watching the sunset over the mountains with a view that is... somehow deeply nostalgic. That is the best way I can describe what it felt like to watch this movie.

To the TikTokers and consumers of short, algorithm-processed content, I’ll say this: don’t watch it. Nothing will sink in, and you’ll just end up quitting halfway through. But to those who want to experience the very best that cinema has to offer... believe me, it’s worth it. Not just for the story itself, but for how they chose to tell it. Yes, it has a few under-explored subplots, but that was precisely the point: the emptiness of something (or someone) they lost.

The direction is rock-solid, featuring performances that will give you goosebumps down to your fingertips, and moments that will stay with me for a very long time. Moments where I felt... represented? Maybe that’s not the perfect word, but seeing Conrad go through all of that while dreaming of a girl he "can't have". I didn't have a similar family experience, but I’ve had times when I didn't believe in myself, and that doubt consumed my dreams. It consumed the plans I made, not just with my friends, but with my family. A bedroom that mirrored who I was, a passion for writing that haunted me (and still does), and a "dream girl who lives only in my dreams and nowhere else".

Joachim and Eskil, this won’t be the last time I sing your praises. Delivering a story that is so simple yet so complex is hard... incredibly hard. Thank you.

Ps: I'm still crying over that scene... Yes... That look...

"He could still, many years from now, recall the scene in all its detail. The lock of hair she placed behind her ear. The way the washing label stuck out from the neck of her tanktop. The streetlights that went out as they passed Kevin Anderson's house. That strangely familiar smell of damp earth that he couldn't quite place." - Melanie

films.name: Procter
films.review:
Honestly, I don’t know what to think about this short... The truth is, I wasn’t expecting something so dark and meticulous from Trier. I went into it blind without reading the premise, and I’m so glad I did... because I was expecting something much more sentimental and ended up with an incredibly unsettling story.

Curry Barker is right: YouTube is a massive gateway for discovering the work of up-and-coming creators, or even exploring the past projects of our favorite directors. To the person who uploaded this short with English subtitles: Thank you.

Joanna Hogg.name: Joanna Hogg
Joanna Hogg.bio:
Joanna Hogg (born 20 March 1960) is a British film director and screenwriter. She made her directorial and screenwriting feature film debut in 2007 with Unrelated followed by Archipelago (2010), Exhibition (2013), The Souvenir (2019), The Souvenir Part II (2021), and The Eternal Daughter (2022). Two of her films topped the Sight & Sound annual poll for best film in their respective years, receiving nominations at the British Independent Film Awards, the Independent Spirit Awards and at the Berlin International Film Festival.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Joanna Hogg, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia​.

films.name: The Eternal Daughter
films.review:
The project is filled with symbolism, which I honestly find fascinating: the lack of connection (a phone with poor signal), the old woman appearing at the window (Julie’s fear of her mother’s aging), and the haunted hotel itself (fears looming over Julie’s head). I used the word "fear" quite a lot because it perfectly expresses what the main character felt... But I can’t forget what I felt watching this movie: bored at the start, intrigued by the end. It had everything to be a great film — those first 40/50 minutes are what dragged things down a bit.

I read a comment here on Letterboxd that said, "This would make an excellent short film." If they made something with this story in 30 minutes, I’d give it 5 stars... Easily.

films.name: The Souvenir
films.review:
Definitely not my favorite kind of film... I found it a bit dull at times, but I get why so many people love it: it’s a well-structured story with very well-developed characters. The actors were absolutely chilling — and I have to single out Honor, what a phenomenal actress.
Am I interested in seeing the second part? Yes and no... On one hand, I want to know what happens with Julie, but on the other, I keep thinking about the extremely plain cinematography, which lacked any real spark. Still, I have to watch it.

films.name: The Souvenir: Part II
films.review:
Well, it really feels like I watched a completely different movie than everyone else... I don’t think this is better than the first one — which already says a lot.
I found the premise barely interesting, with a deliberately slow pace that nearly put me to sleep.

Joe Johnston.name: Joe Johnston
Joe Johnston.bio:
Joseph Eggleston Johnston II (born May 13, 1950) is an American film director, producer, writer, and visual effects artist. He is best known for directing effects-driven films, including Honey, I Shrunk the Kids (1989), The Rocketeer (1991), Jumanji (1995), Jurassic Park III (2001), The Wolfman (2010), and Captain America: The First Avenger (2011).

Description above from the Wikipedia article Joe Johnston, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

Joe Russo.name: Joe Russo
Joe Russo.bio:
Joe Russo (born July 18, 1971) is an American filmmaker with his brother, Anthony Russo, collectively known as the Russo brothers (/ˈruːsoʊ/ ROO-soh). They direct most of their work together. They are best known for directing four films in the Marvel Cinematic Universe (MCU): Captain America: The Winter Soldier (2014), Captain America: Civil War(2016), Avengers: Infinity War (2018), and Avengers: Endgame (2019). Endgame grossed over $2.798 billion worldwide, briefly becoming the highest-grossing film ever. 

The brothers have also worked as directors and producers on the comedy series Arrested Development (2003–2005), Community (2009–2014), and Happy Endings (2011–2012). They won a Primetime Emmy Award for Arrested Development.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Russo brothers, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Ricky Gervais: Mortality
films.review:
I don’t think this is his best material, and I actually think it’s far from it. This rating ends up being a comparison with all the specials I’ve watched over the past few months, even years: they’re all incredibly “safe” politically, and it gets a bit boring to hear jokes that never stray beyond the usual boundaries of what’s considered acceptable.

That’s exactly why I like this guy. He isn’t afraid to go straight for dark jokes, with that sharp British sarcasm that, even if it feels like a continuation of his older material, still makes me laugh uncontrollably.

I get tired of hearing that there are limits to humor, which is precisely why I enjoy when he pushes back against that idea, even if he does it repeatedly. Some people say it gets tiring… I also find it tiring when someone gets canceled over a JOKE that stepped outside the so-called “normal.” It reaches a point where, beyond being exhausted from trying to explain that it’s just a JOKE, I start feeling genuinely uncomfortable with modern society’s inability to take anything as a comedic moment or a form of satirical relief.

If it hits too close to home and people get sensitive? Get some perspective and laugh, because life is life, and we’re all going to die anyway.

Jon Favreau.name: Jon Favreau
Jon Favreau.bio:
Jonathan Kolia Favreau (/ˈfævroʊ/ FAV-roh; born October 19, 1966) is an American actor and filmmaker. He has received numerous accolades including an Emmy Award and two Saturn Awards. As an actor, Favreau has appeared in many films such as Rudy (1993), PCU (1994), Swingers (1996), Very Bad Things (1998), Deep Impact (1998), The Replacements (2000), Daredevil (2003), Wimbledon (2004), The Break-Up (2006), Four Christmases (2008), Couples Retreat (2009), I Love You, Man (2009), People Like Us (2012), The Wolf of Wall Street (2013), and Chef (2014).

As a filmmaker, Favreau has been significantly involved with the Marvel Cinematic Universe. He directed, produced, and appeared as Happy Hogan in the films Iron Man (2008) and Iron Man 2 (2010). He also served as an executive producer or appeared as the character in the films The Avengers (2012), Iron Man 3 (2013), Avengers: Age of Ultron (2015), Spider-Man: Homecoming (2017), Avengers: Infinity War (2018), Avengers: Endgame (2019), Spider-Man: Far From Home (2019), Spider-Man: No Way Home (2021), and Deadpool & Wolverine (2024).

He has also directed the films Elf (2003), Zathura: A Space Adventure (2005), Cowboys & Aliens (2011), Chef (2014), The Jungle Book (2016), The Lion King (2019), and The Mandalorian and Grogu (2026). Favreau has been known for his work on the Star Wars franchise with Dave Filoni, creating the Disney+ original series The Mandalorian (2019–2023), which Filoni helped develop, with both serving as executive producers. Alongside Filoni, he serves as an executive producer on all of the show's spin-off series, including The Book of Boba Fett, Ahsoka, and Skeleton Crew. He produces films under his production company banner, Fairview Entertainment, and also presented the variety series Dinner for Five and the cooking series The Chef Show.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Jon Favreau, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.
Jon Favreau.birthplace: Queens, New York City, New York, USA

Jon Turteltaub.name: Jon Turteltaub
Jon Turteltaub.bio:
Jonathan Charles "Jon" Turteltaub is an American film director and producer. He is a graduate of Wesleyan University and the USC School of Cinematic Arts. He is the son of television comedy writer Saul Turteltaub.

He has directed several successful mainstream films for the Walt Disney Studios, including; 3 Ninjas (1992), Cool Runnings (1993), While You Were Sleeping (1995), Phenomenon (1996), Instinct (1999), Disney's The Kid (2000), 2004's National Treasure, as well as its 2007 sequel National Treasure: Book of Secrets, and The Sorcerer's Apprentice.

Turteltaub produced the CBS television series, Jericho, and also directed the first three episodes, "Pilot", "Fallout", and "Four Horsemen".
Jon Turteltaub.birthplace: New York City, New York, USA

Jon Watts.name: Jon Watts
Jon Watts.bio:
Jonathan Watts (born June 28, 1981) is an American film director, producer, and screenwriter. He is best known for directing the Spider-Man films within the Marvel Cinematic Universe (MCU): Homecoming (2017), Far From Home (2019), and No Way Home (2021). He also directed and co-wrote the films Clown (2014), Cop Car (2015), and Wolfs (2024), as well as directing many episodes of the parody television news series Onion News Network. Watts has also directed music videos for electronic music artists such as Fatboy Slim and Swedish House Mafia.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Jon Watts, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Wolfs
films.review:
Atores de peso. Cinematografia decente, mas... Pouca ação. Pouca comédia. Discursos secantes. Cenas desnecessárias.
Um pouco de tudo na realidade. Não me conseguiu satisfazer nem um pouco, cheguei a meio do filme a querer que acabasse... Dou 2 estrelas porque é um bom conceito, as personagens são bem conseguidas e o final era o que se esperava. 
Clooney e Pitt, eu gosto imenso de vocês, estiveram bem para aquilo que foi pedido. Tentem só não fazer mais cenas deste género.

Josh Safdie.name: Josh Safdie
Josh Safdie.bio:
Joshua Henry Safdie (born April 3, 1984) is an American filmmaker and screenwriter, best known for his work with his younger brother, Benny Safdie, as one half of the acclaimed Safdie Brothers. Together, they developed a reputation for raw, urgent storytelling in films such as Heaven Knows What (2014), Good Time (2017), and Uncut Gems (2019).

In 2024, it was reported that the Safdie Brothers would no longer co-direct and would instead pursue solo projects. Josh's first feature since the split, Marty Supreme (2025), was critically acclaimed, earning him nine Oscar nominations, including best picture and best director.
Josh Safdie.birthplace: New York City, New York, USA

films.name: Marty Supreme
films.review:
🏆 THE 98TH OSCARS PREPARATIONS 🏆

More than 60 minutes have passed since I finished watching this film. I got home and I’m now listening to the sound of rain falling while trying to calm myself down, if that’s even possible. Hardly.

I think everyone close to me knows that the Safdie Brothers have been climbing higher and higher in my personal ranking. 2025/2026 was no exception. Did I have extremely high expectations and was I scared because of that? Absolutely. My heart was racing and the film hadn’t even started yet. The moment I saw that very Safdie-esque transition at the beginning, I immediately realized the importance and the influence this project could have on the future of cinema - whether in editing, performances, cinematography, or even marketing campaigns. The ability Josh and the rest of the team (from producers to marketing directors) had to bring so many people into theaters to watch a film where the main character is obsessed with playing table tennis… I honestly have no words.

Give Chalamet the Oscar. What an actor, what a beast. He’s reached a level where he can completely erase every role he’s done or is currently doing from your memory and introduce a brand-new embodiment: one capable of putting us through stress, anxiety, and a complete inability to distinguish reality from fiction.

Once again, we find ourselves rooting for a narcissistic character who only cares about himself… where have I seen this before?

A modern classic. And my pick for Film of the Year 2025. I still haven’t seen Hamnet or Sentimental Value, but I honestly find it impossible for this opinion to change.

films.name: Uncut Gems
films.review:
Is it strange to say that I had forgotten just how insane the final 40 minutes of this film are? Maybe. But at the same time, it feels natural, because I can’t remember any other film capable of making me feel this way - in such a dirty, relentless manner. Adam Sandler is so charismatic that you can’t help but root for his character until the very end, which makes everything more gripping and much harder to digest once it’s over.
Everything in this film is, in my view, executed extremely well, and the direction does almost all the magic - whether through the camerawork or the near-psychotic, stress-inducing editing. It’s a modern classic, and revisiting it only reinforces my stance on the film and further solidifies everything I have to say about this feature and about the Safdie brothers.

films.name: Good Time
films.review: "Beau is Afraid" but indie style, with less steroids, and in my opinion: much better.

films.name: Heaven Knows What
films.review:
Another film from the Safdie brothers — and believe me when I say I keep getting more and more amazed by the quality they showed early on in their international independent work.
A film with a “simple” premise managed to transport me into the darker side of New York, filled with social struggles and substance abuse — a project that exposes the city’s decay in an almost documentary-like way, portraying a painful path that’s incredibly hard to escape.

I’m also blown away by the performances and by the way they chose to tell the story. I honestly thought I’d find it boring, but that hour and a half just flew by.

Protect them. I’ve heard that "The Smashing Machine (2025)" isn’t selling well, and that really worries me. I just hope everything goes right for them.

films.name: Daddy Longlegs
films.review:
Still preparing to dive into their solo films.

The first serious project by the Safdie brothers manages to offer plenty of positives: a production that, no matter how simple it may seem, touches the soul — almost in a documentary-like format — and a story that maintains its sensibility, strength, and emotion from beginning to end.
I started watching the feature film without knowing it was inspired by the Safdie brothers’ real experiences with their father. But as I said, the production itself hinted that there was something genuine behind those scenes.
Deeply moving — at its core, it’s a portrait filled with both love and pain. The Safdies don’t paint the father as a villain, nor do they romanticize him. They show him as someone who loves his children deeply but is incapable of taking care of them in a stable way — a bittersweet blend of tenderness and irresponsibility.
Very solid for a first film, and an incredible tribute to their childhood.

films.name: Goldman v Silverman
films.review:
I’m mentally preparing myself for two upcoming Safdie brothers films, because I really don’t want either of them to miss. “Uncut Gems” and “Good Time” are easily two of the best films of the 21st century — and honestly, they’ve proudly made it into my list of favorite A24 projects, and favorite films ever. Seeing the two brothers going their separate creative ways genuinely scares me. Protect them at all costs.

This short film captures perfectly the struggle of a street performer trying to showcase his art. A sharp critique of society, of the absurd “designated performance zones” in major cities (not just in New York — I see plenty of that here in Portugal, in Porto), and of the humiliation and mockery that exist even within the profession itself. Seven minutes that fly by naturally — simple, yet incredibly powerful.

films.name: Toback Vs. Mailer: The Incident
films.review:
Why did I watch this?

Picture this: I’m tired, I’ve spent the whole day studying and working on projects, it’s already late, and I’m not about to start a full-length film now… So I decided to stick with something short. I wanted to watch "Two Ships (2025)", but I couldn’t find it anywhere, so I went digging through the filmography of directors I love, and ended up finding this Safdie short.

Are there positives? Sure. You can very easily tell that James Toback and Norman Mailer are complete pieces of shit (trust me, it’s good to know that). At the same time, the way the café scenes blend with the animated segments was brilliant, and the animation itself is genuinely fun, well made, with a style that feels very different from anything I’ve seen before.

It killed some time, and honestly, I’m glad I didn’t end up on TikTok just to fall asleep, because at least I watched something… educational:

GAMBLING GAMBLING GAMBLING.
ALCOHOL ALCOHOL ALCOHOL.

Justin Lin.name: Justin Lin
Justin Lin.bio:
Justin Lin (traditional Chinese: 林詣彬; simplified Chinese: 林诣彬; pinyin: Lín Yìbīn; Pe̍h-ōe-jī: Lîm Gē-pin, born October 11, 1971) is a Taiwanese-American film and television director, producer, and screenwriter. His films have grossed over $3 billion USD worldwide as of March 2017. He is best known for his directorial work on Better Luck Tomorrow (2002), the Fast & Furious franchise from The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift (2006) to Fast & Furious 6 (2013) and F9 (2021), and Star Trek Beyond (2016). He is also known for his work on television programs like Community and True Detective.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Justin Lin, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift

Kristoffer Borgli.name: Kristoffer Borgli
Kristoffer Borgli.bio: Kristoffer Borgli (born August 9, 1985) is a Norwegian director and screenwriter based in the United States. He is best known for Sick of Myself (2022), Dream Scenario (2023), and The Drama (2026).

films.name: Dream Scenario
films.review:
While watching this project, I kept thinking, "This is exactly the kind of movie Ari Aster would make, from the editing to the script itself"... When the credits started rolling, I read "Produced by Ari Aster," and my jaw dropped: one of the best plot twists ever. The man is haunting me... Or am I the one haunting him? Either way, great movie.

Edit: Another one from A24, let’s keep the momentum going!

films.name: Former Cult Member Hears Music for the First Time
films.review:
I think I went through the same thing when I heard Geese for the first time.
Did I hit my head on a table? No, but I did feel like screaming from all the different sounds I was hearing (Cameron Winter, you magnificent beast).

Jokes aside... an EXTREMELY IMPORTANT message.

films.name: Sick of Myself
films.review:
This kind of feeling - where the pleasure of watching a project blends with the discomfort of being fully aware of everything I just witnessed - has only happened with a few directors during my journey through the world of cinema: Robert Eggers with his fantastical and gothic worlds, Ari Aster with his incredibly satirical, fertile, and challenging imagination, and… Kristoffer Borgli, who keeps winning my heart as I explore more of his short and feature-length work.
A director who challenges the very way a moral should unfold within a story, someone unafraid of delivering the purest message in an eccentric, uncomfortable, yet entertaining, didactic, and provocative manner.

"Sick of Myself" brings an IMPORTANT message about the idealization of people on social media and the obsessive hunger for attention and existential jealousy. Modern narcissism, shattered identity, and a sexual tension that, beyond awkward, escalated into extreme levels of horror I never expected to see on screen.
An ending that demands the viewer to imagine their own closure, set within a narrative where identity is pushed so far into question that you lose track of what is real, what is imagined, and what is exaggerated.

films.name: The Loser
films.review:
It’s truly a privilege to watch Kristoffer Borgli’s work.

I just watched a short film that has more substance than many feature-length films released throughout the year, and that alone says a lot about my experience in just a few minutes. Being able to see such a well-defined “character” in so little time is extraordinary.

The editing is IMPECCABLE. It adds comedic moments and allows us to escape the cringe just enough: they never drag, they hit at exactly the right moment, cutting to another scene or image so we can catch our breath, even if only briefly.

MAN I LOVE NORWEGIAN FILMMAKERS (AND MAN I LOVE KRISTOFFER BORGLI).

films.name: The Drama
films.review:
Kristoffer Borgli, you keep surprising me and climbing higher and higher in my personal ranking.
I genuinely don’t understand some of the negative opinions… sure, everyone has their own taste, but you can’t walk into a cinema expecting a rom-com just because the story revolves around a couple. Especially when we’re talking about Borgli, an extraordinary mind, delivering sharp social commentary in his films, chaotic editing that somehow holds together an avalanche of emotions, and camera work that keeps getting more refined.
Borgli expands his eccentricity almost like a funnel, but in reverse: he starts with something intimate and calm, builds a connection with the characters, and then gradually increases the suspense, the tension, and those awkward moments until you reach a point where you don’t even know if you should laugh, cry, or just look away from the screen because of how uncomfortable everything feels. And that’s exactly why I wanted to see this in a cinema: until now, I hadn’t had the chance to experience one of his films on the big screen.
A24 really has some brilliant minds in their hands, people capable of creating projects that stay with you for a long time. And this one even had Ari Aster as a producer (I swear, those two must love working together).

films.name: Whateverest
films.review:
Even though this is a mockumentary, I genuinely need to talk about something that was present throughout those 15 minutes of film…
Love for music can appear in the strangest possible ways, but the moment it enters your life, it stays with you forever. Even if you do stupid things or stray from what's considered "normal", what truly matters is that the love remains - always there - whenever you put your headphones on or let a vinyl spin in a room filled with more memories than your own head.

That's exactly how I feel about music. I'm just like Todd: eccentric, constantly thinking about music, unable to get it out of my head. I've always imagined myself in that world, performing or doing something with my voice.

Being addicted to music is probably the best drug one can take, and I'm glad I'm on the addicts' side. I want to keep feeling it, living it, and breathing music until the end of my days.

films.name: Softcore
films.review:
A short film that shows just how ridiculous blind dates can be and how unbearably cringe outings become when the connection between two people doesn't spark in the very first moments.

Who in their right mind invites someone over to their place on a first date, without even knowing the other person's musical tastes? An incredibly ironic question - perfectly suited to describe a short film that is astonishingly detailed, with surprising, almost horror-like camerawork, sprinkled with romantic-comedic touches that made me laugh and feel scared at the same time.

films.name: Willem Dafoe
films.review: YES, I NEED TO KNOW THE NAME OF THE ACTOR WITHOUT HAVING TO SEARCH IT IN AN AI OR ON GOOGLE. YES, it does my ego good.

films.name: Eer
films.review:
If I had an ear like that? I'd still keep listening to music on the beach, during that sunset, and if necessary, I'd even stand right next to that guy's wife. I'd stay glued to them just so they could witness the bloody collapse happening inside my eardrum.

It's a work that carries many of the psychological and moral elements of "Sick of Myself", and it already shows how much I liked what I saw. "Liked" is a strong word here, because the goal is to leave you completely uncomfortable while watching this. What's funniest is how you end up focusing on the strangest things in each scene, which makes everything feel even more immersive.

films.name: It's Not a Phase
films.review: Excuse me? I actually feel sorry for you, don't tell me not to.

films.name: A Place We Call Reality
films.review:
There’s one truth behind every Borgli project: he’s weird, and I’m absolutely here for it.

At the same time, I can’t help but feel that this short doesn’t quite do justice to his beautifully twisted mind. We’re talking about a complete satire of reality and imagination - about how the two coexist simultaneously, about the toxicity of a society trapped in its own version of the Matrix. It attempts to expand on themes like narcissism, emotional fragility, fear of acceptance, and an obsessive critique of cultural norms.

The problem? It feels like too much for something this short.

I genuinely loved the concept, the themes, and the completely unhinged yet disturbingly hilarious approach. But it ends up feeling rushed. The soundtrack is overbearing at times, and the film doesn’t quite manage to bring its ideas together into a cohesive moral statement. Even if the intention was to leave everything open-ended, I’m not entirely convinced that was the strongest choice.

Kristoffer Borgli, remember the name. I’m incredibly curious to see where his cinema goes next. Very excited to see "The Drama (2026)".

films.name: Syndromes
films.review:
The idea isn’t bad, and the direction is definitely on a solid level. I mean, we’re talking about Kristoffer Borgli, of course the editing and direction were going to be good.

Anyway… I’m going to have trauma from this new X-Men.

films.name: Real Life Exp.
films.review:
I’m not sure what to say about this short… I’m a bit lost when it comes to the message Borgli was trying to convey, and that’s why my rating doesn’t go beyond 2.5 stars. It doesn’t make good use of the camera, nor does it make the characters truly interesting. I know that ten minutes isn’t a lot of time, but it could still have offered some background to help us connect with them.

Instead, we just watch two girls dying of thirst, with no real concern about leaving that place. That’s it.

Lana Wachowski.name: Lana Wachowski
Lana Wachowski.bio: Lana Wachowski (born June 21, 1965) is known as one of the Wachowski sisters — a directing, writing and producing duo most famous for creating The Matrix series.

Larry Charles.name: Larry Charles
Larry Charles.bio:
Larry Charles (born February 20, 1956) is an American writer, director, and producer. He is best known as a staff writer for the American sitcom Seinfeld for its first 5 seasons, contributing some of the show's darkest and most absurd storylines. He has also directed the films Borat, Religulous, Brüno, and The Dictator.

films.name: Borat: Cultural Learnings of America for Make Benefit Glorious Nation of Kazakhstan
Dicks: The Musical
films.review:
A musical meant to make you laugh… Unfortunately, I felt so much cringe that the jokes ended up fading into the background. I tried to give it a chance—there are a few (very few) positives, with one or two solid songs, but other than that...  

The best part of it was Megan Thee Stallion.

Lilly Wachowski.name: Lilly Wachowski
Lilly Wachowski.bio: Lilly Wachowski (born December 29, 1967) is known as one of the Wachowski sisters — a directing, writing and producing duo most famous for creating The Matrix series and V for Vendetta.

Louis Leterrier.name: Louis Leterrier
Louis Leterrier.bio:
Louis Leterrier (French: [lwi lətɛʁje]; born 17 June 1973) is a French film and television director. Best known for his work in action films, he directed the first two Transporter films (2002–2005), The Incredible Hulk (2008), Clash of the Titans (2010), Now You See Me (2013), and the tenth and eleventh Fast & Furious instalment, Fast X (2023) and Fast XI (2026). He also directed the streaming television series The Dark Crystal: Age of Resistance (2019) and Lupin (2021).
