Matthew Vaughn.name: Matthew Vaughn
Matthew Vaughn.bio: Matthew Allard de Vere Drummond (born Matthew Allard Robert Vaughn; 7 March 1971), known professionally as Matthew Vaughn, is an English filmmaker. He has produced films including Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels (1998) and Snatch (2000), and directed Layer Cake (2004), Stardust (2007), Kick-Ass (2010), X-Men: First Class (2011), and Argylle (2024). Vaughn also co-created the Kingsman comic book series and resulting franchise, directing, producing and co-writing the films Kingsman: The Secret Service (2014), Kingsman: The Golden Circle (2017), and The King's Man (2021).

films.name: Kingsman: The Golden Circle
films.genres: Action · Adventure · Comedy · Crime · Science Fiction

Michael Bay.name: Michael Bay
Michael Bay.bio:
Michael Benjamin Bay (born February 17, 1965) is an American film director and producer. He is best known for making big-budget, high-concept action films with fast cutting, stylistic cinematography and visuals, and extensive use of special effects, including frequent depictions of explosions. The films he has directed include Bad Boys (1995) and its sequel Bad Boys II (2003), The Rock (1996), Armageddon (1998), Pearl Harbor (2001), the first five films in the Transformers film series, 13 Hours: The Secret Soldiers of Benghazi (2016), 6 Underground (2019), and Ambulance (2022). His films have grossed over US$7.8 billion worldwide, making him one of history's most commercially successful directors.

He is co-founder of the production house the Institute and co-owns Platinum Dunes, a production house that has remade horror films, including The Texas Chainsaw Massacre (2003), The Amityville Horror (2005), The Hitcher (2007), Friday the 13th (2009), and A Nightmare on Elm Street (2010).

Description above from the Wikipedia article Michael Bay, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

Noah Baumbach.name: Noah Baumbach
Noah Baumbach.bio:
Noah Baumbach (born September 3, 1969) is an American filmmaker. He received Academy Award nominations for writing his films The Squid and the Whale (2005) and Marriage Story (2019), both of which he also directed, while the former garnered him one of the few screenwriters to ever sweep "The Big Four" critics awards: Los Angeles Film Critics Association, National Board of Review, New York Film Critics Circle, and National Society of Film Critics.

He has written and directed a number of other films, including Margot at the Wedding (2007), While We're Young (2014), and The Meyerowitz Stories (2017). He is also known for his collaborations with his partner Greta Gerwig in Greenberg (2010), Frances Ha (2013), Mistress America (2015), and White Noise (2022), as well as with Wes Anderson, co-writing The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou (2004), and Fantastic Mr. Fox (2009).

Description above from the Wikipedia article Noah Baumbach, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.
Noah Baumbach.birthplace: Brooklyn, New York City, New York, USA

films.name: De Palma
films.review:
It was extraordinarily fun to watch, mainly because he was talking directly to us the entire time.
I loved the way he broke down the concept of artistic freedom vs. the film industry, and I agree with him 100% — at least from what I can observe from the outside.

He's a director who's often misunderstood, but thankfully has unmistakable classics under his belt.
Here, he opens up about all the struggles he faced in his early years in a documentary that’s brilliantly executed and genuinely interesting.

films.name: Frances Ha
films.review:
It’s an autobiographical film that tries a bit too hard to be autobiographical.

The message is genuinely good, and I believe many people will relate to the characters or to the authenticity of being childlike while navigating adulthood. I felt connected to Frances throughout the entire movie, and everything she experienced was conveyed - just not in the most impactful way. I wish the film had leaned more into its dramatic moments.

It’s definitely not a bad film, which is why I’m giving it this rating, and it’s certainly not a waste of time. You follow a journey that could very well be your own - the beginning of adult life, full of highs and lows. Frances had plenty of lows, but the child within her managed to stay alive, genuine in every moment. Hold on to that moral; it’s worth having moments like these.

films.name: While We're Young
films.review: Damn it... Now I wanna get high to the Blade Runner (1982) soundtrack too.

films.name: The Squid and the Whale
films.review:
FINALLY A BREAK FROM UNI TO WATCH FILMS.

As soon as the film ended and I saw the credits, I thought, “of course this was produced by Wes Anderson.”

To be completely honest, it’s been hard for me to find something in Noah Baumbach’s work that truly delivers memorable moments. That might sound a bit harsh considering what I’ve experienced so far, but I feel like his dramas are missing something. Even with extraordinary actors, not everything can be saved, and some moments were genuinely dull.

I don’t think this justifies a runtime of an hour and twenty, something closer to 50 minutes would’ve been enough to convey everything, and might have even improved my opinion of the project.

Yes, it’s based on real events from his life, and I understand that it can be difficult to build a gripping script from that kind of premise. At the same time, I feel like the relationship between the children and the mother could have been explored more deeply, and especially the parents’ affairs with other people. It was presented way too quickly, almost in an ironic tone that felt a bit off to me. Was it meant to be comic relief? It didn’t work. Could it have leaned more into drama and carried a heavier tone to keep us more engaged? Definitely.

films.name: Jay Kelly
films.review:
An incredibly dry film, though I can’t deny the quality of the performances. The truth is, I only finished it because Clooney was the lead and because I have a soft spot for Adam Sandler, like everyone else.

A film with no real substance, extremely vague, built around a premise that feels somewhat randomly thrown together, and populated by characters it’s hard to connect with on an emotional level.

Paul Greengrass.name: Paul Greengrass
Paul Greengrass.bio:
Paul Greengrass CBE (born 13 August 1955) is an English film director, film producer, screenwriter, and former journalist.

One of his early films, Bloody Sunday (2002), won the Golden Bear at the 52nd Berlin International Film Festival. Other films Greengrass has directed include three entries of the Bourne action-thriller film series: The Bourne Supremacy (2004), The Bourne Ultimatum (2007) and Jason Bourne (2016). He also directed United 93 (2006), for which Greengrass won the BAFTA Award for Best Director and received an Academy Award nomination for Best Director; as well as Green Zone (2010) and Captain Phillips (2013). In 2004, he co-wrote and produced the film Omagh, which won the Single Drama award from the British Academy Television Awards.

In 2007, Greengrass co-founded Directors UK, a professional organisation of British filmmakers, and was its first president until 2014. He ranked 28th on EW's The 50 Smartest People in Hollywood in 2007. In 2008, The Telegraph named him among the most influential people in British culture. In 2017, Greengrass was honoured with a British Film Institute Fellowship.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Paul Greengrass, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

Paul W. S. Anderson.name: Paul W. S. Anderson
Paul W. S. Anderson.bio: Paul William Scott Anderson (born 4 March 1965), also known as Paul WS Anderson or Paul Anderson, is an English film director who regularly works in science fiction movies and video game adaptations.

Pete Docter.name: Pete Docter
Pete Docter.bio:
Peter Hans Docter (born October 9, 1968) is an American filmmaker and animator who has served as Pixar's chief creative officer (CCO)  since 2018. He has directed the company's animated films Monsters, Inc. (2001), Up (2009), Inside Out (2015), and Soul (2020). From his nine Academy Award nominations, he is a record-three-time recipient of Best Animated Feature for Up, Inside Out and Soul. Docter has also won six Annie Awards from nine nominations, a BAFTA Children's Film Award and a Hochi Film Award. He describes himself as a "geeky kid from Minnesota who likes to draw cartoons".

Description above from the Wikipedia article Pete Docter, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Soul
films.review:
“You can't crush a soul here, that's what life on Earth is for.” - 22

Sometimes we need to reconnect with our inner peace, especially in moments when stress, anxiety, and discouragement show up uninvited. I found this film during one of those moments, and I promised myself I would come back to it whenever I felt “lost” in things I still had to do: deadlines, responsibilities, times when I spend more time stuck in my room than outside, actually living life.

Don’t get me wrong, I love studying. I love working on projects that challenge me, that push me to my limits. But I reach a point where stopping feels like pressing a button covered in dust somewhere deep in my brain.

I used to be a lot like Joe… focused exclusively on what I could achieve academically and professionally. I forgot there was more to life than that.

This is a very special film to me. It reminds me of where I came from, what I’ve been through, and who I am now.

A project that… makes me stop. Think. Feel nostalgic for what I’ve lived and plan everything I still want to discover and experience.

Oh and by the way... Music is a big part of me, and without it... I would probably be one of those damned souls.

Ridley Scott.name: Ridley Scott
Ridley Scott.bio:
Sir Ridley Leighton Scott (born 30 November 1937) is an English filmmaker. He directs films in the science fiction, crime, and historical epic genres with an atmospheric and highly concentrated visual style. He ranks among the highest-grossing directors, with his films grossing a cumulative $5 billion worldwide. He has received many accolades, including the BAFTA Fellowship for Lifetime Achievement in 2018, two Primetime Emmy Awards, and a Golden Globe Award. He was knighted by Queen Elizabeth II in 2003 and appointed a Knight Grand Cross by King Charles III in 2024.

An alumnus of the Royal College of Art in London, Scott began his career in television as a designer and director before moving into advertising as a director of commercials. He made his film directorial debut with The Duellists (1977) and gained wider recognition with his next film, Alien (1979). Though his films range widely in setting and period, they showcase memorable imagery of urban environments, spanning 2nd-century Rome in Gladiator (2000) and its 2024 sequel, 12th-century Jerusalem in Kingdom of Heaven (2005), medieval England in Robin Hood (2010), ancient Memphis in Exodus: Gods and Kings (2014), contemporary Mogadishu in Black Hawk Down (2001), futuristic cityscapes of Los Angeles in Blade Runner (1982) and extraterrestrial worlds in Alien, Prometheus (2012), The Martian (2015) and Alien: Covenant (2017).

Scott has been nominated for three Academy Awards: Directing for Thelma & Louise, Gladiator, and Black Hawk Down. Gladiator won the Academy Award for Best Picture and received a nomination in the same category for The Martian. In 1995, Scott and his brother Tony received a British Academy Film Award for Outstanding British Contribution to Cinema. Scott's films Alien, Blade Runner and Thelma & Louise were each selected for preservation in the United States National Film Registry by the Library of Congress for being considered "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant". In a 2004 BBC poll, Scott was ranked 10 on the list of most influential people in British culture. Scott also works in television and has earned 10 Primetime Emmy Award nominations. He won twice, for Outstanding Television Film for the HBO film The Gathering Storm (2002) and Outstanding Documentary or Nonfiction Special for the History Channel's Gettysburg (2011). He was Emmy-nominated for RKO 281 (1999), The Andromeda Strain (2008), and The Pillars of the Earth (2010).
Ridley Scott.birthplace: 1937 (88 yaşında)  Doğum Yeri South Shields, County Durham, İngiltere, Birleşik Krallık

films.name: Gladiator II
films.review: A minha mãe andava a chatear-me para ver isto. Lá fui eu armar-me em pirata. Legendas duvidosas mas filme incrível. Ver outra vez não chateia.

films.name: Thelma
films.review:
I went into this film without reading the synopsis. As I’ve mentioned in another review, I’ve been trying to explore more of Trier’s work, and that’s exactly what I’ve been doing. Watching this project made the differences between it and the three previous films I’ve seen from him very clear - and no, that’s not a negative. There’s a lot to say about this one.

It’s impressive. He continues to surprise me, whether through overwhelming melancholy or by venturing into something closer to psychological thriller territory, everything feels perfectly placed.

This is one of the most symbolic and emotionally devastating messages I’ve encountered within this kind of genre. As always, I try to avoid spoilers in my reviews, so don’t assume the story is generic, it’s anything but that. It’s incredibly complex to describe, yet it held me from beginning to end. Not only because of the thriller elements, but also because of the way he introduces and develops the protagonist within the religious constraints of her family.

The way he succinctly portrays the struggles of deeply conservative families, especially when imposed on young people with promising futures but painful memories, felt nothing short of brilliant.

There are stunning scenes, meticulously haunting moments that will stay with me for a long time. The seizure scene in the swimming pool left me psychologically shaken, and the moment I fully understood what the story was building toward made me cry uncontrollably. Not to mention the final scene, executed in such a powerful way that it leaves you questioning what truly happened.

Joachim Trier, now I can finally say this with certainty: you’ve entered my list of favorite directors.

films.name: Napoleon
films.review:
Considerando toda a cinematografia do Scott, sentia-me esperançoso e ansioso por ver em tela grande a história mais sombria da França e talvez das mais impactantes e tenebrosas da história. Um tirano muito bem representado pelo Joaquin com toques arrepiantes pelo caminho. Interessante que todos os momentos mais dramáticos e com menos “falinhas mansas” do sujeito eram de cortar a respiração, serviu o propósito. 
Produção, imagem, cinematografia, breath taking, é 10/10.
Existiram talvez alguns pontos negativos, e o principal: filme um pouco corrido, com cortes em cenas que deveriam estar no cinema e deviam acompanhar a jornada da personagem. O início por exemplo, tinha bastantes solavancos, com time skips um pouco confusos.
A verdade é que fiquei com bastante expectativa de ver a extended version. 4 horas de Napoleão? Sou apologista.

Robert Eggers.name: Robert Eggers
Robert Eggers.bio: Robert Houston Eggers (born July 7, 1983) is an American filmmaker. After working for years as a production designer, he became known for writing and directing the period horror films The Witch (2015), The Lighthouse (2019) and Nosferatu (2024), as well as co-writing and directing the Viking action film The Northman (2022). Noted for his attention to historical period detail, his films often incorporate elements of folklore and mythology.
Robert Eggers.birthplace: New York City, New York, USA

films.name: The Tell-Tale Heart
films.review:
Yes. Another one with a 5-star rating. And yes... I'm biased in this review, it's one of my all-time favorite stories, and one of my favorite directors.

Before watching a film by Eggers, whether it's a short or a feature, I always keep in mind that the atmosphere experience will truly be important to the progression of the story. The reality is that all the settings and the old man's way of being were extremely unsettling.

The very expression of the servant, the contradiction between the love and hatred he had for the old man, was so well conveyed that it left me completely chilled and afraid to watch until the end, knowing and being very familiar with the story.

Everything in this short film reminded me of one of the most terrifying phrases I’ve ever read in a tale, especially coming from Poe:
“Now this is the point. You fancy me a mad. Madmen know nothing. But you should have seen me. You should have seen how wisely I proceeded...”

films.name: The Lighthouse
films.review: Moral of the story: don't EVER spill yer' beans.

films.name: The Witch
films.review:
I know I'm a suspect, as you've noticed, I have a crush on Eggers: just like Caleb for his sister's breasts.

Pride is the worst sin. Glad I'm a sinner.

films.name: Nosferatu
films.review:
What a freaking masterpiece. Nothing more to say.
I've said it once and I'll say it again: Robert Eggers is my favorite filmmaker.

Also, the girl is more hornier than me.

films.name: The Northman
films.review:
"You must choose between kindness for your kin, and hatred for your enemies."

Robert Eggers is officially my new favourite director. What a freaking masterpiece. Not prepared for the Hamlet analogy, still in shock to be honest.

films.name: Brothers
films.review: You know what? I'm worried about Ari Aster... But I'm more concerned about this man... Eggers.

films.name: Hansel and Gretel
films.review: I'm preparing for "Nosferatu"...

Robert Zemeckis.name: Robert Zemeckis
Robert Zemeckis.bio:
Robert Lee Zemeckis (born May 14, 1952) is an American filmmaker known for directing and producing a range of successful and influential movies. He often blends cutting-edge visual effects with storytelling. He has received several accolades, including an Academy Award and a Golden Globe Award, as well as nominations for five British Academy Film Awards and a Daytime Emmy Award.

Zemeckis started his career directing the comedy films I Wanna Hold Your Hand (1978), Used Cars (1980), and Romancing the Stone (1984). He gained prominence directing the science-fiction comedy Back to the Future trilogy (1985–1990), the fantasy comedy Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988), and the comedy-drama Forrest Gump (1994), the latter of which won Academy Awards for Best Picture and Best Director.

He has also directed the satirical black comedy Death Becomes Her (1992), the science fiction film Contact (1997), and the drama films Cast Away (2000), Flight (2012), The Walk (2015), and Allied (2016). His exploration of motion capture techniques can be seen in the animated films The Polar Express (2004) and A Christmas Carol (2009), as well as the action fantasy drama Beowulf (2007) and the drama Welcome to Marwen (2018). He has collaborated with film composer Alan Silvestri since 1984 and directed Tom Hanks in five films.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Robert Zemeckis, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Back to the Future Part II
Here
films.review: Ainda tenho bastantes pontos para realçar sobre o que vi mas acima de tudo destaco os últimos 20/25 minutos: fizeram-me chorar, bastante, e não estava a contar com isso, especialmente por ter visto, antes destes momentos finais do projeto, um filme extremamente entediante com histórias que só estavam lá por estar. Alguns momentos em que sentia uma  profunda irritação pelo que estava a ver e outros (os instantes finais) que me deixaram de coração cheio. Tudo para concluir que em vez de 100 minutos, faria algo de 30, uma curta metragem só com a história que interessa.

Ryan Coogler.name: Ryan Coogler
Ryan Coogler.bio:
Ryan Kyle Coogler (born May 23, 1986) is an American filmmaker. He has received an Academy Award, a BAFTA Award, four Black Reel Awards, a Grammy Award, a Golden Globe Award, and ten NAACP Image Awards.

Coogler directed a few short films at the USC School of Cinematic Arts before his feature-length debut with Fruitvale Station (2013). He then transitioned to directing and writing franchise films, including the Rocky series spinoff, Creed (2015), as well as the Marvel films Black Panther (2018) and Black Panther: Wakanda Forever (2022). Coogler also produced the historical drama Judas and the Black Messiah (2021) and the supernatural horror film Sinners (2025), which he also wrote and directed. He received nominations for the Academy Award for Best Picture for both films, while for Sinners, he was also nominated for Best Director and won the Oscar for Best Original Screenplay.

In 2013, he was included on Time's list of the 30 people under 30 who are changing the world. In 2018, Coogler was named the runner-up of Time's Person of the Year, and he was included in the annual Time 100 list of the most influential people in the world. In 2021, Coogler, his wife, Zinzi Coogler, and Sev Ohanian founded multimedia production company Proximity Media.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Ryan Coogler, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Sinners
films.review:
Taking a little break from the A24 catalogue just to say that this is probably going to be the best 2025 film.
Hoping that Eddington is as good as this.

Sam Raimi.name: Sam Raimi
Sam Raimi.bio:
Samuel Marshall Raimi (/ˈreɪmi/ RAY-mee; born October 23, 1959) is an American filmmaker. He is best known for writing and directing the Evil Dead trilogy (1981–1992) and directing the Spider-Man trilogy (2002–2007). He also directed Darkman (1990), The Quick and the Dead (1995), A Simple Plan (1998), The Gift (2000), Drag Me to Hell (2009), Oz the Great and Powerful (2013), and Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness (2022).

His films are known for their highly dynamic visual style, inspired by comic books and slapstick comedy. He founded the production companies Renaissance Pictures in 1979 and Ghost House Pictures in 2002. Raimi has also produced several successful television series, including Hercules: The Legendary Journeys (1995–1997), its spin-off Xena: Warrior Princess (1995–2001), and Ash vs Evil Dead (2015–2018) starring longtime friend and collaborator Bruce Campbell, reprising his role in the Evil Dead franchise.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Sam Raimi, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness

Sean Baker.name: Sean Baker
Sean Baker.bio: Sean Baker (born February 26, 1971) is an American filmmaker. He is best known for directing independent feature films about the lives of sex workers, including Take Out (2004), Starlet (2012), Tangerine (2015), The Florida Project (2017), Red Rocket (2021), and Anora (2024), the last of which won him the Palme d'Or at the 2024 Cannes Film Festival, as well as Academy Awards for Best Picture, Best Director, Best Original Screenplay and Best Film Editing. He is also known for creating the Fox/IFC puppet sitcom Greg the Bunny (2002–2006) and its spin-offs.

films.name: Anora
films.review:
She just wanted to be loved... 
Love can be so blind that it can make us believe in people we shouldn't trust.

I really think it's a little overrated... Even though Mikey and Moore did an amazing job, I still believe Fernanda Torres deserves that Oscar more.

films.name: Red Rocket
films.review:
Sean, I’m sorry to say this, but this kind of film really isn’t for me. I do understand your essence and the message you’re trying to bring to cinema and smaller productions, but I just can’t connect much with the stories you choose to tell.
Maybe it’s simply because I’m not a fan of things that are so explicit, or of stories that feel both strange and oddly “normal” at the same time… I honestly can’t explain it.
That being said, while watching the film I could easily see why so many people are drawn to this type of project: the characters are very well developed, the story is solid — even entertaining at times — and the cinematography is surprisingly strong given the production budget.
And that’s exactly why I’m giving it the rating I am… Even if this genre doesn’t appeal to me, I can clearly recognize that you’re a remarkable director, unafraid to tell these kinds of stories.

films.name: The Florida Project
films.review:
I'm going to be completely straightforward in this review because, after watching three of Sean’s projects, I still feel the same: films with brilliant cinematography but stories that never grab me from the start.
Unfortunately, I just can’t connect with the scripts he brings to his features. This one in particular felt dull for most of the runtime — except, clearly, for the final part.
The premise does have interesting elements, yes, but it ends up falling short. Honestly, it didn’t need to be such a long film to say everything it wanted to say.
I don’t want to sound insensitive — and I promise I actually cried at the end — the run to Disney World was extremely powerful and full of symbolism.

Shawn Levy.name: Shawn Levy
Shawn Levy.bio:
Shawn Adam Levy (/ˈliːvaɪ/; born July 23, 1968) is a Canadian filmmaker and actor. He is the founder of 21 Laps Entertainment. His work has spanned numerous genres, and his films as a director have grossed $3.5 billion worldwide.

Following early work as a television director, Levy gained recognition in the 2000s for directing comedy films like Big Fat Liar (2002), Just Married (2003), Cheaper by the Dozen (2003) and The Pink Panther (2006). He then found further success as the director of the first three films in the Night at the Museum film franchise (2006–14). In the early 2010s, he directed films including Date Night (2010), Real Steel (2011), and The Internship (2013), and developed several comedy television pilots. Executive produced the ABC sitcom Last Man Standing. Levy produced the 2016 sci-fi film Arrival, earning him an Academy Award nomination for Best Picture.

Since 2016, Levy has been an executive producer on the Netflix original series Stranger Things. He has directed the third and fourth episodes of each of the show's four seasons and the Netflix limited series All the Light We CanNot See (2023). Levy has recently collaborated with Ryan Reynolds by directing Free Guy (2021), The Adam Project (2022), and Deadpool & Wolverine (2024), with the latter emerging as his highest-grossing film and the highest-grossing R-rated film of all time.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Shawn Levy, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Deadpool & Wolverine
films.review: It's still extremely fun... And the scout leader's joke is still the best of all.

films.name: Night at the Museum: Secret of the Tomb
Night at the Museum: Battle of the Smithsonian

Sofia Coppola.name: Sofia Coppola
Sofia Coppola.bio:
Sofia Carmina Coppola (/ˈkoʊpələ/ KOH-pə-lə, Italian: [soˈfiːa ˈkɔppola]; born May 14, 1971) is an American filmmaker and former actress. She has won an Academy Award, two Golden Globe Awards, a Golden Lion, and a Cannes Film Festival Award. She was also nominated for three BAFTA Awards, as well as a Primetime Emmy Award.

Her parents are filmmakers Eleanor and Francis Ford Coppola, and she made her acting debut as an infant in her father's acclaimed crime drama The Godfather (1972). Coppola later appeared in several music videos and had a supporting role in the fantasy comedy film Peggy Sue Got Married (1986). She then portrayed Mary Corleone, the daughter of Michael Corleone, in the sequel The Godfather Part III (1990).

Coppola transitioned into filmmaking with her feature-length directorial debut in the coming-of-age drama The Virgin Suicides (1999). It was the first of her collaborations with actress Kirsten Dunst. Her films often deal with themes of loneliness, wealth, privilege, isolation, youth, femininity, and adolescence in America. Coppola received the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for the comedy-drama Lost in Translation (2003), and was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Director, becoming the third woman to do so. She has since directed the historical drama Marie Antoinette (2006), the family drama Somewhere (2010), the satirical crime drama The Bling Ring (2013), the southern gothic thriller The Beguiled (2017), the comedy On the Rocks (2020), and the biographical drama Priscilla (2023).

In 2015, Coppola released the Netflix Christmas musical comedy special A Very Murray Christmas, which earned her a nomination for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Television Movie.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Sofia Coppola, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.
Sofia Coppola.birthplace: New York City, New York, USA

films.name: Priscilla
films.review:
It’s not a perfect film. In my opinion, it’s actually quite far from it… But I can clearly understand the importance of a film like this and the masterful cinematography I witnessed. Delicate, with stunning color work and an aesthetic that left me speechless.
My issue was really with the film’s pace — very slow, with scenes that felt overly drawn out without much point, and others that could’ve had a much stronger impact on the viewer (like the suite scene near the end).

films.name: On the Rocks
films.review:
It was quite fun, even though I get the criticism. I don’t exactly feel like a 40-year-old woman who enjoys renting movies, but I can totally see this kind of vibe fitting the project.
It deals with a pretty interesting topic, especially when it comes to betrayal and over-involved parents... I think Sofia could’ve pushed Bill Murray’s character a bit further, and the final moment between husband and wife didn’t feel satisfying at all.

films.name: The Bling Ring
films.review:
Based on a true story, so criticizing the script feels a bit tricky. However, I can easily call out the cinematography and editing because, honestly… terrible.
As for the case itself, it was definitely interesting to learn about. The irresponsibility—or rather, the sheer naivety—reached such an extreme that reality never even seemed to cross their minds.

Steven Soderbergh.name: Steven Soderbergh
Steven Soderbergh.bio:
Steven Andrew Soderbergh (born January 14, 1963) is an American film director, producer, screenwriter, cinematographer, and editor. A pioneer of modern independent cinema, Soderbergh later drew acclaim for formally inventive films made within the studio system.

Soderbergh's directorial breakthrough, the indie drama Sex, Lies, and Videotape (1989), lifted him into the public spotlight as a notable presence in the film industry. At 26, Soderbergh became the youngest solo director to win the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival, and the film garnered worldwide commercial success, as well as numerous accolades. His next five films, which included King of the Hill (1993), were commercially unsuccessful. He pivoted into more mainstream fare with the crime comedy Out of Sight (1998), the biopic Erin Brockovich (2000) and the crime drama Traffic (2000). For Traffic, he won the Academy Award for Best Director.

He found further popular and critical success with the Ocean's trilogy and film franchise (2001–18); Che (2008); The Informant! (2009); Contagion (2011); Haywire (2011); Magic Mike (2012); Side Effects (2013); Logan Lucky (2017); Unsane (2018); Let Them All Talk (2020); No Sudden Move (2021); and Kimi (2022). His film career spans a multitude of genres, but his specialties are psychological, crime and heist films. His films have grossed over US$2.2 billion worldwide and garnered fourteen Academy Award nominations, winning five.

Soderbergh's films often revolve around familiar concepts which are regularly used for big-budget Hollywood movies, but he routinely employs an avant-garde arthouse approach. They center on themes of shifting personal identities, vengeance, sexuality, morality, and the human condition. His feature films are often distinctive in the realm of cinematography as a result of his having been influenced by avant-garde cinema, coupled with his use of unconventional film and camera formats. Many of Soderbergh's films are anchored by multi-dimensional storylines with plot twists, nonlinear storytelling, experimental sequencing, suspenseful soundscapes, and third-person vantage points.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Steven Soderbergh, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Presence
films.review: It's super different from anything I've ever seen... The way they filmed it was simply AWESOME. There could have been more story, and the cuts and editing of the film ruined the suspense of some scenes a bit.

films.name: The Laundromat
films.review:
Se é uma forma engraçada de se contar uma história ridiculamente horrível e que expõe os defeitos do ser humanos? Sim é. Contou um dos casos mais preocupantes de corrupção do mundo. Mas não da forma que eu estava à espera. A partir do momento em que vemos duas personagens a partirem a quarta parede percebo o que o diretor e escritor quiseram fazer: ridicularizar a cena. 
Atores de peso a fazerem um papel sólido. Mas… Um guião com falta de muito sumo. Se querem fazer uma sátira que metam coisas ainda mais estapafúrdias. Se é para meter graça que metam. Lembro-me de me partir a rir 2 vezes. Não houve mais nenhum momento. E atenção, é um filme de comédia!?

films.name: Black Bag
films.review:
This film disappointed me far too much - in every possible way. For me, it was the exact opposite of what a thriller should be: it didn’t captivate me, it didn’t make me eager to reach the end, and it made me lose interest in the case incredibly fast. Above all, it delivered a final twist so underwhelming that I don’t think I’ve ever felt this kind of let-down in a film that CLAIMS TO BELONG to this genre. Incredibly predictable, desperately trying to hold on to suspense until the very last shot.
Lines like “See who picks up the gun” landed painfully badly, making me roll my eyes repeatedly while trying to focus on the film’s few positive aspects: incredible actors and an editing job that was… acceptable.

Steven Spielberg.name: Steven Spielberg
Steven Spielberg.bio:
Steven Allan Spielberg KBE (born December 18, 1946) is an American filmmaker. A major figure of the New Hollywood era and pioneer of the modern blockbuster, Spielberg is widely regarded as one of the greatest and most influential filmmakers in the history of cinema and is the highest-grossing film director of all time. Among other accolades, he has received three Academy Awards, four Golden Globe Awards and three BAFTA Awards, as well as the AFI Life Achievement Award in 1995, an honorary knighthood in 2001, the Kennedy Center Honors in 2006, the Cecil B. DeMille Award in 2009, the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2015, and the National Medal of Arts in 2023. According to Forbes, he is the wealthiest celebrity.

Spielberg was born in Cincinnati, Ohio, and grew up in Phoenix, Arizona. He moved to California and studied film in college. After directing several episodes for television, including Night Gallery and Columbo, he directed the television film Duel (1971), which Barry Diller approved. He made his theatrical debut with The Sugarland Express (1974), also beginning his decades-long collaboration with composer John Williams, with whom he has worked for all but five of his theatrical releases. He became a household name with the summer blockbuster Jaws (1975). He continued directing acclaimed escapist box-office hits with Close Encounters of the Third Kind (1977), E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (1982), and the original Indiana Jones trilogy (1981–1989). He also explored drama in The Color Purple (1985) and Empire of the Sun (1987).

In 1993, Spielberg directed back-to-back hits with the science fiction thriller Jurassic Park, the highest-grossing film at the time, and the epic historical drama Schindler's List, which has often been ranked among the greatest films ever made. He won the Academy Award for Best Director for the latter as well as for the World War II epic Saving Private Ryan (1998). Spielberg has since directed the science fiction films A.I. Artificial Intelligence (2001), Minority Report (2002), War of the Worlds (2005) and Ready Player One (2018); the historical dramas Amistad (1997), Munich (2005), War Horse (2011), Lincoln (2012), Bridge of Spies (2015) and The Post (2017); the comedies Catch Me If You Can (2002) and The Terminal (2004); the animated film The Adventures of Tintin (2011); the musical West Side Story (2021); and the family drama The Fabelmans (2022).

Spielberg co-founded Amblin Entertainment and DreamWorks Pictures, and he has served as a producer for many successful films and television series, among them Poltergeist (1982), Gremlins (1984), Back to the Future (1985), An American Tail (1986), Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988), Animaniacs (1993), Freakazoid! (1995), Twister (1996), Band of Brothers (2001) and Transformers (2007). Several of Spielberg's works are considered among the greatest films in history, and some are among the highest-grossing films of all time. Seven of his films have been inducted into the National Film Registry by the Library of Congress as being "culturally, historically or aesthetically significant". In 2013, Time listed him among the 100 most influential people. In 2023, Spielberg was the recipient of the first-ever Time 100 Impact Award in the US.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Steven Spielberg, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom
Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull

Ti West.name: Ti West
Ti West.bio:
Timon C. West (born October 5, 1980) is an American film director, producer, screenwriter, editor, cinematographer, and actor, best known for his work in horror films. He directed the horror films The Roost (2005), Trigger Man (2007), The House of the Devil (2009), The Innkeepers (2011), the Western In a Valley of Violence (2016), as well as the X film series. He has also acted in a number of films, mostly in those directed by either himself or Joe Swanberg.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Ti West, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Pearl
films.review:
It’s impressive how every family dinner scene in A24 horror films are, as a great colleague and friend—Mr. Martin Scorsese—would say, "Absolute Cinema." The dinner between the father (though barely present), mother, and daughter was simply brilliant—fear, panic, anxiety, stress, anguish—all culminating in a single scene.  

Mia Goth’s monologue will stay with me forever. I can’t stop thinking about everything she said and the way she delivered it.

I was completely mesmerized the entire time, unable to take my eyes off every little detail I could catch. I found it truly extraordinary how subtly they transported me to that era—through the clothing, the atmosphere, and the portrayal of the hopes (or dreams) of that time. Simply amazing.

And the ending—almost "comedic"—reminded me of "Looney Tunes". Just her unhinged, psychotic expression alone managed to leave me both unsettled and laughing at the same time.

I’m still considering the 5 stars—for now, I’ll leave it as is. If something shifts in me, I’ll add a note to this review.

films.name: X
films.review:
Watching this just made me want to finish the "trilogy" as quickly as possible. The characters aren’t really introduced properly, and it feels intentional—setting things up for standalone films about Pearl and Maxine. 

Pearl, in particular, seems to have so much more to tell, and I’m genuinely excited to learn more about her. One detail I found absolutely brilliant was having Pearl and Maxine played by the same actress, which already hints at the idea that Pearl once had dreams similar to Maxine’s. In fact, you can pick up on that throughout the film, even if it’s not explicitly stated.  

The only reason I’m not giving this more than 4 stars is the horror aspect. I loved the psychological depth, and the horror elements were well-executed, but it lacked that *essential* touch—more unexpected, out-of-the-box moments.  

PS: The final scene shows the "program" Pearl and Howard were watching, and trust me, it’s worth sticking around for. It only made me more eager to learn about Maxine, especially with all the strict conservatism and religious fundamentalism surrounding her father.

films.name: MaXXXine
films.review:
Well… I wrap up the "trilogy" with "MaXXXine", and I can confidently say this was one of the best experiences I’ve had with horror films. Even though this film didn’t exceed my expectations (especially in the final act), I had an incredible time getting to know these characters.  

Exploring more of Maxine, her future in the film industry, was fascinating. The critique of Hollywood is expertly woven throughout—from how horror films often don’t get the spotlight they deserve, to the role of women in the industry, and the countless obstacles that conservatism and extremism have created (and continue to create) to this day.

I was leaning towards a 4-star rating during the first hour, but once the serial killer is known, the script’s quality—and even some of the performances—took a noticeable dip.

Todd Phillips.name: Todd Phillips
Todd Phillips.bio: Todd Phillips (born Todd Philip Bunzl; December 19, 1970) is an American filmmaker. Phillips began his career in 1993 and directed films in the 2000s such as Road Trip, Old School, Starsky & Hutch, and School for Scoundrels. He came to wider prominence in the early 2010s for directing The Hangover film series. In 2019, he co-wrote and directed the psychological thriller film Joker, based on the DC Comics character of the same name, which premiered at the 76th Venice International Film Festival where it received the top prize, the Golden Lion. Joker went on to earn Phillips three Academy Award nominations for Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Adapted Screenplay, with his co-writer Scott Silver, his second, third, and fourth Academy Award nominations after also being nominated for Best Adapted Screenplay for Borat at the 79th Academy Awards.
Todd Phillips.birthplace: Brooklyn, New York City, New York, USA

films.name: War Dogs
films.review: And how about giving up Physics and finding errors in laws in the USA? Maybe I can get away with it, I have some ideas.

Trey Edward Shults.name: Trey Edward Shults
Trey Edward Shults.bio:
Trey Edward Shults was born in Houston, Texas. In 2010, he made his directorial debut in the short film Mother and Son, which he also wrote, edited, and produced. It starred Krisha Fairchild and Lucas Quintana. In 2011 and 2012, Shults variously served as film loader, post-production intern, and intern, on three of Terrence Malick's films, Song to Song, Voyage of Time, and The Tree of Life. At the time, Shults was a business management student but dropped out to work on films and study films himself. In 2011, Shults wrote, directed, produced, and edited, a short film starring Krisha Fairchild, titled Two to One.

In 2014, Shults directed, wrote, produced, edited, and appeared in Krisha, a short film starring Krisha, Robyn, and Billie Fairchild, all of whom are related to Shults. The following year, he directed, wrote, and acted in a feature-length adaptation of the same name, which premiered at South by Southwest in March 2015, winning the Grand Jury Award. The film is based upon a real-life incident involving Shults's cousin, who experienced a relapse at a family reunion. It was acquired by A24 and released in 2016.

In March 2015, Shults signed with William Morris Endeavor. Shults also served as the camera PA on the 2016 film Midnight Special.

films.name: Waves
films.review:
Please, always use protection.

On a more serious note... I'm still in shock from the sheer amount of emotions I felt throughout this project. And I honestly can't even express them properly — it was just that intense. This will stay with me forever, and I'm genuinely grateful to have come across something with so much meaning and so many important messages, especially in today’s world. I just hope more people get to watch it — it’s something special.

Ps: That scene in the garden... That moment between father and daughter, absolutely perfect.

films.name: Krisha
films.review:
I had some hopes before watching this film, and that turned out to be a problem… I found the beginning a bit dull, and even the scenes where she struggles with the urge to use drugs or alcohol could have been handled better—diving deeper into her mind, understanding her motives. Even without explicit explanations, nothing should feel glossed over, and I think that aspect could have been explored more effectively.  

There are plenty of positives, but I’ll focus on the final act because it truly stood out and exceeded my expectations—brilliantly performed, with a strong emphasis on the mother-son dynamic. And that final shot lingering on Krisha’s face only intensified the drama. I loved it.

films.name: It Comes at Night
films.review:
So, it's easy to tell throughout the film that "it" refers to fear, terror, or paranoia that comes at night — which isn't particularly clever, and it ends up being just another generic horror movie. Yes, there are some really interesting moments, or at least ones that are decently executed — like Travis' dreams, or even the final shot of the film, with the parents sitting at the table, staring at each other (which, in my opinion, was a brilliant touch).

Even with these positives, the lack of coherence in the characters made this feel extremely ordinary: it's yet another horror film where a character is dumb enough to run into the woods at night, with a deadly syndrome just waiting to get inside them. The illness itself is never explained, but it clearly works as a metaphor for fear...

Overall, a horror film like so many others, with one or two standout moments in terms of aesthetic and cinematography.

Wes Anderson.name: Wes Anderson
Wes Anderson.bio:
Wesley Wales Anderson (born May 1, 1969) is an American filmmaker. His films are known for their symmetry, eccentricity and distinctive visual and narrative styles, and he is cited by some critics as a modern-day example of the auteur. Three of his films, The Royal Tenenbaums (2001), Moonrise Kingdom (2012), and The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014) appeared in BBC Culture's 2016 poll of the greatest films since 2000.

Anderson was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for The Royal Tenenbaums (2001), Moonrise Kingdom (2012) and The Grand Budapest Hotel (2014), as well as the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature for the stop-motion films Fantastic Mr. Fox (2009) and Isle of Dogs (2018). With The Grand Budapest Hotel, he received his first Academy Award nominations for Best Director and Best Picture, and won the Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy and the BAFTA Award for Best Original Screenplay. He currently runs the production company American Empirical Pictures, which he founded in 1998. He won the Silver Bear for Best Director for Isle of Dogs in 2018.

films.name: The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar
Fantastic Mr. Fox
films.review:
Ladies and gentlemen... Mr. Wes Anderson.
Nem sei o que dizer. Estou sem palavras, é tudo tão lindo, tão interativo, tão minucioso... Sentia-me uma criança, simplesmente encantado com cada adereço, cada animação, imagem, personagem, voz. 
Uma mensagem simples mas tão poderosa, um filme de animação que certamente os meus filhos vão ver.
É como vos disse: não sei o que dizer de tão incrível que é. Vejam.

films.name: The Grand Budapest Hotel
films.review: Wes Anderson, you never cease to amaze me. One of the most beautiful films I've ever seen - cinematography that always leaves me with goosebumps... What a fantastic project, with a very subtle plot but with criticisms to human beings that are very well hidden. It was everything I asked for and you still managed to overcome it... You really are incredible Anderson.

films.name: Isle of Dogs
films.review:
Every reason is a good enough reason to be deported. (contains irony)

This man’s creativity fascinates me. It leaves me at a loss for words. This is clearly a project that delivers harsh criticism of society and the way politics are conducted, but what surprised me the most was the way he chose to express those ideas (many of them as axioms).

It’s not a perfect film, and I don’t think it’s his best, but at the same time I feel a strong need to talk about it. It’s an incredible film, both in terms of animation and storytelling. I’ve seen negative reviews complaining about the lack of “substance” in the story, and while watching the film I couldn’t help but notice where those people stand on the political spectrum. At the same time that they fail to understand the content, they throw stones at the characters as if they were to blame for the so-called “lack of substance.”

It’s absolutely worth watching - it has incredible historical and political elements, and a direction that left me speechless.

films.name: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
films.review:
Wes Anderson created a Wes Anderson film in which every scene features Wes Anderson's signature, with actors who appear in almost all of Wes Anderson's films. 
I love the way he tells these types of stories.

films.name: Bottle Rocket
films.review:
"Ain't it funny how you used to be in the nut house and now I'm in jail?" - Dignan

What an incredible phrase...
It's quite entertaining, the hour and a half flies by but it's clearly a film with some, I would say few, flaws.

films.name: Rushmore
films.review:
It definitely wasn't the plot I was expecting. Love is so blind that it is capable of destroying not only your own life, but also those around you.

Using "The Wind" in the soundtrack... I have to say, Mr. Anderson, that I really like you.

films.name: The Phoenician Scheme
films.review:
So… yet another Wes Anderson film with Wes Anderson cinematography, Wes Anderson humor, and a script that feels very Wes Anderson.
Probably one of his less accomplished ones — especially in terms of story and how it unfolds — but still incredibly solid.

films.name: The Royal Tenenbaums
films.review:
I was a bit disappointed. I wasn’t expecting to encounter characters in a Wes Anderson film that I simply couldn’t connect with emotionally, but unfortunately, that’s what happened.

The cinematography is unmistakably his, and I love it. The editing is also very well executed. Still, I couldn’t fully engage with the story itself, even though the actors delivered extraordinary performances.

I went in expecting to have a lot of fun, yet I didn’t laugh once,  which is unusual for a Wes Anderson film, at least compared to my previous experiences with his work.

I feel like I might be being a little harsh on the film as a whole, so let me emphasize this again: Wes Anderson has a remarkable ability to immerse us in a story and keep us engaged all the way to the end. That alone deserves praise, especially considering I didn’t connect with the narrative as much as I had hoped.

Yorgos Lanthimos.name: Yorgos Lanthimos
Yorgos Lanthimos.bio:
Yorgos Lanthimos (Greek: Γιώργος Λάνθιμος, born 23 September 1973) is a Greek filmmaker. He has received multiple accolades, including a BAFTA Award and a Golden Lion, as well as nominations for five Academy Awards and a Golden Globe Award.

Lanthimos started his career in experimental theatre before making his directorial film debut with the sex comedy My Best Friend (2001). He rose to prominence by directing the psychological drama film Dogtooth (2009), which won the Un Certain Regard prize at the Cannes Film Festival and was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film. Lanthimos transitioned to making English-language films with the black comedy The Lobster (2015), which earned him an Academy Award nomination for Best Original Screenplay, and the psychological thriller The Killing of a Sacred Deer (2017).

He collaborated with actress Emma Stone in the period black comedies The Favourite (2018) and Poor Things (2023) and the anthology film Kinds of Kindness (2024). He received nominations for the Academy Award for Best Director and Best Picture for The Favourite and Poor Things, in addition to winning the Golden Lion for the latter.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Yorgos Lanthimos, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Poor Things
films.review:
Existe um conflito dentro de mim que não me permite dar 5 estrelas a este filme. E para vos ser sincero não consigo explicar o que senti ao ver isto. Uma mistura de emoções…. Tanto me sentia desconfortável como encantado em cada cena. Uma cinematografia que não lembra a ninguém, uma soundtrack arrepiante que nos transporta para um cenário “horripilante”, “falso” diria. O próprio cgi dá conta do recado em relação a esse aspeto. 
Podia continuar com mais elogios mas quero manter esta crítica curta. Mas por favor: deem o Oscar à Emma Stone: que Deusa, que magnífico vê-la a atuar. Dafoe é Ruffalo idem aspas, só mostraram o quão grandes atores são.
São regras da casa terminar com “formidable”

films.name: The Lobster
films.review:
Yorgos, és um maníaco, insanamente marado, louco... Deixas-me sempre sem palavras quando acabo de ver um filme teu.
Tanto simbolismo, tanta metáfora e coisas tão hiperbolizadas mas que fazem TANTO sentido dentro da sociedade atual... Um espelho completamente exagerado do nosso mundo, deixou-me tão desconfortável e tão pensativo ao mesmo tempo - genial.
Cinematografia arrepiante, diálogos super estranhos, às vezes quase que nem parece bem representado pelo ator em si... E no fim chegas à conclusão que era tudo de propósito para pensares fora da caixa, para te deixar fora da tua zona de conforto.

films.name: Bugonia
films.review:
I think it’s completely understandable if people don’t consider this Yorgos’s best film - and, in my view, it doesn’t reach the madness of The Lobster or the visual and technical fascination of Poor Things. But let’s be honest: this was the most fun I’ve ever had with one of his films.

A movie capable of showing just how ridiculous a human being can be, where the project itself embraces that ridiculousness and pulls us into a universe that really isn’t that far from our own. It doesn’t take long for the insanity to escalate, but the last 30 minutes shine above everything that came before.

Yorgos… you always manage to surprise me, you know that.

Human beings have no solution. We are our own colonizers — one of the few species capable of destroying itself.

Ps: Jesse Plemons has been taking on roles I could easily see Dafoe playing… I kept watching and thinking: “Dafoe, is that you?”

films.name: Kinds of Kindness
films.review:
Dou esta quantidade de estrelas por vários motivos… mas o principal? Não me vai sair da cabeça nos próximos tempos. As 3 histórias contadas de forma tirana, obscena, com tópicos incomodativos e pouco convencionais: tudo para me manter preso à cadeira.
Se é o melhor filme que vi este ano? Provavelmente não. Se compensa ver e ficar a pensar nele durante horas a fio? Compensa, a 100%.
Resta-me ir para casa e apreciar um belo de um hambúrguer: “D.C.C eat a Sandwich”

films.name: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
films.review:
This has many drama/thriller aspects but the psychological horror predominates...
The use of the camera is so well done, almost as if it were a being/person/something hovering over the family, simply incredible. Extremely disturbing sexual scenes (placed on purpose for less than humanistic purposes), chilling music and an uncomfortable story from beginning to end. A film a la Yorgos Lanthimos, very creepy to say the least.

Zack Snyder.name: Zack Snyder
Zack Snyder.bio:
Zachary Edward Snyder (born March 1, 1966) is an American filmmaker. He made his feature film debut in 2004 with Dawn of the Dead, a remake of the 1978 horror film of the same name. Since then, he has directed or produced a number of comic book and superhero films, including 300 (2006) and Watchmen (2009), as well as the Superman film that started the DC Extended Universe, Man of Steel (2013), and its follow-ups, Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016) and Justice League (2017), the latter of which had a director's cut released in 2021. He also directed the animated film Legend of the Guardians: The Owls of Ga'Hoole (2010), the psychological action film Sucker Punch (2011), the zombie heist film Army of the Dead (2021), and the two-part space opera films Rebel Moon (2023) and Rebel Moon—Part Two: The Scargiver (2024).

In 2004, he founded the production company The Stone Quarry (formerly known as Cruel and Unusual Films) alongside his wife Deborah Snyder and producing partner Wesley Coller.

Description above from the Wikipedia article Zack Snyder, licensed under CC-BY-SA, full list of contributors on Wikipedia.

films.name: Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
